name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads an aggregated overview of training and consulting offerings and routes users to key tasks such as browsing courses, workshops, consulting, pricing, booking, and insights.

core_features: Load aggregated home overview data using getHomeOverview, including hero configuration, summary sections, featured courses, featured workshops, featured case studies, and recent articles.
Provide a primary action area using hero data, exposing two main calls-to-action that route users into booking and course browsing flows.
Offer a training search form that forwards the user to the Online Courses listing view with the search term encoded in the URL as a query parameter.
Render a summary navigation grid from summarySections, with each item routing to the appropriate section (online courses, workshops, consulting, pricing, insights) via URL navigation.
Display featured courses and workshops using data from getHomeOverview, allowing users to navigate into the respective detail or listing views with URL parameters that identify the selected item.
Highlight featured case studies, including logic that routes users to a filtered case studies listing for specific industries when applicable (e.g., technology).
List recent articles (insights) and enable navigation to the insights/blog section, including a specific CTA for remote work content that passes tag and sort URL parameters.
Expose utility navigation from the home page to the training plan, saved items, consultant directory, pricing calculator, consulting overview, and shopping cart, using URL parameters where defined.
user_workflows: Homepage data load: 1) User lands on index.html without needing any URL parameters. 2) The page calls getHomeOverview. 3) When the response resolves, the hero area, summary grid, featured courses, featured workshops, featured case studies, and recent articles components bind to the returned data objects and render their content.

Search for a training topic: 1) User types a topic into the homepage search input (e.g., leadership fundamentals). 2) User submits the form. 3) The form submit handler reads the input value and constructs a navigation URL to courses.html with view=list and query set to the URL-encoded search term (courses.html?view=list&query={encodedTerm}). 4) Browser navigates to that URL, where the courses page handles the search results.

Browse online courses from primary navigation: 1) User clicks the secondary hero CTA button whose label comes from hero.secondaryCtaLabel. 2) The click handler navigates to courses.html with view=list (courses.html?view=list). 3) The courses page presents the full course listing view.

Enter booking flow from home: 1) User clicks the primary hero CTA button whose label comes from hero.primaryCtaLabel. 2) The click handler constructs a URL to booking.html with serviceId set from a configuration or preselected service context (booking.html?serviceId={preselectedCoachingServiceId}). 3) Browser navigates to booking.html where the booking page preselects the corresponding coaching service using the serviceId parameter.

Navigate via summary sections: 1) User scans the summarySections-based navigation tiles. 2) User clicks a tile with a known key (such as one mapped to training programs, consulting, pricing, or insights). 3) Based on the tile’s key, the click handler chooses the target URL, e.g., workshops.html?view=list for training programs, consulting.html?view=list for consulting, pricing.html for pricing. 4) Browser navigates to the chosen page using those URL parameters.

Explore featured courses: 1) User sees a featured courses strip built from featuredCourses. 2) User clicks a specific course card. 3) The click handler reads the selected Course.id and constructs a URL like courses.html?id={courseId}. 4) Browser navigates to courses.html, where the courses page handles displaying the selected course detail or focused view based on the id parameter.

Explore featured workshops: 1) User views a featured workshops strip built from featuredWorkshops. 2) User clicks a workshop card. 3) The click handler reads Workshop.id and navigates to workshops.html?id={workshopId}. 4) The workshops page uses the id parameter to show details or a contextual view of that workshop.

Explore featured case studies and industry-specific listings: 1) User views featured case studies bound from featuredCaseStudies. 2) Clicking on a case study card can either: (a) navigate to a case_studies.html view for that specific case study (e.g., case_studies.html?id={caseStudyId}), or (b) if the user chooses a 'view more in this industry' interaction on a case study whose industry equals 'technology', navigate to case_studies.html?industry=technology. 3) The case studies page uses the industry parameter to filter results.

Explore insights and remote work content: 1) User views recentArticles rendered from getHomeOverview. 2) Clicking an article title navigates to blog.html with an articleId parameter (blog.html?articleId={articleId}) for the insights page to handle. 3) If the user clicks the dedicated remote work insights CTA, the handler navigates to blog.html with tag=remote_work and sort=most_popular (blog.html?tag=remote_work&sort=most_popular).

Access consulting experts: 1) User clicks the 'Meet our leadership consultants' CTA available from a summary section or insights area. 2) The click handler navigates to consultants.html with expertise=leadership_development and sort=experience_desc (consultants.html?expertise=leadership_development&sort=experience_desc). 3) The consultants listing page uses these parameters to pre-filter the directory.

Use utility navigation: 1) From the header or a utility area on Home, the user can select 'My Training Plan', 'Saved Items', or the cart icon. 2) The respective click handlers navigate to training_plan.html, saved_items.html, or cart.html (without additional parameters) so those pages can load the user’s data. 3) The user can then return via browser back or site navigation.
interactions: On page load, trigger a single call to getHomeOverview and store the result in page-level state accessible to all child components.
Hero primary CTA click: read any preconfigured or contextual serviceId value intended for 1:1 coaching, construct booking.html?serviceId={serviceId}, and navigate there.
Hero secondary CTA click: navigate to courses.html?view=list.
Search form submit: prevent default form submission, URL-encode the search input value, and navigate to courses.html?view=list&query={encodedSearchTerm}.
Summary tile click (training programs/workshops key): navigate to workshops.html?view=list.
Summary tile click (consulting key): navigate to consulting.html?view=list.
Summary tile click (pricing key): navigate to pricing.html.
Summary tile click (insights/blog key): navigate to blog.html (optionally adding sort or tag parameters as defined by requirements, without hardcoding article content).
Featured course card click: navigate to courses.html?id={course.id}.
Featured workshop card click: navigate to workshops.html?id={workshop.id}.
Featured case study card click: navigate to case_studies.html?id={caseStudy.id}.
Featured case study industry CTA click (for case studies where industry == 'technology'): navigate to case_studies.html?industry=technology.
Recent article title click: navigate to blog.html?articleId={article.id}.
Remote work insights CTA click (determined by filtering recentArticles for articles whose tags array includes 'remote_work'): navigate to blog.html?tag=remote_work&sort=most_popular.
Consultants CTA click: navigate to consultants.html?expertise=leadership_development&sort=experience_desc.
My Training Plan utility link click: navigate to training_plan.html.
Saved Items utility link click: navigate to saved_items.html.
Cart icon click: navigate to cart.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page itself does not require incoming URL parameters and initializes its view solely from the getHomeOverview response. Page state consists of the loaded home overview data and transient UI state (such as search input value), but no persistent client-side storage is used. All routing to other pages relies on constructing URLs with query parameters, allowing downstream pages to derive their state from the URL. When the user submits the search form, the search term is encoded into the query parameter 'query' and the target 'view' parameter is set to 'list' for courses.html. When the user navigates to specific list or detail pages (courses.html, workshops.html, case_studies.html, blog.html, booking.html, consultants.html), the Home page appends parameters like view, query, id, articleId, industry, tag, sort, or serviceId as required by the defined navigation flows. The Home page does not read or modify any existing URL parameters; it only composes outbound URLs using known parameter names so that target pages can reconstruct context purely from those URLs.

components.functionality: Calls getHomeOverview on initial page load and exposes the returned hero, summarySections, featuredCourses, featuredWorkshops, featuredCaseStudies, and recentArticles to descendant components.

Displays the primary home entry actions using hero data, including heading, subheading, a primary CTA button for session booking, and a secondary CTA button for browsing online courses. It does not define any static labels; all text is taken from hero properties.

Provides a single text input for users to enter a training-related search term and, on submission, redirects the browser to the courses listing page with the search term encoded in the query parameter and the view parameter set to list.

Renders a set of navigation tiles from summarySections. On click, each tile routes to the corresponding section (courses, workshops, consulting, pricing, insights) by mapping the summarySections[i].key to the appropriate target URL and query parameters.

Displays a horizontal or grid-based list of featured courses using the featuredCourses collection. Each course card is clickable and sends the user to the courses page with the selected course id encoded as a URL parameter.
Shows featured workshops from featuredWorkshops. Clicking a workshop navigates to the workshops page with an id parameter indicating which workshop was selected.

Displays a list of featured case studies from featuredCaseStudies. Clicking a case study card navigates to a case studies view for that specific item. For items whose industry equals 'technology', a dedicated interaction (such as a 'more in this industry' link) navigates to the case studies listing filtered by industry using an industry URL parameter.

Lists recent articles from recentArticles. Clicking an article navigates to the blog/insights page with the selected article identified in the URL parameters. It also enables a remote work insights CTA, determined by filtering articles that have 'remote_work' in their tags array, which navigates to the blog page with tag and sort parameters set.

Provides quick-access links and icons from the home page to the training plan, saved items, consultants listing (pre-filtered for leadership development), pricing calculator, consulting overview, and shopping cart, constructing the appropriate URLs with defined parameters.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: All home components are stacked in a single, linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered max-width container (~1120px). Above them, the global cyan utility bar and white navigation bar remain unchanged from the mockup. Within the main content area, each section (hero, search, navigation grid, feature strips, articles, utility nav) appears as a full-width band of content aligned to a central column, with only light intra-section horizontal structuring (grids/rows) where needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All home components are stacked in a single, linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered max-width container (~1120px). Above them, the global cyan utility bar and white navigation bar remain unchanged from the mockup. Within the main content area, each section (hero, search, navigation grid, feature strips, articles, utility nav) appears as a full-width band of content aligned to a central column, with only light intra-section horizontal structuring (grids/rows) where needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and cyan utility bar together still occupy roughly the top 15–20% of the viewport. Below, each home section has generous vertical padding (40–64px top and bottom). Text-centric areas (hero messaging, articles) use a 50–60% width central column; visual/interactive areas (navigation grid, featured strips) extend slightly wider (70–80% of container) while retaining large left/right margins. No section occupies the full edge-to-edge width of the viewport for content; full-width is used only for subtle background bands.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains low content density: few items per row (2–3 cards max), large breathing room between sections, and comfortable spacing within components (16–24px padding). Forms and navigation elements are simplified into one or two clear rows. Lists are short and vertically spaced. This mirrors the Privacy Policy page’s airy editorial feel, even though there are more interactive elements.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the cyan utility bar to the logo and main navigation, then drops into the hero area as the primary focal point. From there, the flow is straight downward: hero actions → search form → high-level navigation grid → featured learning strips (courses, workshops, case studies) → recent articles → bottom utility navigation. Typography hierarchy and section titles guide scanning, with each band visually separated by whitespace rather than heavy separators, supporting a simple scroll-down reading pattern.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing cyan utility bar and white navigation/header, the home page opens into a bright, uncluttered hero section centered in the viewport, with a bold headline and primary actions floating in a wide column that covers roughly the middle 60% of the screen. Underneath, a clean, centered search bar spans about two-thirds of the content width, framed by generous white space above and below so it feels approachable, not busy. Scrolling down, a compact grid of large, tappable navigation tiles sits in the center of the page, each card surrounded by comfortable margins and aligned in a simple 2–3 column layout that never touches the edges of the container. Further down, three separate horizontal feature strips—courses, workshops, and case studies—stack one after another, each with a left-aligned title and a modest row of cards that sit comfortably within the main width, echoing the calm, corporate tone. Near the bottom, a short list of recent articles appears in a narrower text column to emphasize readability, and finally a slim, understated utility navigation band echoes the cyan accent color and gently closes the page just above the global footer. Throughout, everything stays aligned to a central grid with ample whitespace, reinforcing a modern, minimal, professional aesthetic where users scroll naturally downward, discovering more detail section by section without ever feeling overwhelmed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is a non-visual layer that logically wraps the entire main content area beneath the global header. It conceptually spans the full viewport width and height, but by default is invisible. When active (e.g., initial page load or refresh), it appears as a subtle, semi-transparent white overlay centered over the main content column, with any loading indicator (spinner or message) centered horizontally and vertically within the viewport. The overlay respects the existing cyan utility bar and white nav bar, starting directly below them so the global header remains visible while content is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed or absolute overlay anchored to the viewport, covering the main content area while active and removed from the visual flow when inactive so it does not affect spacing of surrounding components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly below the global navigation/header, the hero actions panel forms the first visible section of the home content. It occupies a full-width band of white background, with top padding of about 56–64px from the bottom of the header and bottom padding of around 48–56px. Within this band, a central content column spanning roughly 60% of the page width is horizontally centered. The main hero headline sits at the top of this column, visually prominent and either centered or slightly left-aligned but still anchored within the central column. Below the headline, a short supportive subheading or tagline (if present) uses a slightly narrower line length but stays within the same column. The primary actions (e.g., two main call-to-action buttons and possibly one secondary text link) are placed immediately beneath the text, centered on the hero column, arranged in a single horizontal row on desktop with 16–24px spacing between them, and stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing on smaller screens. Ample whitespace surrounds the content so that the hero appears bright and uncluttered, echoing the spacious, single-column feel seen on the Privacy Policy page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main scrolling flow; it scrolls normally with the page and does not overlap or stick to the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training search form sits directly below the hero actions panel, separated by approximately 40–48px of vertical whitespace to maintain the airy flow. It occupies a distinct horizontal band on a white background that visually reads as part of the same page canvas. Inside the main container, the search form is centered and spans about 65–70% of the page width, matching or slightly exceeding the hero text column, while still leaving substantial left and right margins. The form layout is simple and linear: on desktop, the primary search input (e.g., text field for keywords) stretches across most of this width, with any secondary controls (filters or dropdowns) aligned in the same row but spaced with clear gaps so elements never feel cramped. A prominent search button sits at the right end of the row or just below the main input, depending on available width. Vertical padding inside the form (16–24px) ensures that fields are comfortably sized and easy to scan. On smaller screens, the form fields stack vertically, retaining generous vertical spacing between each input to preserve the low-density aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section within the content container, flowing directly after the hero. No fixed or sticky behavior; it scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary navigation grid appears below the training search form, with roughly 48–56px of vertical separation. It occupies a full-width band of content on a white or very subtle tinted background to differentiate it gently from neighboring sections without strong borders. The section heading (e.g., 'Explore Our Services' or similar) is placed at the top of this band, aligned to the left within the central container but still constrained to the main text column width (~60% of page), with about 8–16px of space below it. Underneath, the navigation grid itself spans about 70–80% of the container width and is centered horizontally. On desktop, key navigation tiles (e.g., Browse Courses, Workshops, Consulting, Pricing, Booking, Insights) are laid out in a simple grid of 2–3 columns with 16–24px gaps between tiles and 24–32px vertical spacing between rows. Each tile has internal padding (16–24px) and a flat card appearance, but with plenty of whitespace around them so the grid feels light, not like a dense dashboard. The outer margin above and below the grid maintains at least 32–40px of whitespace, keeping it visually distinct as a wayfinding hub.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static grid section within the vertical flow. It does not resize beyond the central container; it remains centered and simply reflows to fewer columns on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured courses strip sits below the summary navigation grid, separated by approximately 48px of vertical whitespace. It reads as a horizontal content band spanning the full container width. At the top of the strip, a section title such as 'Featured Courses' is aligned left within the main content container, starting at the same left edge as the navigation grid heading for consistency. Opposite the title on the right, aligned on the same horizontal line, a small, subtle 'View all courses' text link or button may appear, maintaining the clean corporate look. Below the title row, a single row of course cards spans about 80% of the container width and is centered within it, with cards arranged in 2–3 columns depending on screen width. Each card occupies roughly 30–45% of the row width, leaving consistent gaps of 16–24px between cards. Card content (title, short description, CTA) is vertically spaced inside each card with generous padding (16–24px) to maintain low density. The strip has ample bottom padding (~40px) before the next section, so the row of cards feels like a focused, standalone feature band rather than crowded against following content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal strip, aligned to the central grid, with cards reflowing (fewer per row) on smaller viewports while maintaining consistent margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured workshops strip directly follows the featured courses strip, with similar vertical spacing (40–48px) to create a clear yet coherent transition. Its structure mirrors the courses strip for visual consistency: a left-aligned heading ('Featured Workshops' or similar) at the top of the section, with an optional right-aligned 'View all workshops' link on the same baseline. Below this heading row, a centered row of workshop cards occupies about 80% of the container width. The cards align vertically with those in the courses strip above, forming a rhythm of equal-width bands as the user scrolls. Again, only 2–3 cards appear per row with 16–24px horizontal gaps and generous vertical padding inside each card. The white or subtly tinted background continues the calm, corporate tone, and significant whitespace remains above and below the card row to preserve the low-density, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content strip that follows the same responsive behavior as the featured courses strip, reflowing cards within the central container and scrolling normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured case studies strip appears beneath the workshops strip, again separated by roughly 40–48px of whitespace so each section feels distinct but part of a cohesive vertical narrative. As with the previous strips, a left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Client Success Stories') sits at the top of the band within the main container, with an optional right-aligned 'View all case studies' link. Underneath, case study cards are arranged in a single row that spans around 80% of the container, centered and aligned with the card grid above. Because case studies are often more narrative, each card may be slightly taller or include a short summary, but internal spacing remains generous (16–24px padding, 8–16px between text elements) so they are easy to scan. Only a small number of cards (2–3) are shown to avoid visual overload, and the bottom of the strip includes 48px of padding before the next section, preserving the spacious vertical flow inherited from the Privacy Policy layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block section with the same grid and responsive behavior as the other featured strips, simply reflowing cards and maintaining central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent articles list sits below the featured case studies strip, separated by about 56–64px of vertical whitespace to signal a shift from offerings to thought leadership. This section returns to a more editorial pattern resembling the Privacy Policy page. The section heading (e.g., 'Latest Insights') is left-aligned within the main content container and constrained to a 50–60% width central column. Below the heading, the list of articles is presented in a single vertical column that also spans roughly 50–60% of the page width and is centered horizontally, emphasizing readability. Each article entry consists of a title and brief meta information (date, category) and possibly a one-line summary, with 16–24px of vertical spacing between entries. Typically, only 3–5 articles are shown to keep the section light. There are no heavy borders; instead, whitespace and typographic hierarchy separate items. The bottom margin of this section is generous (48–64px), creating a soft visual transition into the utility navigation area near the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, single-column list section that scrolls with the page, mirroring the central, narrow text layout used on the Privacy Policy page for continuity.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home utility navigation is positioned near the very bottom of the page, just above the global footer, following the recent articles list with approximately 40–48px of whitespace. It takes the form of a slim, full-width horizontal band that echoes the cyan accent of the top utility bar, though possibly with slightly lighter intensity or a reduced height to keep it understated. Within this band, content is centered inside the main container. Utility links (e.g., secondary navigation items like FAQs, Support, Legal, or quick anchor links back to key sections) are arranged in a single horizontal row on desktop, centered within the band, with 16–24px spacing between each text link. The text is small to medium size, with sufficient padding (12–16px top and bottom) to maintain tap targets and preserve the minimal, modern aesthetic. On smaller screens, these links stack into 2–3 short rows but remain centrally aligned. This band visually closes the content area and ties the page back to the cyan header, framing the main content column vertically.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-adjacent band that spans the full width of the viewport but keeps its content centered in the main container; it scrolls into view as the user reaches the bottom of the page.

name: Online Courses
title: Online Courses
description: Page for searching, filtering, sorting, and browsing online courses, with an integrated course detail view and cart actions controlled by URL parameters.

core_features: Display a list of online courses based on search, filters, sort, and pagination using the searchCourses interface.
Load and display available filter and sort options (levels, topics, delivery formats, price ranges, rating thresholds, sort options) using the getCourseFilterOptions interface.
Apply search query, level, maxPrice, minRating, and sort parameters from the URL to construct the filters object passed to searchCourses.
Support pagination of course results via the page parameter and the searchCourses page/pageSize response.
Render a course detail view when view=detail and courseId are present in the URL, using getCourseDetails.
Within the detail view, display course information (including level, price, delivery format, rating) and related courses from getCourseDetails.
Allow adding the currently viewed course to the cart via addCourseToCart, handling success and error messages and showing an option to navigate to cart.html.
Allow navigation from the course list to the detail view and back again while preserving search query, filters (level, maxPrice, minRating), sort, and page via URL parameters.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Browse and filter courses: 1) User navigates to courses.html without parameters. 2) Page controller reads URL (no view specified) and defaults to list mode. 3) Page calls getCourseFilterOptions to load available filter and sort options. 4) Page constructs an initial filters object from the URL parameters (query, level, maxPrice, minRating) if present, and any in-page filter defaults for topics and delivery formats. 5) Page calls searchCourses with the current query, filters, sort, page, and a configured pageSize. 6) User adjusts filters (e.g., selects a level, sets a max price, chooses a minimum rating) and submits or applies them. 7) Page updates the corresponding URL parameters (level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, page reset to 1) and re-calls searchCourses. 8) User navigates between result pages via pagination controls; each page change updates the page URL parameter and re-calls searchCourses.

Workflow 2 – Search by keyword then open course detail: 1) User types a keyword into the search input and submits. 2) Page updates the query parameter in the URL and resets page to 1. 3) Page calls searchCourses with the new query and current filters/sort. 4) User reviews the result list and clicks a course card. 5) Page navigates to courses.html with view=detail and courseId set to the selected course, preserving query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page from the current URL. 6) Detail view controller reads the URL, calls getCourseDetails with courseId, and displays the course and relatedCourses.

Workflow 3 – Add a course to cart from detail and view cart: 1) In the course detail view, user selects a quantity if applicable and triggers the add-to-cart action. 2) Detail view calls addCourseToCart with courseId and quantity. 3) On success, page displays the updated cart summary info from the addCourseToCart response (e.g., subtotal, total, currency) in a notification or cart summary component. 4) User optionally clicks a "View Cart"-type action that navigates to cart.html (no additional parameters required). 5) On error (e.g., addCourseToCart returns success=false), page displays the message from the response and does not navigate away.

Workflow 4 – Return from detail view to the previous results: 1) User is viewing a course detail (view=detail, courseId set, plus query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, page preserved in the URL). 2) User clicks a back-to-results UI element. 3) Page navigates to courses.html with view=list and the same query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page values from the current URL. 4) List controller reads the URL, rebuilds the filters object, calls searchCourses, and displays the result list in the same state as before opening the detail view.

Workflow 5 – Open course detail directly from another page: 1) User clicks a link to a specific course from index.html or workshops.html. 2) Link targets courses.html with view=detail and courseId populated, and optionally includes a query parameter. 3) Detail view controller reads view=detail and courseId, calls getCourseDetails, and renders the course. 4) If query and other filter parameters are not present, the back-to-results link either returns to courses.html with view=list and any available parameters or can be hidden/disabled according to product requirements.
interactions: Typing into the search input and submitting triggers updating the query URL parameter, resetting page to 1, and calling searchCourses with the new query and existing filters and sort.
Changing a level filter in the filter panel updates the level URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchCourses call with updated filters.
Changing a maximum price filter updates the maxPrice URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers searchCourses with updated filters.
Changing a minimum rating filter updates the minRating URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers searchCourses with updated filters.

Changing topic or delivery-format filters (if present) updates in-memory filter state (and the filters object passed to searchCourses) and triggers searchCourses; these selections are not encoded in URL parameters unless explicitly mapped to the existing parameter set.
Changing a sort option updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to 1, and calls searchCourses using the current query and filters and new sort.
Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter and re-calls searchCourses with the same query, filters, and sort but the new page.

Clicking on a course card in the result list navigates the browser to courses.html with view=detail and courseId set to the clicked course’s id, including the current query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page values in the URL.

On initial load of a detail view (view=detail and courseId present), the page calls getCourseDetails with courseId and binds the returned course and relatedCourses to the detail and related-courses components.
Clicking a related course in the detail view replaces the courseId in the URL (keeping view=detail and any other parameters) and triggers a new getCourseDetails call for the newly selected course.
Submitting the add-to-cart action in the detail view calls addCourseToCart with the current courseId and user-selected quantity, and then displays success or error feedback based on the response.
Clicking the "View Cart"-type navigation from the cart feedback area navigates to cart.html.

Clicking the back-to-results link or breadcrumb in the detail view navigates to courses.html with view=list and the query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page parameters preserved from the current URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and API responses. On every load or URL change, a central controller reads the parameters: 1) view: if view=detail and a valid courseId is present, the page renders the detail state; otherwise it renders the list state. 2) courseId: used only in detail state; when present with view=detail, the page calls getCourseDetails(courseId). 3) query: optional search string; when present in list state it is passed to searchCourses; when navigating from list to detail and back, the current query value is preserved in the URL. 4) level, maxPrice, minRating: when present, these parameters are parsed into appropriate types and used to build the filters object passed to searchCourses. On filter changes in the UI, these parameters are updated in the URL, and page is reset to 1 to avoid invalid pagination. 5) sort: when present, it is passed directly to searchCourses; if not present, the page may choose a default sort value, for example using the first option returned by getCourseFilterOptions; this default is not hardcoded but inferred from options. 6) page: when present, it is passed to searchCourses; if missing or invalid, the page defaults internally to 1 without storing this default in localStorage. The list controller listens to URL changes: any parameter update (query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, page) leads to constructing a new filters object and calling searchCourses(query, filters, sort, page, pageSize), where pageSize is an application-level configuration. The detail controller also listens to URL changes: if courseId changes while view=detail, it calls getCourseDetails for the new courseId. Navigation back to results simply switches view=list in the URL while preserving query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page, so the list is reconstructed by calling searchCourses with these persisted values. No state is stored in localStorage; all cross-view state is encoded in URL parameters and derived from the latest API responses.

components.functionality: Determines whether to render the list or detail view based on the view and courseId URL parameters; orchestrates calls to getCourseFilterOptions, searchCourses, and getCourseDetails; passes resulting data and current state (query, filters, sort, page) to child components.

On initialization of the list view, calls getCourseFilterOptions and exposes levels, topics, deliveryFormats, priceRanges, ratingOptions, and sortOptions to other components such as the filter panel and sort control.

Provides a text input for the course search query and an optional submit control. On submit, updates the query URL parameter, resets the page parameter to 1, and notifies the controller to re-run searchCourses with the new query and current filters and sort.

Displays filter controls for level, price range (including maxPrice selection), rating (minRating), and optionally topics and delivery formats based on getCourseFilterOptions data. When filters change: level, maxPrice, and minRating are written to the URL parameters; internal filter state (including topics and delivery formats if used) is updated; page is reset to 1; the controller is instructed to call searchCourses with an updated filters object derived from URL and in-memory selections.

Renders available sort options using sortOptions from getCourseFilterOptions. When the user selects a sort option, it updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchCourses call via the controller.

Displays meta-information about the current search results, such as totalCount and the current page range derived from searchCourses response (page and pageSize). Also shows an overview of active filters derived from the URL parameters (query, level, maxPrice, minRating).

Renders the current page of courses from searchCourses.items as a list of selectable cards. Each card exposes key course properties such as title, level, delivery_format, price, currency, rating, rating_count, and thumbnail_url. Clicking a card navigates to courses.html with view=detail and courseId set to that course, preserving query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page URL parameters.

Uses searchCourses.totalCount, page, and pageSize to compute the number of pages and render pagination controls. When the user selects a different page, it updates the page URL parameter and triggers searchCourses with the existing query, filters, and sort but the new page value.

Activated when view=detail and courseId are present in the URL. Calls getCourseDetails with courseId, then displays the course’s detailed information (title, full_description, level, topics, delivery_format, price, currency, rating, rating_count, duration_hours, instructor_name, thumbnail_url) and passes relatedCourses to the related-courses list component. Also contains the add-to-cart button and a back-to-results link that navigates back to view=list with preserved query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page parameters.

Displays related courses from getCourseDetails.relatedCourses, using the same card representation as the main course list. Clicking a related course updates courseId in the URL, keeps view=detail and any other parameters, and triggers getCourseDetails for the selected course.

Provides a control in the course detail view to add the current course to the cart. Optionally allows selecting a quantity. On action, calls addCourseToCart with the current courseId and selected quantity. It then relays the result (success flag, message, cart, items, subtotal, discounts, tax, total, currency) to the cart-notification component.

Listens for responses from addCourseToCart. When a response is received, it displays success or error information using the message field and may show a brief summary of the updated cart totals from the response. It includes a control that, when triggered, navigates the user to cart.html.

In detail view, displays navigational context (e.g., a link representing the Online Courses list and the current course). The list link navigates to courses.html with view=list and preserves query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, and page URL parameters. It relies on the current Course data from getCourseDetails and the URL parameters.

Central utility that reads URL parameters on load and update, and provides functions to update the URL (e.g., when search, filters, sort, page, or view change) without reloading the entire application context. It ensures that all user interactions that affect query, level, maxPrice, minRating, sort, page, view, and courseId are reflected in the URL and that the appropriate APIs (searchCourses or getCourseDetails) are called after updates.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the Online Courses page uses a primarily vertical, single-column flow inside a centered max-width container (~1140px). The top of the content column holds breadcrumb and search, followed by a full-width filter panel, then a compact row with results summary on the left and sort control on the right. The course list flows vertically beneath, followed by pagination. When a course is selected, a full-width but visually centered detail view appears below the listing area, followed by related courses. Non-visual controller/state components are conceptually aligned with the top of the content area but do not consume visible space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the Online Courses page uses a primarily vertical, single-column flow inside a centered max-width container (~1140px). The top of the content column holds breadcrumb and search, followed by a full-width filter panel, then a compact row with results summary on the left and sort control on the right. The course list flows vertically beneath, followed by pagination. When a course is selected, a full-width but visually centered detail view appears below the listing area, followed by related courses. Non-visual controller/state components are conceptually aligned with the top of the content area but do not consume visible space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: About 15–20% of the viewport height is occupied by the existing header and cyan bar. The Online Courses content occupies the remaining vertical space with generous vertical gaps (32–48px) between major sections (breadcrumb/search, filters, results header row, course grid, detail view). Horizontally, primary reading/text blocks (search input, detail content, descriptions) are kept at ~60% of the page width and centered within the main container, while functional UI elements like filters and course cards use up to 80–90% of the container width but maintain ample side margins.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally low-density and breathable: few elements per row, lots of whitespace, and no tightly packed multi-column tables. Course cards are arranged in a light two-column grid on larger screens with generous padding and gaps; text blocks within them are short and well spaced. Filters are grouped into clear vertical stacks with ample padding. The course detail view mirrors an editorial layout with wide margins and generous line spacing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves vertically: from the existing bright header down to the breadcrumb, then to the prominent search form, then into the filter panel, and from there to the results summary/sort row. The eye then scans downward through the course list, briefly pauses on pagination, and continues to the detail view and related courses when present. Horizontal scanning is limited to small, expected areas (sort control on the right, social proof and metadata within cards), avoiding any complex Z-pattern. When the cart banner appears, it briefly interrupts at the top of the content area but remains visually light.

layout.overall_description: Under the full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the Online Courses page begins with a centered content column inside a max-width container, sitting in a field of white space. A subtle breadcrumb anchors the top-left of this column, followed by a large, centered search bar that visually dominates the upper portion of the page while still leaving plenty of breathing room above and below. Directly underneath, a light, full-width filter panel stretches across most of the container, framed by moderate padding and thin dividing lines so it feels structured but not heavy. Below this, a slim horizontal row displays the number of matching courses on the left and sorting controls on the right, acting as a transition into the course list. The course grid itself consists of two wide, minimally styled cards per row on larger screens, each surrounded by generous margins so the layout remains calm and uncluttered. Pagination sits centered beneath, with comfortable spacing separating it from both the grid above and any detail view below. When a specific course is selected, a softly framed detail view opens in the same central column, spanning roughly two-thirds of the page width and echoing the simple, editorial feeling of the site’s Privacy Policy page—large title, readable text width, and plenty of white space. The primary ‘Add to Cart’ button is placed prominently within this detail block, with any cart notification appearing as a slim, full-width strip just beneath the global header. Finally, a related courses strip appears below the detail view, using a small number of spacious cards to close the page while maintaining the overall airy, straightforward vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and conceptually sits at the very top of the Online Courses content area, immediately below the main site header and above all other page-specific components. It does not occupy visible space or alter the spacing between elements, but it governs which visible sections (course list vs. detail view vs. combined view) are rendered in the central column. Its presence is effectively aligned with the left edge of the main content container and spans the full internal width logically, without any rendered background or borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; participates only in logical layout. No impact on document flow or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader component is invisible in the UI. It is logically positioned immediately before the filter panel within the code structure so that the filter options are ready when the filter panel is rendered. It spans the width of the main content container conceptually but does not introduce any visible element, padding, or gap between components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered; operates behind the scenes without affecting layout or scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course search form is the first clearly visible functional element of the Online Courses page content, positioned below the breadcrumb and comfortably separated from it by approximately 32–40px of vertical whitespace. It is centered within the main content container and consists primarily of a single, horizontally oriented search input with an optional search icon/button attached on the right. On larger screens, the search input spans about 60% of the page width, leaving wide, symmetric white margins on both sides so it feels focused and editorial rather than app-like. Vertically, there is 24–32px of padding inside the search control and another 32–48px of whitespace before the filter panel below, reinforcing the spacious, low-density feel of the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal document flow; scrolls with the page. On smaller screens it may expand to ~90–100% of the content width while remaining centered with consistent side padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel sits directly beneath the search form, separated by a vertical gap of about 32px. It is rendered as a light, full-width block within the main content container, with a subtle border or very light background tint to distinguish it from the white page background while staying visually minimal. The panel horizontally spans about 90% of the container width, leaving a small margin on either side so it still appears as part of the central column rather than edge-to-edge. Inside, filters are arranged in a vertical stack of groups: each group has a label at the top left with 8–12px of space above and a set of checkboxes, toggles, or pill-shaped options below, aligned left. On larger screens, groups can form two loose columns inside the panel (each column roughly 45% of the panel width) but with enough horizontal spacing so content never appears crowded. The panel’s total height is content-driven; generous internal padding (24px top/bottom, 16–24px left/right) ensures the overall block feels airy. A gap of 32px separates the bottom of the panel from the results summary/sort row below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow. Optionally can become collapsible on smaller screens (expanding as a full-width block below the search form), but remains non-sticky and scrolls away as the user scans results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned in a slim horizontal row immediately below the filter panel. This row spans the full width of the main content container but is visually light, acting as a header for the results grid. The sort control itself is right-aligned within this row, sitting flush with the right edge of the central column. It typically appears as a dropdown or segmented control with a concise label (e.g., 'Sort by'), occupying roughly 15–20% of the container width on desktop. On the left of this same row sits the course results summary, ensuring the user’s eye moves left to right across the line before continuing downward. There is about 16–24px of vertical padding above and below this row, and a 24–32px gap separates it from the top of the course list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content flow, aligned baseline with the results summary on larger screens. On smaller screens, it can drop below the summary and center-align, maintaining vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary ('X courses found', plus optional active filter count) sits in the same horizontal row as the sort control, aligned to the left edge of the main content container. It occupies about 40–50% of the row’s width, presented in a small to medium weight type that is clear but not visually dominant. The summary text anchors the top of the results section, giving users immediate feedback on their filter/search actions. Slight top padding (8–12px) ensures the text is vertically centered relative to the sort control on the right. The row maintains about 16–24px vertical padding overall and transitions into the course list below with 24–32px of whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in normal flow, always appearing immediately above the course list. On narrow viewports it remains above the sort control when stacked.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course list occupies the central portion of the page below the results summary/sort row. It is laid out as a spacious, low-density grid within the main content container. On desktop, it typically shows two course cards per row: each card is about 45% of the container width, with a horizontal gap of around 32px between columns and substantial vertical spacing (~32–40px) between rows. The grid is centered so that both columns sit symmetrically within the container, retaining generous outer margins. Each course card has internal padding of 16–24px, a light border or subtle outline, and a predominantly white background to reinforce the clean, corporate aesthetic. Card height is content-driven but consistent enough to keep the grid visually tidy. On smaller screens, the layout collapses to a single column, with each card spanning ~90–100% of the container width and maintaining vertical gaps. A vertical margin of 40–48px separates the bottom of the course grid from the pagination controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable. The grid reflows responsively (two columns on larger screens, one column on smaller) but always remains centered within the max-width content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination component is centered horizontally beneath the course list, separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical whitespace. It occupies about 40–60% of the container width, with individual page numbers, previous/next arrows, or 'Load more' control spaced out so they feel uncluttered. The pagination row itself is visually light: minimal borders and a lot of background breathing room, matching the overall minimal aesthetic. There is another 40–48px of whitespace below the pagination before any course detail view begins, ensuring clear separation between browsing and detailed reading contexts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; sits at the end of the course grid in document flow. As more courses are loaded or pages change, it maintains its position relative to the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The course detail view appears below the course list and pagination, occupying the same central column to maintain the strong vertical flow. When active, it is separated from the pagination by about 48px of whitespace, clearly indicating a transition from overview to in-depth content. The detail block spans roughly 70% of the page width on desktop and is centered within the max-width container, leaving wide, even margins on both sides similar to the layout of the 'Privacy Policy' page. The course title is placed at the top left of this block in a large, bold style, with ample margin (24–32px) below before any meta information or description. Content inside (description text, curriculum, instructor info) is arranged in a single-column stack with line lengths tuned to readability (roughly 60–70 characters per line). Key sections are separated vertically by 24–40px, preserving a calm, editorial feel. The bottom of this block transitions into the related courses section with a vertical gap of 40–48px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in document flow. When the URL state specifies a selected course, this block becomes visible without overlaying the list; instead, it extends the page downward. On very large screens, the block remains centered and does not expand to the full width, preserving generous side margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related courses list sits directly beneath the course detail view, with a vertical gap of approximately 40–48px providing clear separation. It begins with a modest section heading aligned to the left edge of the detail content, followed by a short row or grid of related course cards. On desktop, this section shows 3 related courses in a single row, each card occupying roughly 28–30% of the container width, with equal horizontal spacing between them. The entire related courses block is still contained within the main content width and aligned with the detail view, preserving consistent margins. Cards mirror the styling of the main course list but are slightly smaller and visually lighter to signal secondary importance. The section is bounded below by at least 48px of whitespace before the page footer or any subsequent content, maintaining the airy, minimal feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the vertical flow, appearing only when a course detail is visible. On smaller screens, cards stack to a single column with generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'Add to Cart' button is placed prominently within the course detail view block, typically below the course title and key meta information but above the long-form description. It is left-aligned with the main text column inside the detail view and does not span the full width—occupying roughly 200–260px—so it feels like a clear, focused call to action rather than a heavy banner. There is at least 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below the button to keep it visually distinct. In the overall composition, this button sits around the upper-middle of the detail view, ensuring it is visible early without requiring excessive scrolling, while still respecting the page’s minimal, non-aggressive aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the detail view’s content stack. On smaller screens it remains full-width or nearly full-width within the text column but keeps consistent side padding and margin.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart notification banner appears as a slim, full-width bar directly beneath the main site navigation (and cyan utility bar if present), spanning the entire viewport width rather than just the centered content container. Its background is a light, positive color (e.g., pale green) with simple, left-aligned text indicating that a course has been added to the cart. Content inside the banner is horizontally centered within the same max-width container as the rest of the page, with 8–12px of vertical padding. Visually, it echoes the utility bar’s thin strip appearance but is lighter and less prominent, ensuring it catches the user’s attention momentarily without overpowering the page. A comfortable gap (24–32px) separates the bottom of the banner from the top of the Online Courses content (breadcrumb/search area).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed or sticky at the top of the content area when first shown, so it appears immediately after adding to cart. It can dismiss automatically or via a close icon, returning the page to its normal scroll position without affecting the underlying content layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb trail ('Home / Online Courses', etc.) is placed at the top of the Online Courses content region, directly below the global header (and any active cart notification banner), and above the search form. It is aligned to the left edge of the main content container and spans no more than 60–70% of the width, with the remainder of the row left empty. The breadcrumb text is small and understated, maintaining a low visual weight while still providing orientation. There is about 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the header/banners and the breadcrumb, and another 16–24px of space between the breadcrumb and the search form below, keeping all elements clearly separated while preserving the linear reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; scrolls away as the user moves down the page. On smaller screens it may wrap to multiple lines but remains left-aligned with consistent side padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This state management component is invisible and functions behind the scenes to synchronize filters, search parameters, selected course, and pagination with the URL. It is conceptually anchored at the page level around the courses-view-controller, but it does not render any visible UI or occupy any space. No additional padding, margins, or visual elements are introduced by this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic only; does not affect document flow, size, or scroll behavior.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Displays the user's current cart contents, allows updating or removing items, and provides navigation to continue browsing or proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load the current cart state using getCartDetails, including cart metadata, all cart items, associated course data, and aggregated pricing (subtotal, discounts, tax, total, currency).
Display a structured list of cart items with course information (title and other details from the Course entity) and pricing details from CartItem and getCartDetails.
Allow users to change the quantity of each cart item, triggering updateCartItemQuantity and then refreshing the cart view via getCartDetails.
Allow users to remove items from the cart using removeCartItem and then refreshing the cart view via getCartDetails.
Display a cart summary area showing subtotal, discounts, tax, total, and currency based on the latest response from getCartDetails.
Provide a "Continue browsing" action that uses the URL parameter `from` (if present) to determine where to send the user, defaulting to the courses listing view when appropriate.
Provide a "Proceed to checkout" action hook that can be connected to a future checkout flow, enabled only when the cart contains at least one item.
Handle loading, empty-cart, and error states for all cart operations using only the data returned from the assigned interfaces.
user_workflows:
1. View cart from header:
   1.1. User clicks the persistent "Cart" navigation link in the header.
   1.2. Browser navigates to cart.html, optionally including a `from` URL parameter if the prior page includes it (e.g., cart.html?from=courses).
   1.3. On page initialization, the cart-page-controller reads `from` from the URL (if present).
   1.4. cart-page-controller calls getCartDetails.
   1.5. While waiting, a loading-state component displays a loading indicator.
   1.6. On success, the controller passes the returned cart, items, and pricing information to cart-items-list and cart-summary-panel.
   1.7. If no items are returned, cart-items-list shows an empty-cart state and cart-actions-bar adjusts actions (e.g., disables checkout).

2. Adjust quantity of a cart item:
   2.1. User locates a specific course in cart-items-list.
   2.2. User increases or decreases the quantity using the quantity-control within that cart-item-row.
   2.3. quantity-control validates that the new quantity is a positive integer; if the value is less than 1, it can either reset to the prior valid value or offer a confirmation to remove the item (implementation choice).
   2.4. When the user confirms the new quantity, cart-item-row emits an onQuantityChange event to cart-page-controller with { cartItemId, newQuantity }.
   2.5. cart-page-controller calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, newQuantity).
   2.6. While the request is in progress, the affected row may indicate a pending update via local UI state; cart-actions-bar may temporarily disable actions to prevent conflicting updates.
   2.7. On success, to regain access to related Course data, cart-page-controller calls getCartDetails.
   2.8. The updated cart, items (with Course data), and pricing are passed down again to cart-items-list and cart-summary-panel.
   2.9. If newQuantity results in the item being removed (e.g., newQuantity is 0, if that behavior is enabled), cart-items-list renders the updated list with that item absent.
   2.10. On error from updateCartItemQuantity, error-notification displays an error with an option to retry the operation; cart-page-controller does not update the local view until a successful retry.

3. Remove an item from the cart:
   3.1. User clicks a remove action (e.g., a "Remove" button) within the cart-item-row for a specific item.
   3.2. cart-item-row emits an onRemoveItem event with the cartItemId to cart-page-controller.
   3.3. cart-page-controller may optionally prompt for confirmation via UI (implementation detail) before proceeding.
   3.4. On confirmation, cart-page-controller calls removeCartItem(cartItemId).
   3.5. While waiting for the response, the corresponding row may show a pending-removal state; cart-actions-bar may temporarily disable other operations if needed.
   3.6. On success, cart-page-controller calls getCartDetails to refresh the combined CartItem + Course data and pricing totals.
   3.7. cart-items-list re-renders without the removed item; if no items remain, the list shows an empty-cart state and cart-actions-bar disables checkout.
   3.8. On error, error-notification shows a message with an option to retry the removal; the item remains visible until a successful call.

4. Continue browsing from the cart:
   4.1. User clicks the "Continue browsing" action in cart-actions-bar.
   4.2. cart-page-controller reads the current page URL for the `from` parameter.
   4.3. If `from` equals "courses", the controller navigates to courses.html with the URL parameter view=list, as defined by the outgoing navigation (courses.html?view=list).
   4.4. If `from` equals "index", the controller navigates to index.html (home page).
   4.5. If `from` is absent or any other value, the controller defaults to navigating to courses.html?view=list to encourage course browsing.
   4.6. No cart data is modified by this action; the cart state persists via the underlying storage used by the interfaces.

5. Proceed to a future checkout flow:
   5.1. User reviews items and totals in the cart-summary-panel.
   5.2. If at least one item exists in the cart (based on items length from getCartDetails), cart-actions-bar enables the "Proceed to checkout" button; otherwise, it remains disabled.
   5.3. User clicks the enabled "Proceed to checkout" button.
   5.4. cart-page-controller emits an onProceedToCheckout event that the broader application can handle by navigating to the appropriate checkout page or flow (not defined within this page specification).
   5.5. No additional calls to the cart interfaces are made on this click; this page assumes the cart state is up to date since the last successful getCartDetails call.
interactions:
Page initialization:
      - Trigger: Page load of cart.html.
      - Behavior: cart-page-controller reads `from` from the URL query string, then calls getCartDetails.
      - Outcome: cart-items-list and cart-summary-panel receive and render the cart items and totals; cart-actions-bar configures button states based on items and `from`.

Change quantity (increment/decrement or direct edit):
      - Trigger: User edits quantity in quantity-control within a cart-item-row and confirms the change (e.g., by blur, Enter, or clicking a confirm control).
      - Behavior: cart-item-row emits onQuantityChange(cartItemId, newQuantity) to cart-page-controller. The controller calls updateCartItemQuantity(cartItemId, newQuantity), then on success calls getCartDetails to reload full details.
      - Outcome: Updated quantities and pricing appear in the list and in cart-summary-panel; potential removal if quantity is set to 0 as supported by updateCartItemQuantity.

Remove item:
      - Trigger: User clicks the remove-item-button in a cart-item-row.
      - Behavior: cart-item-row emits onRemoveItem(cartItemId) to cart-page-controller. The controller calls removeCartItem(cartItemId), then on success calls getCartDetails.
      - Outcome: Item is removed from cart-items-list; totals update; empty-cart state may appear if no items remain.

Retry after error:
      - Trigger: An interface call (getCartDetails, updateCartItemQuantity, or removeCartItem) fails; error-notification is shown.
      - Behavior: User clicks a retry control within error-notification.
      - Outcome: cart-page-controller re-invokes the failed interface call and, on success, updates components as usual.

Continue browsing:
      - Trigger: User clicks the "Continue browsing" link/button in cart-actions-bar.
      - Behavior: cart-actions-bar emits onContinueBrowsing; cart-page-controller inspects `from` in the URL and navigates accordingly (e.g., courses.html?view=list or index.html).
      - Outcome: User is redirected to the determined browsing page; cart state remains unchanged.

Proceed to checkout:
      - Trigger: User clicks "Proceed to checkout" in cart-actions-bar when the cart is non-empty.
      - Behavior: cart-actions-bar emits onProceedToCheckout; cart-page-controller raises a navigation intent for the application (actual checkout destination handled elsewhere).
      - Outcome: Application transitions toward a checkout flow without additional cart mutations from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The main explicit URL state is the optional query parameter `from`. On each load of cart.html, cart-page-controller parses the query string and stores `from` as a page-level state value (e.g., originSource). This value is not modified by the page itself. It influences only navigation logic for the "Continue browsing" action. When the user clicks "Continue browsing", cart-page-controller checks originSource:
- If originSource === 'courses', it navigates to courses.html with query parameter view=list (courses.html?view=list), in line with the defined outgoing connection.
- If originSource === 'index', it navigates to index.html.
- For any other value or if originSource is undefined, it defaults to courses.html?view=list.
The cart content and pricing state are not driven by URL parameters; they are entirely derived from the responses of getCartDetails, updateCartItemQuantity, and removeCartItem. After any operation that mutates the cart (quantity change or item removal), the page refreshes its rendered state solely using a fresh getCartDetails call, ensuring consistency with underlying data. Internal UI states such as loading, error, and whether the cart is empty are all derived from the latest interface responses and call statuses, not from URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, data loading, state transitions, and communication between all cart-related components using the assigned interfaces and URL parameters.
Renders the collection of cart items returned by getCartDetails, combining CartItem and Course data per row. Shows an appropriate empty state if there are no items.
Displays a single cart item with course information, unit price, quantity control, line total, and a remove action. Emits events upward when the user changes quantity or removes the item.
Allows the user to adjust the quantity of a given cart item via increment/decrement controls or direct numeric input, enforcing valid integer quantities and delegating confirmed changes upward.
Triggers removal of the associated cart item when clicked, delegating the actual removeCartItem call to the cart-page-controller.
Displays aggregated pricing information from getCartDetails, including subtotal, discounts, tax, total, and currency. Responds to updates after quantity changes or item removals.
Provides primary actions related to the cart: continuing browsing and proceeding to checkout. It enables or disables the checkout action based on whether the cart contains items.
Shows a loading indicator while getCartDetails, updateCartItemQuantity, or removeCartItem calls are in progress. Helps prevent conflicting user actions during asynchronous operations.
Displays error messages when an interface call fails (initial load or subsequent updates) and exposes a retry control that allows re-invoking the failed operation via cart-page-controller.

Shows a dedicated view when getCartDetails returns an empty items array, prompting the user to continue browsing. It interacts with cart-actions-bar for navigation instead of performing navigation itself.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom single-column layout that mirrors the Privacy Policy page: header at the top (cyan utility bar + white nav), followed by a centered content column. Inside this column the sequence is: page title ('Cart') at the top, then status indicators (error or loading) directly beneath the title, then the cart-items-list, followed by the cart-summary-panel, and finally the cart-actions-bar at the bottom. The cart-items-list and cart-summary-panel share the same centered column width to maintain a clean editorial feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom single-column layout that mirrors the Privacy Policy page: header at the top (cyan utility bar + white nav), followed by a centered content column. Inside this column the sequence is: page title ('Cart') at the top, then status indicators (error or loading) directly beneath the title, then the cart-items-list, followed by the cart-summary-panel, and finally the cart-actions-bar at the bottom. The cart-items-list and cart-summary-panel share the same centered column width to maintain a clean editorial feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the full-width header area (top ~15–20% of viewport), the main content sits within a centered container (~1100–1200px max-width). Inside that container, the cart content column for title, items, summary, and actions occupies about 55–65% of the page width, preserving generous left and right margins. Vertical spacing between major blocks (title → status → list → summary → actions) follows a 32–64px rhythm, with slightly tighter spacing between closely related elements (item rows, controls within a row) at 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout preserves the low-density, airy feel of the mockup. Even though the cart adds interactive elements, each cart-item-row is given ample vertical padding and breathing room, with no dense tables or cramped controls. The list remains visually light by limiting the number of visible columns: product information, quantity, and price are aligned in a simple horizontal row, with remove actions treated as subtle secondary elements. The summary panel is concise and compact but still uses generous line spacing and padding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a simple vertical reading pattern: the eye moves from the bright cyan utility bar and white navigation to the centered 'Cart' heading, then directly down through any error/loading messages, into the list of items, then to the prominent summary panel, and finally to the primary checkout action at the bottom. Alignment and consistent column width ensure the eye naturally scrolls down rather than scanning side-to-side, echoing the Privacy Policy page’s strong vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width cyan utility bar and the white navigation bar, the cart page content begins with a generous band of whitespace before a bold, left-aligned or slightly centered 'Cart' page title appears within a centered content container. This container is constrained to around 1100–1200px, while the primary content column (title, items, summary, buttons) sits in the middle 55–65% of that width, bordered by wide, calm margins on both sides. Directly under the title, any error or status messages appear as full-width strips within that column, without breaking the minimal aesthetic. Beneath these, the cart-items-list stacks each cart-item-row vertically, with subtle dividing space between rows instead of heavy borders. Each row uses a simple left-to-right arrangement: product details on the left, quantity control toward the center-right, and price aligned to the far right, with the remove button presented as a light-weight secondary action. Once the items list ends, a slightly emphasized but still minimal summary panel spans the same column width, framing key totals with a soft background or border. At the very bottom of the content column, the cart-actions-bar holds the main checkout button and a lighter secondary action to continue browsing, aligned to the right but nested within the same centered column. Throughout, whitespace and consistent alignment maintain a clear, professional, and airy feel that matches the original mockup while supporting the more transactional cart function.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content area below the existing full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation header. It establishes a centered content container with a max width of approximately 1100–1200px. Within this container, it creates a single primary content column occupying roughly 55–65% of the page width, centered horizontally. The top edge of this column sits about 48–64px below the bottom of the nav bar, providing a generous buffer of whitespace before the 'Cart' title. All cart-related components (title text, status indicators, items list, summary panel, actions bar, and empty/loading states) are stacked vertically within this column, maintaining consistent left and right alignment and equal horizontal padding (16–24px) relative to the column edges.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that scrolls with the page; responsible for centering and constraining all cart content below the global header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-items-list sits within the primary centered column, positioned directly beneath any visible status indicators (or the page title if no statuses are active). There is approximately 32–40px of vertical space between the bottom of the last status message and the top of the first cart-item-row. The list itself spans the full width of the content column (about 55–65% of the page width), with uniform internal horizontal padding in line with the site’s 8px grid (16–24px total padding per side). Each cart-item-row is stacked vertically with roughly 16–24px of vertical spacing between rows, maintaining a light, uncluttered appearance. The list does not appear as a boxed table; instead, it reads like a simple editorial list with subtle dividers or just whitespace separating the items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the main column; grows vertically as items are added and pushes the summary panel and actions bar further down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is a horizontally oriented band within the cart-items-list, spanning the full width of the content column. Internally, the row is divided into three main zones from left to right: the product details block on the left (occupying roughly 55–60% of the row width), the quantity-control block toward the center-right (around 15–20% of the width), and the item price and remove action on the far right (around 20–25% of the width). The product title and any meta information are top-aligned on the left, with comfortable line spacing and about 16–24px of vertical padding at the top and bottom of the row. The quantity-control aligns vertically with the center of the product title area, leaving ample whitespace above and below. The item price is right-aligned within its zone, at the same vertical baseline as the quantity-control for a clean horizontal alignment. The remove-item-button appears as a small, secondary text or icon button either directly under the product title aligned to the left, or just beneath the price aligned to the right; in both cases, it sits with about 8–12px of vertical separation from the primary content to keep the row visually light. Row separation is achieved through whitespace or a very subtle divider line rather than heavy borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level row that participates in the vertical stacking of items; adapts to narrower viewports by stacking the central and right zones below the product details with preserved vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-control is placed within each cart-item-row toward the center-right, occupying a compact horizontal area while remaining visually comfortable. It aligns roughly one-third in from the right edge of the row, sitting between the product details and the price block.
