The control itself is sized to be easily tappable but not visually heavy—approximately 80–120px wide, with 8–12px vertical padding and 12–16px horizontal padding inside, adhering to the 8px grid. It sits on the same horizontal baseline as the item price, with about 8–16px of whitespace separating it from adjacent elements. The minimal styling (flat buttons or steppers, no heavy shadows) echoes the clean, modern aesthetic of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-block within the cart-item-row that maintains its relative position between the product details and price; on smaller screens it may drop to a new line directly beneath the product details, centered or left-aligned within the row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-item-button is a subtle, tertiary-action element inside each cart-item-row. It is visually smaller and lighter than primary buttons—styled as a text link or a minimal icon+label without a heavy filled background. Spatially, it sits at the far right of the row, just below the price, or slightly below the product title on the left if the layout demands a simpler stack on narrow screens. In the standard desktop arrangement, it is right-aligned in the same column as the price, separated by about 4–8px vertically to prevent crowding. The button’s touch target still follows the 8px grid with at least 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, but visually it maintains low prominence, preserving the clean, airy feel of the row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element within the cart-item-row; moves with its parent row and may reflow below other content on narrow viewports while maintaining right or left alignment consistent with the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel appears directly beneath the final cart-item-row, separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical whitespace to denote a new section. It spans the same width as the cart-items-list, aligning perfectly with the content column edges for a cohesive vertical column. The panel itself is a lightly emphasized block—perhaps with a soft background tint or a thin border—but remains flat and minimal, in line with the mockup’s aesthetic. Inside the panel, there is generous internal padding (24–32px on all sides). Summary lines (subtotal, taxes, total) are arranged in a simple vertical list, with labels left-aligned and amounts right-aligned within the panel. The final total may be given slightly more weight (bolder text, slightly larger font) but still maintains the overall clean and restrained feel. The panel occupies roughly 20–30% of the viewport height at most, maintaining the low-density character of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that sits in the vertical flow after the items list; expands vertically as summary content grows, but always matches the width and alignment of the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-actions-bar is positioned directly below the cart-summary-panel, separated by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace. It shares the same centered width as the summary panel and cart-items-list, maintaining the single-column alignment. Within the bar, actions are aligned primarily to the right to support a clear progression toward checkout: the primary button (e.g., 'Proceed to Checkout') appears on the far right, with a slightly less prominent secondary action (e.g., 'Continue Browsing') to its left, separated by 8–16px of horizontal space. The bar has minimal vertical height, with just enough padding (16–24px top and bottom) to keep the buttons comfortably sized without feeling bulky. There is no heavy background; at most, a very subtle top border or light tint can be used to separate the bar from the summary, preserving the minimal, flat design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal action strip that remains at the bottom of the main content column and scrolls with the page; on narrow screens the buttons stack vertically, centered within the column with 8–16px vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-state appears within the main content column, replacing the cart-items-list, summary panel, and actions bar when active. It is centered both horizontally and, as much as possible, vertically within the available content area beneath the page title. The indicator (spinner or progress icon) is placed at the center of this column, with a short status label directly below it, both occupying at most 30–40% of the column height. Ample whitespace surrounds the indicator, mirroring the low content density of the mockup. The width of the loading-state content is narrow (about 30–40% of the page width), centered within the main column so the user’s attention is focused without visual noise.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily replaces the cart-items-list, summary, and actions within the main column while active; scrolls normally with the page and remains centered within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-notification is a full-width banner within the main content column, positioned directly below the page title and above the cart-items-list or loading/empty states. It spans the same width as the primary content column, aligning precisely with the title and other content edges. The notification uses a subtle, flat background color (e.g., a light red or amber tint) with no heavy shadows, and includes 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding. Text is left-aligned for readability, with the message occupying a single, concise line or two at most to maintain the clean visual. There is about 16–24px of vertical space below the notification before the next component, ensuring it feels distinct but not bulky.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline banner that appears in the vertical flow under the title; it pushes other content down but does not overlay or float, and disappears when there are no errors.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-cart-state takes over the main content column when there are no items in the cart. It is positioned beneath the page title (and any error-notification if present), with a large amount of whitespace above and below to reinforce the minimal, editorial feel. The key content—a simple illustration or icon, a short heading like 'Your cart is empty', and a brief explanatory line—sits centered horizontally within the column, using only about 40–50% of the column width. Vertically, this block is placed around the center of the viewport below the header, giving the impression of a light, open space rather than a dense message. A secondary button or link prompting users to continue browsing may appear beneath the message, centered and spaced with 16–24px above and below. The overall impression is calm and uncluttered, closely echoing the sparsely populated Privacy Policy page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block that replaces the cart-items-list, summary, and actions when there are no items; remains centered within the main content column and scrolls normally with the page.

name: Consulting Services & Packages
title: Consulting Services & Packages
description: Page for browsing, filtering, sorting, and viewing details of consulting service packages, with state controlled via URL parameters and backed by consulting package APIs.

core_features: Determine page mode (list or detail) from the 'view' URL parameter and render the appropriate content.
Fetch available audience, industry, and sort options using getConsultingPackageFilterOptions and bind them to filter and sort controls.
List consulting packages using searchConsultingPackages with filters, sort, and pagination derived from URL parameters.
Filter consulting packages by audience and industry, updating the URL parameters and refreshing the list via searchConsultingPackages.
Sort consulting packages by the selected sort option, updating the 'sort' URL parameter and reloading results.
Paginate through consulting packages using the 'page' URL parameter and searchConsultingPackages.
Display detailed information for a specific consulting package using getConsultingPackageDetails when view=detail and packageId is present in the URL.
Display similar consulting packages in the detail view using the similarPackages returned from getConsultingPackageDetails.
Provide navigation from package list items and similarPackages into the detail view by updating 'view' and 'packageId' URL parameters.
Provide a call-to-action in the package detail view that navigates to proposal_request.html with sourceType=consulting_package and consultingPackageId set to the selected package ID.
user_workflows: Browse consulting packages in list view: User navigates to consulting.html without specifying view or with view=list. The page reads URL parameters (audience, industry, sort, page). The page calls getConsultingPackageFilterOptions to populate filter and sort controls. The page then calls searchConsultingPackages with filters based on the URL and displays the resulting packages as a list with pagination. The user adjusts filters and sort options; each change updates the URL parameters and triggers a new searchConsultingPackages call. The user navigates between result pages using pagination controls, which update the 'page' parameter and reload the results.

Open a consulting package detail from the list: In list view, the user clicks a consulting package card. The page navigates to consulting.html with view=detail and packageId set to the clicked package's ID, and preserves current audience, industry, sort, and page values in the URL. On load, the detail view reads packageId from the URL and calls getConsultingPackageDetails. The detailed package information is displayed along with similarPackages. The user can click on a similar package, which updates packageId (and view=detail) in the URL and reloads getConsultingPackageDetails for the new selection.

Request a proposal from a package detail: User is in detail view for a specific package (view=detail and a valid packageId). After reviewing the details and similarPackages, the user clicks the Request Proposal call-to-action. The page navigates to proposal_request.html with sourceType=consulting_package and consultingPackageId set to the current packageId. The proposal request page can then use those parameters to prefill its own context.

Access a package detail via deep link and return to list: User arrives directly on consulting.html with view=detail and a specified packageId from an external page such as index.html. The page calls getConsultingPackageDetails to display the detail. A back-to-list or view-all-packages interaction navigates to consulting.html with view=list and optional audience, industry, sort, and page parameters (if present in the incoming URL) preserved or defaulted. The list view then calls getConsultingPackageFilterOptions and searchConsultingPackages with the resulting URL parameters.

Share or bookmark a filtered and sorted list: User in list view adjusts filters, sort options, and pagination. Each interaction updates the URL parameters (audience, industry, sort, page). The user copies the URL and shares it. Another user opening this URL lands directly on consulting.html with the same filter, sort, and page state applied, because the page re-applies parameters and calls searchConsultingPackages accordingly.
interactions: Change audience filter: User selects an audience option from a control bound to getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.audiences. The page updates the 'audience' URL parameter, resets 'page' to the first page, keeps any existing 'industry' and 'sort' parameters, and triggers searchConsultingPackages with the updated filters.

Change industry filter: User selects or clears an industry option from a control bound to getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.industries. The page updates the 'industry' URL parameter (or removes it when cleared), resets 'page' to the first page, retains 'audience' and 'sort', and calls searchConsultingPackages with the new filters.

Change sort option: User selects a sort option from a control bound to getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.sortOptions. The page updates the 'sort' URL parameter, resets 'page' to the first page, preserves current filters, and calls searchConsultingPackages using the new sort parameter.

Change page: User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number). The page updates the 'page' URL parameter, keeps existing audience, industry, and sort parameters, and calls searchConsultingPackages for the selected page.

Click package card in list: User clicks anywhere on a consulting package card in the list. The page navigates to consulting.html with view=detail, packageId set to the selected package's ID, and the current audience, industry, sort, and page values included in the URL.

Click similar package in detail view: User clicks a similar package item. The page updates the URL to set view=detail and packageId to the clicked similar package ID, preserving audience, industry, sort, and page if present. Then it calls getConsultingPackageDetails for the new packageId.

Click Request Proposal in detail view: User clicks the Request Proposal call-to-action in the detail view. The page navigates to proposal_request.html with sourceType=consulting_package and consultingPackageId set to the current packageId. No API call is made from this page for the proposal.

Navigate directly to list or detail via URL: User manually modifies the URL or arrives from another page with specific parameters. On load, the page reads view, packageId, audience, industry, sort, and page from the URL, normalizes values as needed, and then calls getConsultingPackageFilterOptions and either searchConsultingPackages (for list view) or getConsultingPackageDetails (for detail view).

Back to list from detail: User activates a back-to-list interaction. The page navigates to consulting.html with view=list, and restores or maintains any audience, industry, sort, and page parameters that were present in the URL when entering detail view.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is entirely driven by URL parameters and API responses. The 'view' parameter determines the primary mode: when view=detail and a valid 'packageId' is present, the detail view is active; otherwise, the list view is active (including when 'view' is missing or not recognized). In list view, the page reads 'audience', 'industry', 'sort', and 'page' from the URL. If 'page' is missing or invalid, it is treated as the first page. If 'sort' is missing or not within the options returned by getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.sortOptions, a default sort is applied and represented in the URL. Both 'audience' and 'industry' are optional; when not present, the filters are considered unset. Every filter, sort, and pagination change updates the corresponding URL parameters, so reloading the page or sharing the URL restores the same list state. In detail view, the page reads 'packageId' from the URL; if it is missing or invalid, the page falls back to list view. When entering detail view from list view, the current 'audience', 'industry', 'sort', and 'page' parameters are preserved in the URL so that returning to the list can restore the previous state. Navigation to proposal_request.html from detail view uses query parameters (sourceType=consulting_package, consultingPackageId=current packageId) and does not maintain additional state for this page. The page does not use localStorage or other client-side storage for state; all sharable and restorable state is encoded in the URL parameters and resolved via getConsultingPackageFilterOptions, searchConsultingPackages, and getConsultingPackageDetails.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters on load and on change, determines whether to render the list view or detail view, orchestrates calls to getConsultingPackageFilterOptions, searchConsultingPackages, and getConsultingPackageDetails based on the current state, and passes relevant data and callbacks to child components.

Displays audience and industry filter controls populated from getConsultingPackageFilterOptions. When the user changes a filter, it updates the corresponding URL parameters and requests new search results via the view controller.

Displays a sort control using options returned from getConsultingPackageFilterOptions.sortOptions. Changing the sort option updates the 'sort' URL parameter and triggers a new searchConsultingPackages call through the view controller.

Renders the current page of consulting packages returned from searchConsultingPackages as a list of package cards, and shows contextual information such as total results via the data from searchConsultingPackages.

Represents a single consulting package entry in the list, displaying key attributes from the ConsultingPackage entity, and handles clicks to navigate to the detail view by updating URL parameters (view=detail, packageId, and preserving current list-related parameters).

Provides pagination controls based on searchConsultingPackages.totalCount, searchConsultingPackages.page, and searchConsultingPackages.pageSize. When the user changes page, it updates the 'page' URL parameter and triggers searchConsultingPackages via the view controller.

Displays full details of the selected consulting package using getConsultingPackageDetails.consultingPackage and exposes actions such as navigating back to the list and requesting a proposal. It passes similarPackages to the similar package list component.

Displays similar consulting packages within the detail view using getConsultingPackageDetails.similarPackages and allows the user to switch the detail view to another package by updating the URL packageId and re-calling getConsultingPackageDetails.

Provides the primary call-to-action in the package detail view. When activated, it navigates to proposal_request.html with query parameters sourceType=consulting_package and consultingPackageId equal to the current packageId from the URL.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom, single-column structure from the mockup. Above all consulting components, the page keeps the full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation bar. Below that, a centered page title 'Consulting Services & Packages' introduces the content zone. Immediately under the title, all consulting components (filters, sort, list, pagination, detail, CTA, similar packages) are arranged in a single central column, stacked vertically. The view toggles between a 'list state' (filters + sort + package list + pagination) and a 'detail state' (package detail + CTA + similar packages), controlled by URL/state, but always within the same central column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom, single-column structure from the mockup. Above all consulting components, the page keeps the full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation bar. Below that, a centered page title 'Consulting Services & Packages' introduces the content zone. Immediately under the title, all consulting components (filters, sort, list, pagination, detail, CTA, similar packages) are arranged in a single central column, stacked vertically. The view toggles between a 'list state' (filters + sort + package list + pagination) and a 'detail state' (package detail + CTA + similar packages), controlled by URL/state, but always within the same central column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a generous, editorial feel: the central content column should occupy roughly 60–70% of the page width on desktop (about 7–8 of 12 grid columns), centered with wide left and right margins. Major vertical separations (title → filters, filters/sort → list, list → pagination, detail → CTA → similar packages) use 32–64px spacing. Components themselves have internal padding based on the 8px grid (e.g., 16–24px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). The header and cyan utility bar together keep their ~15–20% viewport height presence at the top, with the remaining 80–85% dedicated to the scrollable consulting content, which still feels airy and uncluttered.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low, mirroring the mockup’s spacious privacy policy page but adapted to a list use case. Filters and sort controls appear as a light, unobtrusive control strip rather than a dense panel. Package cards have plenty of white space, clear separation, and no heavy chrome; each card is concise and vertically roomy with clear breathing room between cards. The detail view follows the same principle: generous line heights and section spacing, no crowded sidebars, no multi-column blocks—just one calm reading column with clearly separated sections and a small number of elements per visual group.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the strong, vertical, scroll-down reading pattern. The eye moves from cyan utility bar → logo and main navigation → large page title → compact control band (filters and sort) → vertical stack of package cards, or, in detail state, title → focused detail content → prominent request proposal CTA → similar packages. Horizontal scanning is minimal and limited to the filter/sort row and content inside each card. High contrast and color are reserved for navigation, key links, and the primary CTA, while most structural elements remain light and flat to keep attention on the content itself.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation bar from the mockup, the page opens into a calm, spacious main content area. Centered on the screen, a single-column layout begins with the bold page title 'Consulting Services & Packages' sitting roughly 40–56px beneath the header, spanning about 60–70% of the page width. Directly under this title, a horizontally oriented but visually light control zone features the filter bar aligned to the left/center of the main column and the sort control aligned to the right, with ample padding above and below so it never feels cramped. Beneath this, a vertical stack of full-width package cards flows down the page within the same central column, each card separated by generous whitespace and framed by subtle borders rather than shadows. Pagination controls sit centered after the list, acting as a natural break in the scroll. When a package is opened via URL/state, the list and pagination are replaced by a detail view occupying the same central column, using tall section spacing and clear typography to support longer copy. Immediately below the main detail content, a more visually assertive but still flat CTA band invites users to request a proposal, followed by a compact list of similar packages that echoes the card style in a lighter, secondary way. Throughout, the wide left and right margins, flat design, and consistent vertical rhythm ensure the page feels modern, professional, and easy to scan, with the user’s attention guided straight down the center of the screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it sits behind the main content column, controlling whether the user sees the list state or the detail state. In the list state, it ensures that the filter bar, sort control, package list, and pagination occupy the central column beneath the page title, while the detail-related components remain hidden. In the detail state, it hides the list and pagination and instead places the package detail, request proposal CTA, and similar packages list into that same central column area. It does not take up any space in the layout; it only orchestrates which components occupy the main vertical flow under the page title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering logical controller; does not occupy layout space or affect flow directly, only toggles visibility of other components within the same single-column content region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned directly beneath the page title within the main content region. On desktop, it begins about 32–40px below the title, aligned to the left edge of the central content column, and spans roughly 70–80% of that column’s width, leaving some breathing room to the right where the sort control visually balances the row. The content column itself sits centered on the page and occupies around 60–70% of the overall page width, so the filter bar appears as a wide but not full-bleed strip of controls floating within generous side margins. Internally, the filter chips, dropdowns, or toggles are arranged horizontally with 16–24px gaps, and there is 12–16px of vertical padding above and below the controls, following the light, flat look (no heavy boxes—thin lines or subtle backgrounds only). On tablet and mobile, the filter bar stretches to nearly 100% of the available width inside 16–24px side padding, and its elements may wrap onto multiple lines, but it always remains within its own vertical block, clearly separated from the title above and the list/sort area below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element in the normal flow. On narrower screens, its height expands as filters wrap; it may break onto multiple rows but remains anchored directly beneath the page title and above the package list/sort area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control shares the same horizontal band as the filter bar to create a single, cohesive control strip. On desktop, it is positioned in the central content column’s top control area, right-aligned to the column’s right edge, visually opposite the filter bar. The sort control typically occupies about 20–30% of the column width, leaving room for a label and a dropdown or segmented control. It sits on the same baseline or just a few pixels lower than the primary filter elements, preserving a clean, linear feel. Vertical padding around it mirrors the filter bar (about 12–16px top and bottom), so the entire control zone reads as a single, airy section between the title and the list. On tablet and mobile, when horizontal space is constrained, the sort control drops to a second line beneath the filter bar, aligned to the left of the column with a 8–16px vertical gap, maintaining the same generous left/right margins and avoiding any cramped side-by-side alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element that aligns right within the control row on large screens and stacks beneath the filter bar on smaller screens. It moves responsively but does not float or stick; it remains part of the normal vertical flow just above the package list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package list sits immediately below the control zone formed by the filter bar and sort control. On desktop, its top edge begins roughly 32–40px below the bottom of the control strip, maintaining clear visual separation while still feeling connected. The list container aligns with the central content column, spanning essentially the full width of that column (about 60–70% of the page width) and centered horizontally, leaving large left and right gutters. Within this container, packages are arranged in a vertical stack of cards, one per row, with no multi-column layout, preserving the simple top-to-bottom reading pattern. Each card is separated by 16–24px of vertical space, ensuring that individual packages are clearly distinguishable. On mobile and tablet, the list continues to be a single column, stretching to almost full width minus standard side padding (16–24px), with vertical spacing and card proportions kept consistent to maintain the airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content within the main column. It expands vertically as more items are loaded but never introduces horizontal scroll; it always remains a single vertical list aligned to the centered content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each package card is a full-width row inside the package list’s central column. Horizontally, the card spans nearly the entire width of the list container, with internal padding of about 24px vertically and 24–32px horizontally, creating a generous white frame around the content. The card background is white or very light, sitting on a slightly different page background for subtle differentiation, with a thin 1px border or light outline instead of any shadow, matching the flat corporate aesthetic. Content inside the card flows from top to bottom: the package name and key label are aligned to the left at the top; below that, a short description and key bullet points sit in a single column. To the right side (on desktop), secondary elements like price or duration can be aligned in the same row as the title or just beneath, but the primary structure remains a left-anchored, single-column reading experience, not a dense grid. At the bottom right of the card, a discreet 'View details' link or button sits with 8–16px margin from the content, giving a clear action without overwhelming the layout. Vertical spacing between text groups inside the card follows the 8–16px rhythm, keeping the card content readable and uncluttered. On smaller screens, all content simply stacks vertically inside the same padded rectangle, preserving the generous whitespace and avoiding compression.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard list item within the scrolling list. It grows vertically based on content but maintains consistent padding and margins. Cards never overlap; they simply stack, keeping the simple, linear reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed directly below the last package card in the list, separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical space so they read as a distinct, concluding element of the list rather than part of the last card. Horizontally, the pagination module is centered within the main content column, occupying only as much width as needed for page numbers, arrows, and perhaps a per-page selector—typically around 30–40% of the column width. This creates a compact, well-balanced control cluster surrounded by ample whitespace on all sides. The buttons or page numbers follow the flat aesthetic, using clear typography and subtle outlines or background fills, with 8–12px horizontal gaps between items to keep the layout breathable. On smaller screens, pagination remains centered, with controls wrapping to a second row if required, but always within the same central block and without any horizontal scrolling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element at the end of the list, part of the normal scroll flow. It does not stick or float; as users scroll, it moves offscreen like any other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the detail state, the package detail component takes over the same central column that the list would normally occupy. It appears directly beneath the page title (and optionally beneath the filter/sort strip if those remain visible in this state), with about 32–48px of vertical space separating it from the element above. The component spans the full width of the main content column, about 60–70% of the total page width, and remains centered with generous left and right margins. Inside, content is structured into clear vertical sections—overview, outcomes, methodology, ideal audience, etc.—each separated by 24–40px of vertical spacing, reinforcing an editorial, easy-to-scan flow. Headings are left-aligned, with body text running in a single column, line length kept comfortable by the column width. Any secondary details (like bullet-point inclusions or FAQs) are kept within this same single column, avoiding multi-column complexity. At the bottom of the detail content, before any CTA or related content, there is an extra 32–40px of whitespace to signal the end of the descriptive section and prepare the eye for the subsequent CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content, replacing the list in the main column when active. It expands vertically as needed for content but does not introduce sidebars or sticky elements; all reading occurs in a single top-to-bottom flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The similar packages list is positioned beneath the request proposal CTA in the detail view state. It begins after a clear vertical gap—approximately 40–56px below the CTA—to visually separate primary conversion content from secondary exploratory options. Like the main list, this component aligns to the central content column and spans its full width, about 60–70% of the page. At the top of this section, a left-aligned heading such as 'You may also be interested in' or 'Similar Packages' sits with 16–24px spacing above the first card. Below the heading, 2–4 compact package cards are stacked vertically, echoing the styling of the main package cards but slightly more compressed (e.g., slightly less text, 16–20px internal padding) to emphasize their secondary, exploratory role. Vertical spacing between similar package cards is around 16–24px, matching the rhythm of the main list while keeping the section feeling light and optional. On smaller screens, the structure remains a single column with the same padding and centering behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block at the bottom of the detail view’s flow. It scrolls normally and does not stick or float; it simply provides additional options when users reach the end of the detailed content and CTA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The request proposal CTA is placed directly beneath the main explanatory content of the package detail, acting as the primary action once users finish reading. It begins about 32–40px below the final paragraph or section of the detail component and spans the full width of the central content column (about 60–70% of the page width), remaining centered. Visually, it is designed as a low-profile but clearly distinct band or card: a flat, slightly tinted background or cyan-accented border distinguishes it from the white page while still respecting the minimalist aesthetic. Inside, content is arranged in a clean, single-row or stacked layout: a concise, left-aligned heading and supporting sentence, with a primary button that may be either right-aligned on desktop or centered beneath the text on smaller screens. Internal padding is generous—24–32px vertically and 24–32px horizontally—so the CTA stands out through size and whitespace rather than heavy decoration. It is visually more prominent than surrounding text and the similar packages list but still integrated into the same vertical rhythm. On mobile, all CTA elements stack vertically, centered within the block, while maintaining the same padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block within the normal scrolling flow. It does not float or stick; it appears after the detail content and before similar packages, creating a clear, linear progression from understanding the service to taking action.

name: Proposal Request
title: Proposal Request
description: Page for requesting a proposal or quote for consulting packages or custom training configurations, using context passed via URL parameters and collecting company and engagement details.

core_features: Read URL parameters (sourceType, consultingPackageId, customTrainingConfigurationId) and validate that they form a valid context for a proposal request.
Call getProposalContext with the URL parameters to load the relevant consulting package or custom training configuration summary, including summaryText, estimateTotal, and currency.
Display a contextual summary panel showing the selected consulting package or custom training configuration based on the data returned by getProposalContext.
Provide a structured proposal request form to collect company information (company name, company size, contact name, email, phone).
Provide fields to capture engagement details, including project goals, project scope, and preferred start date using a date picker control.
Validate required fields (including email format and preferred start date presence) before allowing submission of the proposal request.
Submit the proposal request through submitProposalRequest using the current context and form inputs, and handle success or failure responses.
Display a confirmation or thank-you message with the response from submitProposalRequest when the request is successfully submitted.
Handle and display error states for invalid URL parameters, failed getProposalContext calls, validation errors, and failed submitProposalRequest calls.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Accessing the page with consulting package context: 1) User clicks a proposal/quote call-to-action on consulting.html; 2) consulting.html navigates to proposal_request.html with sourceType set to 'consulting_package' and consultingPackageId set to the selected package ID; 3) Proposal Request page reads URL parameters and validates that sourceType is 'consulting_package' and consultingPackageId is present; 4) Page calls getProposalContext with sourceType, consultingPackageId, and an empty customTrainingConfigurationId; 5) On success, the page displays the consulting package summary and preps the form; 6) User fills in company info, contact details, project goals, project scope, and preferred start date; 7) User submits the form; 8) Page validates inputs and, if valid, calls submitProposalRequest with the consulting context and form data; 9) On success, page shows confirmation and hides or disables the form.

Workflow 2 – Accessing the page with custom training configuration context: 1) User configures a custom training scenario on pricing.html; 2) User selects an action to request a formal proposal, which navigates to proposal_request.html with sourceType set to 'custom_training_configuration' and customTrainingConfigurationId set; 3) Proposal Request page reads URL parameters and validates that sourceType is 'custom_training_configuration' and customTrainingConfigurationId is present; 4) Page calls getProposalContext with sourceType, the given customTrainingConfigurationId, and an empty consultingPackageId; 5) On success, the page displays a summary of the configuration (using summaryText, estimateTotal, currency) and prepares the form; 6) User completes company and engagement details, including preferred start date through the date picker; 7) User submits the form; 8) Page runs validation and calls submitProposalRequest with the configuration context and form data; 9) On success, page shows a confirmation message and may indicate that a follow-up will occur.

Workflow 3 – Handling invalid or incomplete context: 1) User or another page navigates directly to proposal_request.html with missing or invalid sourceType or missing required IDs; 2) On page load, the URL validation logic determines that parameters are invalid (e.g., sourceType not recognized, expected ID missing); 3) The page does not call getProposalContext, instead showing an error banner indicating that proposal context could not be loaded; 4) The form and submit actions are disabled until the URL is corrected; 5) A user can adjust the URL parameters or navigate back to consulting.html or pricing.html to start the request correctly.

Workflow 4 – Handling API and validation errors: 1) User reaches Proposal Request page with valid parameters; 2) getProposalContext is called but returns an error or empty content; 3) The page displays an error banner and does not show the context summary; 4) The form submission is blocked until a valid context is available; 5) In another scenario, getProposalContext is successful and the user fills the form but omits required fields or enters an invalid email; 6) When the user clicks submit, client-side validation runs and highlights the fields with issues and prevents submitProposalRequest from being called; 7) After correcting errors, the user submits again; 8) If submitProposalRequest returns an error, the page shows a submission error banner and keeps the form data so the user can attempt resubmission.

Workflow 5 – Post-submission state and navigation: 1) After a successful submitProposalRequest call, the page receives a response with success=true, message, and a ProposalRequest object; 2) The form is switched into a submitted state (e.g., fields disabled or hidden) and a confirmation component displays the returned message and key details from proposalRequest; 3) The URL parameters remain unchanged so that the confirmation state can be re-derived on soft refresh using in-memory flags; 4) If the page is reloaded fully, it returns to the initial request state and would require resubmission if the user wants to send another request.
interactions: On initial page load, when the URL changes, or when the page is opened: the routing and context initialization logic reads sourceType, consultingPackageId, and customTrainingConfigurationId from the URL query string and validates the combination.
If URL parameters are valid, the page triggers a call to getProposalContext with the parsed parameters; while waiting for the response, a loading state is set for the context-dependent components.

When getProposalContext returns successfully, the proposal summary panel is populated with summaryText, estimateTotal, currency, and any available data from consultingPackage or customTrainingConfiguration.
If getProposalContext fails or returns invalid data, an error state is set; an error banner component shows the error, and the form is disabled to prevent submission without context.

The user interacts with the company information fields (company name text input, company size select, contact name text input, email text input, phone text input); on each change, component state is updated and validation feedback may be updated.

The user interacts with the engagement details fields (project goals multiline input, project scope multiline input, preferred start date date picker); the date picker ensures a valid date object or standardized date string is captured in state.

On clicking the primary submit button in the proposal request form, client-side validation runs: it checks that email is present and matches a basic email pattern, preferred start date has been chosen, and any other required fields are not empty.
If validation fails, the form component prevents submitProposalRequest from being called and surfaces inline validation messages or an aggregated message via the error banner.

If validation passes, the form component calls submitProposalRequest with the current sourceType, consultingPackageId, customTrainingConfigurationId, and the user-entered values for companyName, companySize, contactName, email, phone, projectGoals, projectScope, and preferredStartDate.
While submitProposalRequest is in progress, the submit button is disabled (and optionally a loading state is set) to prevent duplicate submissions.
If submitProposalRequest returns success=false, the page shows an error message using the returned message, keeps the form populated, and re-enables the submit button for another attempt.

If submitProposalRequest returns success=true, the page saves the returned proposalRequest object into local component state, shows a confirmation component based on the response, and may disable or hide the form to prevent further edits.

If the user navigates back from the confirmation state (e.g., using browser back), the URL-based state is re-read; the page may re-run getProposalContext to rebuild the summary and form, without resubmitting the previous request automatically.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily driven by URL query parameters and API responses. On every initial load, the page reads the URL parameters sourceType, consultingPackageId, and customTrainingConfigurationId. The combination is validated as follows: (1) sourceType must be present and equal to either 'consulting_package' or 'custom_training_configuration'; (2) if sourceType is 'consulting_package', consultingPackageId must be present and non-empty, and customTrainingConfigurationId is ignored; (3) if sourceType is 'custom_training_configuration', customTrainingConfigurationId must be present and non-empty, and consultingPackageId is ignored. If validation fails, the page enters an 'invalid_context' state, skips the getProposalContext call, disables the form submission, and displays an error. If validation succeeds, the page enters a 'loading_context' state and calls getProposalContext with the URL parameters. On a successful getProposalContext response, state transitions to 'context_ready', and the summary data (consultingPackage, customTrainingConfiguration, summaryText, estimateTotal, currency) is stored in page-level or component-level state. These values are then read by the summary and form components for display and for constructing the submitProposalRequest payload. The form component maintains its own internal state for user inputs (companyName, companySize, contactName, email, phone, projectGoals, projectScope, preferredStartDate) and validation errors. The preferredStartDate state is updated via the date picker UI and stored in a format acceptable to submitProposalRequest (e.g., a standardized datetime or ISO string, without hardcoding an exact format here). On form submission, if validation passes, the page enters a 'submitting' state and calls submitProposalRequest. Based on the submitProposalRequest response, the page transitions to either 'submission_error' (keeping all form state and exposing error messages) or 'submitted' (storing the returned ProposalRequest and showing the confirmation state). URL parameters are not modified by the page during this process; they continue to represent the context origin and IDs. The 'submitted' state is held in in-memory component state only and is not persisted in the URL, meaning a full reload resets the page back to 'context_ready' with an empty form.

components.functionality: Reads and validates URL parameters (sourceType, consultingPackageId, customTrainingConfigurationId) on page load, determines if the parameters represent a valid context, and triggers the getProposalContext call when appropriate. It sets top-level page state flags such as invalid_context, loading_context, and context_ready.

Invokes getProposalContext with the validated sourceType, consultingPackageId, and customTrainingConfigurationId from the router component. Handles loading and error states for context retrieval and stores the resulting summaryText, estimateTotal, currency, and any consultingPackage or customTrainingConfiguration objects into shared state for other components to consume.

Displays a read-only summary of the proposal context returned by getProposalContext. For a consulting package, it shows information derived from the ConsultingPackage object and summaryText; for a custom training configuration, it shows information derived from the CustomTrainingConfiguration object and summaryText. It also presents estimateTotal and currency if available. If context loading fails or is invalid, the component either hides itself or shows a contextual message based on the error state from the loader.

Shows error messages for invalid URL parameters, getProposalContext failures, and submitProposalRequest failures. It listens to error flags and messages from the router, context loader, and form components and presents them in a consolidated way. It can also expose an action for the user to retry loading context when applicable.

Collects user inputs required to submit a proposal request. Fields include companyName, companySize (mapped to the ProposalRequest.company_size enum values), contactName, email, phone, projectGoals, projectScope, and preferredStartDate selected via a date picker. Before submission, it validates required fields (including checking that email is non-empty and matches a basic pattern, and that preferredStartDate has been selected). If validation succeeds, it calls submitProposalRequest with the current context (sourceType, consultingPackageId, customTrainingConfigurationId) and the form values. It manages local form state, validation errors, and a submitting flag to prevent duplicate submissions.

Provides a controlled date selection input for preferredStartDate, ensuring that selected values are valid dates and converting them into a standardized datetime representation suitable for the preferredStartDate parameter of submitProposalRequest. Integrates with the form's validation to mark the field as required and to clear related validation errors when a valid date is selected.

Displays a confirmation or thank-you message once submitProposalRequest returns success=true. It uses the returned message and ProposalRequest object to show key details such as company_name, source_type, and any available package_name_snapshot or configuration_summary without hardcoding any specific values. It is rendered only when the page is in the 'submitted' state and can hide or disable the proposal-request-form component.

Coordinates high-level page states such as invalid_context, loading_context, context_ready, submitting, submission_error, and submitted. It listens to events from the router, context loader, form, and confirmation components and determines which components should be active or disabled at any given time. It does not modify URL parameters but uses them as authoritative context inputs.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup. Above the main content, the global cyan utility bar and white navigation/header remain unchanged. Within the main content area, all proposal-related components sit in a single, centered column: first any error/notification at the top of the content column, then a concise proposal summary, followed by the main proposal request form (which embeds the date picker), and finally a submission confirmation state that appears in the same central column once the form is successfully submitted. Non-visual components (router, loader, state manager) wrap or control these pieces but do not introduce new visible regions.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup. Above the main content, the global cyan utility bar and white navigation/header remain unchanged. Within the main content area, all proposal-related components sit in a single, centered column: first any error/notification at the top of the content column, then a concise proposal summary, followed by the main proposal request form (which embeds the date picker), and finally a submission confirmation state that appears in the same central column once the form is successfully submitted. Non-visual components (router, loader, state manager) wrap or control these pieces but do not introduce new visible regions.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main proposal content is confined to a centered column that spans roughly 50–60% of the page width, inside a max-width container of about 1100–1200px, mirroring the mockup’s narrow text column. The header and cyan bar together occupy the top 15–20% of the viewport. Between the bottom of the header and the start of the proposal content, there is generous vertical spacing (around 48–64px). Inside the main column, blocks (error banner, summary, form, confirmation) are separated by 24–40px of vertical space, maintaining an airy feel. Side margins remain wide, creating substantial whitespace to the left and right of the central content on desktop.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and editorial. Each interactive block (summary panel, form, confirmation) is visually simple, with plenty of breathing room between fields and sections. The form is broken into clear, vertically stacked sections instead of dense multi-column layouts. No sidebars or secondary columns compete with the main content; only a few key blocks are visible at once, each occupying its own vertical slice with ample padding (16–24px internal padding) and spacing (32–40px) around them.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow inherits the mockup’s strong vertical reading pattern. The eye travels from the cyan utility bar to the logo and navigation, then drops directly into the main content column where it first encounters any error banner (if present), then the proposal summary, then the request form, and finally the confirmation message. All elements are center-aligned within the content column, encouraging a simple scroll-down interaction instead of complex scanning. The primary call to action (form submit) sits near the bottom of the form within this flow, and after submission the confirmation appears in the same central region, maintaining continuity.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the page opens into a calm, spacious canvas dominated by a single narrow column of content centered on the screen. Generous whitespace separates the header from the proposal area, which feels like a focused document rather than an app interface. At the top of this column, any error or status banner stretches across the full width of the column, followed by a compact, lightly framed proposal summary that reminds users what they’re requesting. Beneath it, the main proposal request form flows vertically in clearly separated sections—company details, engagement details, training date selection, and contact information—each with comfortable padding and consistent spacing aligned with an 8px rhythm. The date picker appears as a standard form field within this column, with its calendar overlay anchored to it. After submission, a confirmation message occupies the same central space as the form, preserving the clean, linear reading pattern. Throughout, wide side margins and restrained styling keep attention on the content, echoing the minimal, corporate yet approachable aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component sits logically at the very top of the page’s internal structure, above all visible proposal components but below the global cyan utility bar and header in the DOM hierarchy. It does not render any visual UI and occupies no visible space on the page. Conceptually, it wraps the proposal area and initializes context before the summary panel, form, or confirmation render, but it does not affect alignment, spacing, or the centered, single-column layout of the visible elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; behaves as a structural/context wrapper with zero height and width, not altering the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned just inside the main proposal content tree, this component also has no direct visual footprint. It sits conceptually above the proposal-summary-panel and proposal-request-form within the centered column, controlling their loading and empty states. During loading, its influence is seen as temporary placeholders or skeletons within those components rather than as a separate block. It therefore shares the same central column alignment but does not introduce its own spacing or area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; it does not create its own block in the layout but toggles what is displayed inside the existing centered components. It does not modify the main column’s width or vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary panel is the first substantial visible block within the main proposal area, appearing beneath the page’s internal title/subtitle (e.g., 'Proposal Request') and any optional error banner. It is centered on the page inside the main container, matching the text column width of approximately 50–60% of the overall page width. The panel appears as a simple, flat card with light borders or subtle background differentiation, and internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides. Vertically, there is a generous gap of roughly 32–48px from the bottom of the page title to the top of this panel, and about 24–32px between the bottom of the panel and the top of the proposal-request-form. Content inside the panel is vertically stacked: a concise heading (e.g., 'Your Proposal Overview') at the top, followed by 2–5 short lines summarizing selected services or configuration, all left-aligned but contained within the centered card. The card does not stretch full-width; ample whitespace remains on both sides to maintain the airy, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component participating in normal document flow. It scales responsively with the main content column, maintaining centered alignment and proportional top/bottom margins on different viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner occupies the topmost position within the main proposal content column, directly beneath the bottom edge of the white header/navigation area and above the proposal-summary-panel. When visible, it spans the full width of the central content column (roughly 50–60% of the page width), centered on the page. It appears as a flat, high-contrast bar (e.g., soft red or amber) with text left-aligned inside and 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding. There is clear separation—around 24–32px—between the bottom of the error banner and the next block (typically the summary panel). When not shown, this space collapses entirely so the summary panel slides up under the header. The banner does not extend to the full browser width, maintaining the same wide left/right margins as the rest of the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears in the normal document flow, pushing subsequent content down when visible. It is not sticky or overlayed; it scrolls away naturally with the content as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The request form is the dominant central block on the page, positioned directly below the proposal-summary-panel (or below the error banner if the summary is omitted), with a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px. It sits in the same centered, single-column track, taking up roughly 50–60% of the page width on desktop, aligned with the summary panel’s edges. Internally, the form is divided into clearly separated vertical sections—such as company information, engagement details, training date and logistics, and requester contact info—each separated by 24–32px of vertical space. Form fields are arranged mostly in a single-column layout to preserve low density; any side-by-side fields (like city and postal code) should still feel roomy, with at least 16px of horizontal gap. The form container itself may have a subtle card treatment with 16–24px of internal padding and a flat appearance consistent with the design DNA. The main submit button is placed near the bottom of the form, centered or left-aligned within the content column, with 24–32px of whitespace above and below it to prevent visual clutter. Overall, the form reads like a simple, vertical document, encouraging a straightforward top-to-bottom completion flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable block within the normal document flow. Its height grows with content, pushing the page downward. On narrow screens, it flexes to nearly full width while maintaining generous vertical spacing between fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The date picker is embedded as a specific input field within the proposal-request-form rather than as a standalone section. It occupies a single row in the form’s vertical stack, aligned to the left within the central column and spanning the full width available for a form field (typically 100% of the form’s content width). Vertically, it is surrounded by the same spacing as other fields—around 16–24px above and below—to maintain rhythm. Visually, the closed state looks like a standard text input with a date label and possibly a calendar icon on the right, with flat styling. When the user interacts with it, the calendar overlay appears directly below (or overlapping just below) the input, aligned so its left edge is flush with the input’s left edge. The overlay floats above other form content but stays visually anchored to this field within the central column, not extending beyond the column’s width. Adequate space (at least 8–16px) separates the calendar overlay from adjacent fields so it doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form’s layout in its resting state. On open, it temporarily creates an overlay panel that overlaps subsequent form content but is constrained to the main column’s horizontal bounds. The overlay scrolls with the page and closes when interaction ends, returning the form to its simple vertical stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission confirmation appears in the same central column and approximate vertical position that the form previously occupied, ensuring continuity in the user’s visual flow. After a successful submission, it either replaces the proposal-request-form within the layout or appears immediately below a dimmed/hidden form area, while preserving the centered, 50–60%-width column alignment. The confirmation block is styled as a simple, airy panel—similar in width to the form—with 16–24px of internal padding and generous whitespace (32–48px) above and below. Content is minimal and easy to scan: a bold confirmation heading at the top, a short explanatory paragraph, and possibly a small list of next steps. All text is left-aligned within the panel but centered within the page’s main column. The overall effect feels calm and conclusive, with no additional dense controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed conditionally in the normal document flow, occupying the same spatial region as the form. When active, it may completely hide the form or push it out of view, but it does not change the overall page structure or column width. It scrolls naturally with the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component sits behind the scenes, logically wrapping the summary, form, error banner, and confirmation within the main content column. It coordinates which of those components are visible at any time—e.g., switching from the form view to the confirmation view—without rendering any UI of its own. It does not alter the page’s centered, single-column layout and introduces no visual boundaries or spacing; instead, it controls which existing blocks occupy the established space under the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual state controller with no direct spatial footprint. It determines which visible components participate in the document flow but does not itself consume space or affect alignment.

name: Workshops & Programs
title: Workshops & Programs
description: Page for browsing, filtering, and reviewing workshops and programs, with the ability to view detailed information and save workshops into a Training Plan.

core_features: Load available workshop filter and sort options using getWorkshopFilterOptions and render them in a filter/sort UI.
Display a paginated list of workshops using searchWorkshops, based on URL-controlled filters, sort order, and page number.
Allow users to refine the workshop list by category, format, and duration type, updating URL parameters and re-running searchWorkshops.
Allow users to sort workshops by a selected sort option from getWorkshopFilterOptions, updating URL parameters and re-running searchWorkshops.
Provide navigation from the list view to a workshop detail view using the view and workshopId URL parameters.
Load full workshop details, including agenda and expected outcomes, using getWorkshopDetails when view=detail.
Allow users to save a workshop to their Training Plan via addWorkshopToTrainingPlan, optionally including priority and notes.
Provide a "Back to Workshops" navigation from the detail view that restores the prior filter, sort, and pagination state via URL parameters.
Provide a navigation link to training_plan.html after at least one successful call to addWorkshopToTrainingPlan.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial list view load
1. User navigates to workshops.html without URL parameters or with view=list.
2. Page controller reads URL parameters (view, categories, format, durationTypes, sort, page).
3. If view is missing or not 'detail', controller normalizes view to 'list'.
4. Controller calls getWorkshopFilterOptions to obtain available categories, formats, durationTypes, and sortOptions.
5. Controller constructs a filters object from the URL parameters (splitting comma-separated values where needed) and determines sort and page values (defaulting page if missing or invalid).
6. Controller calls searchWorkshops with the filters, sort, page, and a configured pageSize.
7. The workshop list, sort controls, filters, and pagination components bind to the searchWorkshops response and render the workshops.
8. The current filter/sort state is reflected in the UI controls and in the URL parameters.

Workflow 2: Refining the list with filters
1. User is on view=list with a rendered workshop list.
2. User changes a filter in the filter UI (e.g., selects a category, format, or duration type).
3. The filter component updates its internal filter state and sets page to the first page.
4. The filter component updates the URL parameters (categories, format, durationTypes, page, and retains current sort) via navigation or history update.
5. Page controller detects the URL change, reconstructs the filters object, and calls searchWorkshops with the new filters, current sort, page=1, and pageSize.
6. The workshop list rerenders with the filtered items, and the filters UI reflects the new selection state.

Workflow 3: Changing sort order
1. User is on view=list with a current list of workshops.
2. User selects a sort option from the sort control.
3. The sort control updates the sort value in the URL parameters, resets page to the first page, and preserves current filters.
4. Page controller reconstructs filters and calls searchWorkshops with the same filters, new sort, page=1, and pageSize.
5. The workshop list rerenders, displaying items in the new sort order, and the sort control reflects the selected option.

Workflow 4: Pagination
1. User is on view=list and the workshop list shows pagination controls based on totalCount, page, and pageSize from searchWorkshops.
2. User clicks a pagination control (next page, previous page, or a specific page number).
3. The pagination component updates the page URL parameter while preserving categories, format, durationTypes, and sort.
4. Page controller reads updated URL, reconstructs the filters object, and calls searchWorkshops with the same filters, sort, and the new page.
5. The workshop list rerenders to show workshops for the selected page, and the pagination controls highlight the current page.

Workflow 5: Viewing workshop details from the list
1. User is on view=list and clicks on a workshop card.
2. The card click triggers a navigation to workshops.html with view=detail, workshopId set to the clicked workshop's id, and current categories, format, durationTypes, sort, and page values passed through in the URL.
3. Page controller reads URL parameters; since view=detail and workshopId is present, it calls getWorkshopDetails with workshopId.
4. The detail view component renders workshop details, the agenda, and expected outcomes.
5. The detail view displays a 'Back to Workshops' link that uses the current URL parameters to navigate back to view=list with the same categories, format, durationTypes, sort, and page values.
6. If the user clicks 'Back to Workshops', the page returns to the list view with the original filter/sort/pagination state, and the list is reloaded via searchWorkshops using those parameters.

Workflow 6: Direct deep link to a workshop detail
1. User arrives at workshops.html with view=detail and a workshopId in the URL, potentially without filter or sort parameters.
2. Page controller reads URL parameters and confirms view=detail and workshopId are set.
3. Controller calls getWorkshopDetails with workshopId and renders the detail view.
4. For 'Back to Workshops', the detail component generates a URL with view=list and includes whatever filter/sort/page parameters are present (if any). If none are present, the URL only sets view=list.
5. When the user clicks 'Back to Workshops', the controller detects view=list with any existing filter/sort/page parameters (or defaults) and calls searchWorkshops to render the list view.

Workflow 7: Saving a workshop to the Training Plan from detail view
1. User is on a workshop detail view (view=detail, workshopId set) and reviews the content.
2. User clicks a 'Save to Training Plan' button.
3. The detail view displays a form (or inline controls) for selecting a priority and entering optional notes.
4. User submits the form; the component calls addWorkshopToTrainingPlan with the current workshopId, chosen priority, and notes.
5. On a successful response (success=true), the component can show a success message and optionally update any local indicator that this workshop is part of the Training Plan.
6. After a successful save, the component exposes a 'View My Training Plan' navigation link.
7. If the user clicks 'View My Training Plan', the browser navigates to training_plan.html without additional URL parameters (as described in outgoing navigation).

Workflow 8: Saving a workshop to the Training Plan from list view (optional pattern)
1. User is on view=list and sees a list of workshop cards.
2. Each card may include a 'Save to Training Plan' action.
3. When the user clicks this action on a card, an inline form or minimal control is shown for priority and notes.
4. On submit, the list component calls addWorkshopToTrainingPlan with that card's workshopId, selected priority, and notes.
5. On success, the card displays a confirmation or a state indicator that the item is saved, and may provide a 'View My Training Plan' link that navigates to training_plan.html.
6. The filter/sort/page URL parameters remain unchanged; the user can continue browsing without losing list state.

Workflow 9: Handling invalid or incomplete URL parameters
1. User accesses workshops.html with missing or invalid values for view, page, or other parameters.
2. Page controller normalizes view to 'list' if it is missing or not recognized.
3. If view=detail is requested without a workshopId, the controller treats this as an invalid state and navigates or falls back to view=list, reconstructing filters from whatever parameters are valid.
4. If page is missing or invalid, the controller defaults page to a valid starting value and updates the URL accordingly.
5. After normalization, searchWorkshops or getWorkshopDetails is called as appropriate for the final state.
interactions: On page load, the controller reads URL parameters and calls getWorkshopFilterOptions once to populate filter and sort controls.
Changing any filter input (category, format, duration type) updates the corresponding URL parameters, resets page to the first page, and triggers searchWorkshops with updated filters.
Changing the sort control updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to the first page, and triggers searchWorkshops with the same filters and new sort.
Clicking a pagination control updates the page URL parameter (while retaining filters and sort) and triggers searchWorkshops with the same filters and sort for the new page.
Clicking a workshop card in the list view triggers navigation to workshops.html with view=detail, workshopId, and the current filters, sort, and page encoded in the URL.
When view=detail is active, the controller calls getWorkshopDetails with workshopId, and the detail component binds to the workshop, agenda, and expectedOutcomes fields.
Clicking 'Save to Training Plan' in the detail view opens or submits a form that calls addWorkshopToTrainingPlan with workshopId, priority, and notes.
On a successful addWorkshopToTrainingPlan response, the UI shows confirmation and reveals a 'View My Training Plan' navigation link to training_plan.html.
Clicking 'Save to Training Plan' from a workshop card (if implemented) calls addWorkshopToTrainingPlan with the card's workshopId, priority, and notes, without changing URL filter/sort parameters.
Clicking 'Back to Workshops' in the detail view navigates to workshops.html with view=list and the last-known categories, format, durationTypes, sort, and page values preserved in the URL.

Manually editing URL parameters (e.g., changing view or filters in the address bar) and reloading the page will cause the controller to parse the parameters, normalize them, and call getWorkshopFilterOptions and searchWorkshops or getWorkshopDetails accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page is fully driven by URL parameters and interface results, without using localStorage.

- Primary state parameters:
  - view: Controls high-level mode. If view='detail' and workshopId is valid, the page shows the detail view. Any other view value is treated as 'list'. Missing view defaults to 'list'.
  - workshopId: Used only when view='detail'. If missing or invalid when view='detail', the controller falls back to view='list'.
  - categories: A comma-separated string of category identifiers. When present, it is split into an array and passed into searchWorkshops as part of filters. The filter UI reads this array to set selected category controls.
  - format: A single format identifier string. When present, it is passed into searchWorkshops filters and used to set the format selection UI.
  - durationTypes: A comma-separated string of duration identifiers. When present, it is split into an array and passed into searchWorkshops filters and reflected in duration filter controls.
  - sort: A sort identifier string, obtained from getWorkshopFilterOptions.sortOptions. When present, it is passed to searchWorkshops. If missing or invalid, the controller may choose a default from the available sortOptions and can update the URL to reflect it.
  - page: A numeric page index. When missing, non-numeric, or out of range, the controller normalizes it to a valid starting page (e.g., 1) and may update the URL.

- List view state (view='list'):
  - On entry, controller calls getWorkshopFilterOptions to get valid filter and sort options.
  - The controller constructs a filters object using categories, format, and durationTypes from URL, mapping each to the appropriate structure for searchWorkshops.
  - The controller calls searchWorkshops({ filters, sort, page, pageSize }). pageSize is determined by application settings and not hardcoded on this page.
  - The response items, totalCount, page, and pageSize are bound to components (list and pagination). Filter and sort UI states are derived from the URL parameters, not from component-local defaults, so that reloading the page preserves the same state.
  - Any interaction in the filters, sort, or pagination updates the URL parameters first, then triggers a re-evaluation of state and a new call to searchWorkshops.

- Detail view state (view='detail'):
  - On entry, the controller validates workshopId and calls getWorkshopDetails(workshopId).
  - The detail view binds to the workshop object and supplemental details (agenda, expectedOutcomes).
  - The existing filters/sort/page parameters from the URL are preserved while in detail view, so they can be reused when generating the 'Back to Workshops' URL.
  - The 'Back to Workshops' link sets view='list' and preserves categories, format, durationTypes, sort, and page parameters from the current URL.

- Training Plan state:
  - The Training Plan is not directly queried on this page; it is only updated via addWorkshopToTrainingPlan.
  - The page does not maintain Training Plan state in URL parameters; instead, after a successful addWorkshopToTrainingPlan call, the response (trainingPlan and items) can be used by components to display contextual confirmations or indicators.
  - Navigation to training_plan.html does not carry additional parameters from this page.

- URL updates vs. in-page state:
  - The canonical source of truth for filters, sort, and page is the URL query string.
  - Components may have transient internal state (e.g., form fields for priority and notes) that is not reflected in the URL.
  - Whenever a user action changes filter/sort/pagination, the components update the URL first; the page controller then re-derives derived state and triggers the appropriate interface calls.
  - Reloading or sharing the URL recreates the same list or detail state, since it is encoded in the query parameters.

components.functionality: Orchestrates list and detail views based on URL parameters; calls getWorkshopFilterOptions, searchWorkshops, and getWorkshopDetails; coordinates communication between filter/sort controls, list, detail, and navigation components.

Displays and manages filters for categories, formats, and duration types. Initializes options from getWorkshopFilterOptions and sets control states based on URL parameters. On change, updates URL parameters and triggers list refresh via the controller.

Displays sorting options obtained from getWorkshopFilterOptions.sortOptions and controls the sort URL parameter. When the user selects a sort option, it updates the URL (including resetting the page) and triggers a new searchWorkshops call through the controller.

Renders the set of workshops returned by searchWorkshops in list mode. Each item is represented by a workshop-card component. The list updates whenever searchWorkshops returns new data based on current filters, sort, and page.
Represents a single workshop in the list view. Displays key fields from the Workshop entity and provides interactions such as navigating to detail view and optionally saving to the Training Plan.

Displays pagination controls using totalCount, page, and pageSize from searchWorkshops. Updates the page URL parameter when the user navigates between pages and triggers searchWorkshops through the controller.

Displays full details for a single workshop using data from getWorkshopDetails. Shows workshop metadata along with agenda and expected outcomes. Includes actions to save the workshop to the Training Plan and navigate back to the list view.

Collects priority and optional notes from the user for the current workshop and submits them via addWorkshopToTrainingPlan. Handles success and error states and exposes a 'View My Training Plan' navigation link upon success.
Generates a navigation link from detail view back to list view, preserving categories, format, durationTypes, sort, and page parameters while changing view to 'list'.
Shown after at least one successful addWorkshopToTrainingPlan call on this page. Provides navigation to training_plan.html for the user to review their Training Plan.

Invokes getWorkshopFilterOptions on page load to retrieve available categories, formats, durationTypes, and sortOptions, and passes the results to the workshop-filters-bar and workshop-sort-control components.
