Listens for URL changes that affect list state (view='list', categories, format, durationTypes, sort, page), constructs a filters object, and calls searchWorkshops. Supplies the resulting items, totalCount, page, and pageSize to the workshop-list and workshop-list-pagination components.
When view='detail' and workshopId is present in the URL, calls getWorkshopDetails(workshopId) and passes the result to the workshop-detail-view component.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the existing full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the Workshops & Programs page uses a single, centered content column. The content flows linearly from top to bottom: page title, then a subtle training-plan link, then filter controls, then the sort control, followed by the workshop list and pagination. When a workshop is opened, the list + filters area is replaced by a single-column workshop detail view, with a back-to-list link above the detail. All components are stacked vertically, with only light horizontal alignment (e.g., some elements right-aligned within the same column) to preserve the simple scroll-down reading pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the existing full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the Workshops & Programs page uses a single, centered content column. The content flows linearly from top to bottom: page title, then a subtle training-plan link, then filter controls, then the sort control, followed by the workshop list and pagination. When a workshop is opened, the list + filters area is replaced by a single-column workshop detail view, with a back-to-list link above the detail. All components are stacked vertically, with only light horizontal alignment (e.g., some elements right-aligned within the same column) to preserve the simple scroll-down reading pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header (cyan bar + nav) occupies about the top 15–20% of the viewport. The main content area beneath has generous top padding (~48–64px) before the page title, reinforcing a spacious feel. The central content column spans roughly 50–60% of the page width within a max-width container of ~1100–1200px, leaving broad left and right margins. Within this column, major sections (title, filters, list, detail view) are separated by 32–56px of vertical space, while related controls (like filters and sort) are separated by tighter spacing around 16–24px, all aligned to an 8px spacing grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. The central column is relatively narrow compared to the full viewport, and each component has ample breathing room around it. Filter controls are laid out in a light, airy bar without tight clustering. Workshop cards use generous internal padding and clear separation between cards. The detail view is structured as a vertical narrative with distinct content blocks and comfortable white space. There are no multi-column grids or dense tables; everything is single-column reading, optimized for clarity and scanning rather than maximum information per screen.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from the bright cyan header area down into the centered page title, then follows a straight vertical path through the training-plan CTA, filter bar, sort control, list of workshops, and pagination. In the detail view, the flow runs from the page title at the top to the back-to-workshops link on the left, then down into the workshop detail content and the add-to-training-plan form. Right-aligned elements (sort control, training-plan CTA, add-to-plan button) punctuate the vertical flow but do not create a complex Z-pattern; they act as subtle accents within an otherwise straightforward scroll-down experience.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the page opens into a calm, spacious main area dominated by a single, centered column of content. At the top of this column, the 'Workshops & Programs' title sits centered with plenty of white space above and below, echoing the editorial feel of the Privacy Policy page. Just below, a right-aligned text-style link invites users to view their Training Plan, acting as a light utility without overpowering the title. Further down, a clean, low-profile filter bar spans the width of the central column, followed by a compact sort control aligned to the right edge of that same column. Underneath, a vertical list of generously spaced workshop cards runs down the page, each card occupying the full column width with comfortable padding and open whitespace between items. Pagination controls sit centered at the bottom of the list, leaving ample margin before the footer area. When a user opens a workshop, this central list region is replaced by a single-column detail view that begins with a subtle back link on the left and a prominent workshop title, with the add-to-training-plan button aligned to the right within the same visual band. The detail content flows downward in well-separated sections, maintaining the same airy, reading-friendly aesthetic as the list view. Throughout, the design stays faithful to the mockup’s modern corporate style: flat, minimal, and highly focused on content, with clear vertical rhythm and no clutter.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main content region beneath the site header. It is a centered container aligned with the global max-width (approximately 1100–1200px), but the active content column inside is constrained to roughly 50–60% of the page width, centered horizontally. The top edge of this container begins around 48–64px below the bottom of the navigation bar, mirroring the generous vertical gap seen before the 'Privacy Policy' heading in the mockup. Within this controller, all page-level components (page title, training-plan CTA, filters, sort, list, detail view, pagination) are stacked in a linear vertical fashion with consistent section spacing (around 32–56px between major sections). The controller is responsible for switching the central content column between two modes: (1) List Mode – showing filters, sort control, workshop list, and pagination; and (2) Detail Mode – hiding those list-related components and instead showing the back link, workshop detail view, and add-to-training-plan form. The overall alignment and column width remain constant across modes, preserving a stable reading frame.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level container that scrolls with the page. Not fixed or sticky; it simply defines the bounds and state (list vs detail) for the inner components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits in List Mode, positioned below the page title and training-plan CTA within the central column. There is a generous gap of approximately 32–40px below the title area. The filter bar spans the full width of the content column (roughly 50–60% of the page width, centered), with internal padding of about 16px vertically and 24px horizontally. It has a light, unobtrusive appearance: either a subtle outline or very faint background tint, consistent with the mockup’s minimal styling. Within this bar, individual filter controls (e.g., dropdowns, search input, tags) are aligned left and spaced horizontally on an 8px or 16px grid; if there are too many, they wrap to a second row but still stay within this central width. The top of the bar aligns visually with the left edge of the column, and the right edge is flush with the column’s right boundary, forming a clean, rectangular band. Vertically, the space between the bottom of the filter bar and the sort control below is modest, around 16–24px, keeping the filters and sort functionally grouped while preserving the airy layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block element within the column. It scrolls normally and does not stick to the top; when scrolled past, it leaves the viewport like any other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control appears immediately below the filter bar in List Mode, sharing the same central column. It is right-aligned against the right edge of the column, leaving open space to its left. The vertical gap from the filter bar is about 16–24px, indicating they belong to the same functional group but are distinct elements. The control itself is compact, typically a short 'Sort by' label with a dropdown or segmented control, occupying roughly 20–30% of the column width at most. There is generous whitespace above and below, with about 24–32px before the first workshop card, ensuring the sort UI doesn’t visually merge into the list content. On narrower viewports, the right-aligned sort control may center itself if needed, but it still sits beneath the filter bar with a clear vertical separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block anchored to the right within the standard flow of the column. It scrolls with the page and does not overlap or float over other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The workshop list occupies the main body area of List Mode, positioned below the sort control with a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px. It spans the full width of the central content column (50–60% of page width, centered), forming a single vertical stack of workshop cards. The left and right edges of the list align perfectly with those of the filter bar above, reinforcing the single-column structure. There is no additional framing box for the list; instead, the list is defined by the sequence of cards with consistent vertical spacing (typically 24–32px between cards). The top of the first card is comfortably separated from the sort control, preserving the reading rhythm, and the bottom of the last card sits 32–40px above the pagination controls. The list extends downward as long as there are items, with the airy layout making even long lists feel readable and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard vertical list that grows in height with content and scrolls normally. There is no infinite scroll or sticky behavior implied; pagination is handled explicitly at the bottom.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each workshop card is a full-width item within the workshop list, aligning exactly with the central column’s left and right edges. The card height is flexible, driven by its content, but internal padding is generous: roughly 24px on all sides, with slightly more vertical padding (24–32px) if the content is text-heavy. The card background is white (matching the page background) with either a subtle bottom divider line or a very light border to delineate cards without heavy boxes or shadows, staying true to the flat design aesthetic. Inside the card, the workshop title sits near the top left, bold and slightly larger, followed by key metadata (duration, format, category) arranged beneath it with clear vertical spacing (8–16px). Any primary action such as 'View Details' is positioned toward the bottom right of the card as a text-based button or lightly styled button, while secondary info (short description) flows in the body area, left-aligned. Cards are separated by 24–32px of vertical whitespace, giving each workshop its own breathing room in the column. On hover (desktop), any emphasis remains subtle (e.g., a slight border color change) to maintain the minimal design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list items that sit in the normal document flow. They adapt to varying content length but remain constrained to the column width. No fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls are placed directly below the workshop list, separated from the last card by about 32–40px of vertical space. They are horizontally centered within the central column, with the pagination component itself occupying only the necessary width for page numbers and navigation arrows—typically around 20–30% of the column width. There is ample whitespace above and below the controls, and the bottom of the pagination area leaves a further 48–64px margin before any footer content begins, preserving the airy feel. The styling is light and understated: page numbers are evenly spaced with clear clickable targets, but the control avoids heavy bars or dense clusters. This keeps the user’s attention on the workshop list content while still making pagination easy to find.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element at the bottom of the list. It scrolls with the content, appearing at the end of the page section; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In Detail Mode, the workshop detail view replaces the area where the filter bar, sort control, list, and pagination normally appear. It sits in the same central column, starting at the same vertical position where the list would have begun—beneath the page title, training-plan CTA, and back-to-workshops link. The detail view spans the full width of the column (50–60% of page width, centered) and is organized into vertically stacked sections: a header band, followed by content sections such as overview, learning objectives, agenda, and audience. The header band occupies the top portion of the detail view: the workshop title is left-aligned and placed near the top left, with the add-to-training-plan form aligned to the right on the same horizontal band when screen width allows. There is generous vertical padding in this header (24–32px) to clearly separate it from the back link above and the content sections below. Subsequent sections follow a simple one-column layout, with section headings left-aligned and separated by 24–40px of vertical space. Text blocks are comfortable in line length due to the column width, echoing the editorial feel of the mockup. The bottom of the detail view leaves a clear margin (48–64px) before the footer area, ensuring the page ends in a calm, uncluttered way.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard block section that scrolls normally, replacing the list content region when active. It does not overlay the list; instead it occupies the same spatial footprint in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-training-plan form appears within the workshop detail view’s header area. On desktop-width screens, it is aligned to the right side of the central column, horizontally aligned with the workshop title on the left. The form occupies roughly 25–35% of the column width, leaving ample space between it and the title; both elements share the same vertical band, which has 24–32px padding above and below. The primary control—a 'Add to Training Plan' button—is visually clear but not overly loud: medium-sized, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, respecting the 8px spacing grid. If additional controls (like a dropdown for selecting a training plan) are included, they stack vertically below the main button with 8–16px gaps, still within this right-hand block. On smaller screens, the form drops beneath the workshop title and becomes full width within the column, with 16–24px of vertical space between the title and the form. In all cases, the form remains tucked into the upper part of the detail view, making it easy to find without disrupting the vertical reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block aligned to the right within the detail header area. It participates in normal document flow and reflows beneath the title on narrow viewports; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-workshops link appears only in Detail Mode, positioned just above the workshop detail view within the central column. It is left-aligned against the column’s left edge, approximately 16–24px below the already-centered page title area. The link is presented as a subtle text link with a left-pointing arrow icon or chevron, visually small but clearly identifiable as navigation. It occupies minimal vertical space—essentially a single line of text—with about 16–24px of whitespace separating it from the top of the detail header (where the workshop title and add-to-plan form sit). This placement allows the user’s eye to move naturally from the page title at the very top, down to this back link on the left, and then into the main detail content. The link does not compete visually with the workshop title; it functions more like a breadcrumb element in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline text link within the column. It scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed when the user scrolls away from the top of the detail view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-plan CTA is a light, page-level link that appears in both List and Detail Modes, positioned beneath the main 'Workshops & Programs' page title. The title itself is centered in the column with ample spacing above and below, and the training-plan CTA sits approximately 16–24px below it, aligned to the right edge of the central column. The CTA is styled as a text link or a small text-button—possibly with a subtle icon (e.g., a folder or list) to hint at the Training Plan—keeping it visually secondary to the page title and main content. The CTA occupies only as much width as necessary for its label, leaving open space to its left. Below the CTA, there is a generous gap of about 32–40px before the next section (either the filter bar in List Mode or the back link/detail content in Detail Mode). This placement makes the Training Plan accessible without introducing a competing focal point at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block aligned to the right within the main column. It scrolls normally with the content and does not stick to the top or bottom of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-options loader appears in the exact space occupied by the workshop-filters-bar when filter options are being fetched or refreshed. It spans the same full width of the central column and the same approximate height as the filter bar, aligning with the column’s left and right edges. Instead of the actual filter controls, the loader shows lightweight placeholders—such as gray pill-shaped skeletons or a centered linear loading indicator—arranged horizontally where the filters would be. This keeps the layout stable: the vertical spacing above (to the training-plan CTA) and below (to the sort control) remains unchanged, preventing any jumping as data loads. The loader’s visual weight is intentionally subtle, using muted tones and minimal animation to signal loading without drawing excessive attention away from the primary content areas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the filter bar within the normal flow. It does not overlay the rest of the page; it simply occupies the filters bar’s slot until data is ready.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The workshop-search loader occupies the workshop list area whenever a search or filter action is actively retrieving results. It appears directly beneath the sort control, within the same central column, aligning with the list’s left and right edges. The loader centers its visual indicator—such as a spinner or a subtle animated bar—both horizontally and vertically within a fixed-height placeholder box (approximately the height of 1–2 workshop cards, with 40–64px of vertical padding). This ensures ample whitespace around the loader, maintaining the calm aesthetic even during loading. The vertical spacing to the sort control above and the pagination area below remains the same as when the list is visible, so the page layout doesn’t shift dramatically between states. Once results load, the loader disappears and the workshop cards occupy this same region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block that temporarily replaces the list content region in the normal scroll flow. It does not overlay other sections and scrolls away like any other content when the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The workshop-detail loader appears in Detail Mode within the same central column area reserved for the workshop-detail-view. It is positioned beneath the page title, training-plan CTA, and back-to-workshops link, aligning with the column’s left and right edges. The loader occupies a vertically generous block, roughly equivalent to the combined height of the detail header plus one or two content sections (e.g., 300–500px), with the loading indicator centered both horizontally and vertically. Around the indicator, there is ample empty space, preserving the low-density feel of the page. The distance from the back link above and the footer below is consistent with the eventual detail content, so the layout feels stable while loading. Once the detail data is available, the loader is replaced completely by the workshop-detail-view, without changing the column’s width or alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block that occupies the detail view’s slot in the normal document flow. It scrolls normally and does not overlay or fix itself relative to the viewport.

name: My Training Plan
title: My Training Plan
description: View and manage your saved training plan of workshops, adjust item priorities and notes, remove items, navigate to workshop details, and export the plan for internal review.

core_features: Load and display the current TrainingPlan with all associated TrainingPlanItems and their linked Workshop details using getTrainingPlan.
Visually highlight a specific workshop row in the plan when the highlightWorkshopId URL parameter is present and matches an item’s workshop_id.
Show a structured list of training plan items including workshop title, key workshop attributes, item priority, item notes, and added date.
Allow users to edit the priority and notes for each TrainingPlanItem and persist those changes using updateTrainingPlanItem.
Allow users to remove a workshop from the plan (TrainingPlanItem) with a user confirmation step, using removeTrainingPlanItem.
Provide navigation from each workshop title in the plan to the workshop detail view on workshops.html with URL parameters view=detail and workshopId set to the selected workshop’s id.

Offer export actions that call exportTrainingPlan with a user-selected format and then present or download the returned content according to its contentType (e.g., printable view, downloadable file, or content to copy into an email).
Handle empty-plan scenarios by showing a functional message and a navigation link to workshops.html for adding workshops.
user_workflows: View current training plan: 1) User navigates to training_plan.html (optionally with ?highlightWorkshopId=<id>). 2) Page initializes and calls getTrainingPlan with no parameters. 3) When the response returns, the TrainingPlan and its items (with Workshop details) populate the page header and the items list. 4) If highlightWorkshopId is present and matches one of the items’ workshop.id, that row is marked as highlighted.

Edit priority and notes for a workshop: 1) In the items list, user changes the priority (e.g., via a select control) and/or updates the notes text for a specific item. 2) User triggers a save action for that item (e.g., clicks a Save button on the row). 3) The page calls updateTrainingPlanItem with trainingPlanItemId from the row, and the current values of priority and notes. 4) On success, the component replaces the in-memory TrainingPlan and items with the objects returned from updateTrainingPlanItem (ensuring the UI reflects any server-side logic) and clears any local “pending changes” state for that row.

Remove a workshop from the plan: 1) In the items list, user clicks a Remove control for a given item. 2) A confirmation interaction appears; if user confirms, the page calls removeTrainingPlanItem with that row’s trainingPlanItemId. 3) On success, the component replaces the TrainingPlan and items with the updated objects returned by removeTrainingPlanItem. 4) The removed item’s row disappears; if that item’s workshop_id matched highlightWorkshopId, any highlight state is cleared.

Export the training plan: 1) User opens the export controls on the page. 2) User selects a desired export format (e.g., via a dropdown or radio selection bound to a format value). 3) User activates an Export action, which calls exportTrainingPlan with the selected format parameter. 4) The page receives an object containing content and contentType. 5) Based on contentType, the page either: a) generates a file download, b) opens a new browser tab or window showing the content for printing, or c) displays the content in a modal or textarea for copying into an email client.

Navigate to workshop details: 1) In the items list, user clicks on a workshop title. 2) The page performs a navigation to workshops.html with URL parameters view=detail and workshopId set to that workshop’s id. 3) The user views full workshop information and can perform actions there (such as saving the workshop back to the training plan, which may in turn link back with highlightWorkshopId).
interactions: On page load, call getTrainingPlan, then render the TrainingPlan header and items list; if there are no items, show the empty-state component instead of the list.
On detecting a highlightWorkshopId URL parameter, pass this value into the items list so that the matching workshop row is rendered in a highlighted state.
On changing an item’s priority control, update local state for that item’s pending priority but do not call updateTrainingPlanItem until the user explicitly saves.
On editing an item’s notes field, update local state for that item’s pending notes but do not call updateTrainingPlanItem until the user explicitly saves.

On clicking the Save/Update control for an item row, call updateTrainingPlanItem with the item’s id, the current pending priority, and the current pending notes; handle loading/error feedback and then refresh visible data using the API response.

On clicking the Remove/Delete control for an item row and confirming, call removeTrainingPlanItem with the item’s id; remove the row from the list using the API response data and update any counts shown in the header.
On clicking a workshop title within a row, navigate to workshops.html with view=detail&workshopId=<that_workshop_id>.

On interacting with export controls (changing format), update a local export format state; on clicking Export, call exportTrainingPlan with that format and then handle the returned content based on the contentType field.
On encountering errors from any interface, present functional error feedback (e.g., inline message near the affected component) and allow the user to retry the operation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary cross-page state is passed via URL parameters. The page reads highlightWorkshopId from the query string on load. If highlightWorkshopId is present, it is stored in in-memory component state and passed to the items list component, which compares it against each item's workshop.id to decide whether to render a highlight state for that row. This parameter is treated as read-only from external pages (typically workshops.html), and the training_plan.html page does not rely on localStorage or other persistent client mechanisms. All TrainingPlan and TrainingPlanItem data comes from getTrainingPlan on load and from the responses of updateTrainingPlanItem and removeTrainingPlanItem after modifications. After each successful update or removal, the full TrainingPlan and items returned by the API replace the current in-memory state, ensuring the UI stays in sync with server-side data. Export behavior uses the user-selected format stored in local component state and passes it as the format parameter to exportTrainingPlan; the returned content and contentType are used immediately for the user’s export action and are not persisted between sessions.

components.functionality: Initialize and maintain the current TrainingPlan and associated items list by calling getTrainingPlan on page load and refreshing state after updates or removals.
event_handlers: onLoad: Triggered when training_plan.html is first rendered; calls getTrainingPlan with no parameters and stores the returned trainingPlan and items in page-level state.
onReloadAfterUpdate: Triggered after a successful updateTrainingPlanItem call; replaces the current TrainingPlan and TrainingPlanItem list with the trainingPlan and items returned from the interface.
onReloadAfterRemove: Triggered after a successful removeTrainingPlanItem call; replaces the current TrainingPlan and TrainingPlanItem list with the trainingPlan and items returned from the interface.
onError: Handles errors from getTrainingPlan by storing error state and enabling dependent components to show error messages and a retry option.

components.functionality: Read the highlightWorkshopId URL parameter and manage highlight state for the items list.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: On page initialization, reads highlightWorkshopId from the URL query string. Stores this value in component state and passes it as a prop to the training-plan-items-list component.
onItemsChange: When the items array changes (after updates or removals), checks if any item still has a workshop.id matching the current highlightWorkshopId; if not, clears internal highlight state.

onClearHighlight: Optional event to clear the highlight state (and, if desired, update the URL via history.replaceState without highlightWorkshopId) after the user dismisses or after a defined interaction.

components.functionality: Display high-level TrainingPlan information, such as plan name and notes, along with derived metadata like total number of items and last updated date. This is a read-only summary on this page.
event_handlers: onPlanDataChange: Triggered when TrainingPlan or items data from training-plan-data-loader changes; recalculates derived values such as item count and displays the latest updated_at value.
onErrorStateChange: Receives error state from training-plan-data-loader and, if present, switches the header into an error display mode informing the user that the plan could not be loaded.

components.functionality: Render the list of TrainingPlanItems, each enriched with its linked Workshop details, and delegate item-level editing, removing, and navigation actions to row components.
event_handlers: onReceiveData: Invoked when the TrainingPlan items and corresponding Workshop data are updated by training-plan-data-loader; re-renders the list using the latest array.
onApplyHighlight: Uses the highlightWorkshopId value from training-plan-highlight-handler to mark the matching row as highlighted.

onItemEvent: Captures item-level events (save changes, remove item, title click) bubbled up from training-plan-item-row components and routes them to the appropriate handlers (updateTrainingPlanItem, removeTrainingPlanItem, navigation).

components.functionality: Represent a single TrainingPlanItem row with inline controls to update priority and notes, remove the item, and navigate to the associated Workshop detail page.
event_handlers: onPriorityChange: Triggered when the user changes the priority control for this item; updates local pending state for priority without immediately calling updateTrainingPlanItem.
onNotesChange: Triggered when the user edits the notes field; updates local pending state for notes without immediately calling updateTrainingPlanItem.

onSaveChanges: Triggered when the user explicitly saves changes for this item; calls updateTrainingPlanItem with parameters trainingPlanItemId (from TrainingPlanItem.id), priority (from local pending state), and notes (from local pending state). On success, passes the returned trainingPlan and items back to training-plan-data-loader for state replacement.

onRemoveItem: Triggered when the user activates the Remove control and confirms; calls removeTrainingPlanItem with trainingPlanItemId for this row. On success, passes the returned trainingPlan and items back to training-plan-data-loader for state replacement.
onWorkshopTitleClick: Triggered when the user clicks the workshop title; navigates the browser to workshops.html with URL parameters view=detail and workshopId set to the Workshop.id for this row.
onHighlightRender: Receives highlightWorkshopId from training-plan-items-list; compares it to the row’s Workshop.id and applies a highlighted visual state if they match.

components.functionality: Detect when the TrainingPlan has no associated TrainingPlanItems and, in that case, display an empty-state message plus a navigational link to start adding workshops.
event_handlers: onItemsChange: Monitors updates from training-plan-data-loader; if the items array is empty, hides the training-plan-items-list and shows the empty-state content instead.
onNavigateToWorkshops: Triggered when the user clicks a control in the empty state; navigates to workshops.html (without relying on localStorage), allowing the user to browse and add workshops.

components.functionality: Allow the user to choose an export format for the current TrainingPlan and trigger exportTrainingPlan; manage loading and error feedback related to export.
event_handlers: onFormatChange: Updates local form state with the user-selected export format value, which will be used as the format parameter in the exportTrainingPlan call.
onExportSubmit: Triggered when the user activates the Export action; calls exportTrainingPlan with the current format value from local form state and sets a loading state while awaiting a response.

onExportSuccess: Receives the object returned by exportTrainingPlan (containing content and contentType) and forwards it to the training-plan-export-result-viewer component for handling; clears any export error state and loading state.
onExportError: Handles errors from exportTrainingPlan by updating error state so that the component can display functional error feedback and allow the user to retry.

components.functionality: Interpret the content and contentType returned by exportTrainingPlan and perform the appropriate client-side action (download, open in new window/tab, display inline for copying, or trigger print).
event_handlers: onReceiveExportResult: Triggered when training-plan-export-controls provides a successful export result; inspects contentType and executes the appropriate rendering logic for the content.
onDownloadRequested: For content types that imply a downloadable file (e.g., application/pdf), constructs a Blob or equivalent and triggers a browser download using the provided content.
onOpenInNewWindowRequested: For content types suited to direct viewing (e.g., text/html), opens a new browser tab or window and writes the content into that document for printing or review.
onShowInlineRequested: For content types such as text/plain, displays the content within a modal or dedicated inline area so the user can copy it into an internal email or document.
onCloseExportView: Allows the user to close any inline export display (modal or inline area) and returns the UI to its normal state.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: All page-specific components sit within a single, centered content column beneath the existing global header (cyan utility bar + logo + nav). The column follows a linear, top-to-bottom sequence: a header/summary for the training plan, then the list of items or an empty-state message, followed by export-related controls and finally the export result viewer. Functional elements remain stacked in one column rather than split into sidebars, mirroring the mockup’s simple editorial layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All page-specific components sit within a single, centered content column beneath the existing global header (cyan utility bar + logo + nav). The column follows a linear, top-to-bottom sequence: a header/summary for the training plan, then the list of items or an empty-state message, followed by export-related controls and finally the export result viewer. Functional elements remain stacked in one column rather than split into sidebars, mirroring the mockup’s simple editorial layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column occupies roughly 50–60% of the overall page width, centered within the ~1100–1200px site container, leaving generous left and right margins for whitespace. Vertical spacing between major blocks (header → list → export controls → export viewer) is in the 32–64px range, with tighter 16–24px internal padding within individual components. The header + cyan bar remain about 15–20% of the viewport height; the rest is a spacious scrollable content area.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low, with a focus on clarity and ease of scanning. Each component presents only essential information without crowded tables or multi-column layouts. List rows are comfortably tall with ample padding, and export controls are presented as a small, focused horizontal group rather than a dense form, preserving the minimal, corporate feel of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the global header down into a bold, prominent 'My Training Plan' header area, then naturally continues vertically through the list of training items. Once the list is understood, attention flows downward to export controls and then to the export result viewer. There is no competing lateral navigation; visual weight is controlled by typography, spacing, and alignment to guide a straightforward scroll-down reading and interaction pattern.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the page transitions into a calm, centered content area. A single, moderately narrow column (about 50–60% of the page width) is centered on the screen, framed by generous white margins on both sides. At the top of this column, the 'My Training Plan' header panel introduces the page with a clear title and a brief summary, breathing in ample vertical space around it. Below, the training plan items occupy the middle of the screen in a clean, single-column list: each row spans the width of this central column with comfortable padding and clear alignment for titles, priorities, and quick actions. If no items exist, a centered, airy empty-state message replaces the list in the same area. Further down, the export controls are grouped together in a single horizontal control strip aligned with the column, followed by a large, spacious export result viewer that opens up the bottom portion of the layout. Throughout, the design maintains a minimal, editorial look—no sidebars, no dense grids—encouraging the user to scroll down through a simple, vertical sequence of well-separated sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader behaves as an unobtrusive overlay within the central content column. When active, it appears centered both horizontally and vertically relative to the visible portion of the main column, on top of a subtle, semi-transparent white veil that dims the training plan header, list, and export sections beneath. The loader graphic (e.g., spinner plus a short status line) occupies a compact area roughly 20–30% of the column width, leaving ample whitespace around it to preserve the airy, minimal aesthetic. When not loading, this component is visually absent and does not occupy any space in the normal flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays other components within the main content column; positioned centrally and fixed relative to the column while active, otherwise non-rendered and not part of the layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight handler does not occupy its own visual block; instead, it subtly affects the styling of individual training plan item rows within the list. When an item is highlighted (for example, based on recent interaction or priority changes), its row receives a gentle visual emphasis—such as a thin cyan left border and a slightly tinted background—while preserving the flat design and ample row padding. This emphasis is contained entirely within the width and height of the existing item row, without adding extra margins or disrupting the consistent vertical rhythm of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller that modifies appearance of list items inline; no independent spatial footprint, no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training plan header sits at the very top of the page-specific content area, directly below the site’s global header with a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to create a clear separation. It spans the full width of the central content column (roughly 50–60% of the page width, centered). The main page title 'My Training Plan' appears near the top of this block, left-aligned within the column, set in a bold, larger type size similar to the 'Privacy Policy' heading in the mockup. Any supporting summary text (e.g., number of workshops, next steps) sits beneath the title in one or two short lines, maintaining a low-density feel. The entire header block has about 24–32px of vertical padding top and bottom to establish presence without feeling heavy, and is followed by 32–48px of whitespace before the list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level element in the normal document flow; does not stick or float on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The items list is positioned directly below the training plan header, separated by approximately 32–48px of vertical whitespace. It occupies the same centered column width as the header, aligning all item rows flush with the left edge of the title above. The list reads as a vertical stack of rows, each row spanning nearly the full width of the column, with consistent vertical spacing between rows based on a 16–24px gap. There are no side gutters or nested columns inside the main page layout; any layout within the rows themselves is handled internally but remains horizontally aligned and flat. The top and bottom of the list have enough breathing room (especially below the final row, 32–40px) to visually separate it from the export controls that follow. When content is long, the list extends downward, and the user scrolls naturally, maintaining the single-column reading pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertical stack of rows within the main content column; grows in height with the number of items and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each item row appears as a horizontally oriented card-like strip within the central list, stretching nearly edge to edge inside the main column (with 16–24px internal padding on all sides). The workshop title is left-aligned at the start of the row, given typographic priority while remaining consistent with the clean corporate style. To the right of the title, inline controls such as priority selectors, notes icons, and a link to workshop details are aligned in a single horizontal line, maintaining a flat appearance without heavy borders or shadows. A remove/delete control is positioned towards the far right of the row but still inside the column width, aligned vertically with other controls. Row height is comfortably tall—roughly 56–72px minimum—to maintain low visual density and easy tap targets. Rows are separated by a small vertical gap (16–20px) so they feel distinct yet still part of a continuous list. Any highlight states applied by the highlight handler remain subtle and do not alter the row’s size, only its background/border treatment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline child of the list; fully participates in the vertical stacking and scrolls with the page. No sticky behavior; width is constrained to the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state block occupies the same position and width as the items list within the central column, appearing directly below the training plan header with a 32–48px gap. When there are no items, it replaces the list entirely in the vertical flow. The message is centered horizontally within the main column and vertically padded generously (40–64px top and bottom) to create a light, spacious panel. A concise headline ('No workshops in your training plan yet') is left-aligned within the column to remain consistent with the page’s text alignment, with an optional short explanatory line and a call-to-action link or button placed below it. The surrounding whitespace emphasizes the emptiness rather than filling the area with graphics, aligning with the minimal, editorial tone of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in place of the list; static block element that sits in the normal document flow with no fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The export controls are positioned below the items list (or empty state), separated by approximately 40–56px of vertical whitespace to clearly mark a new functional section. They span the same central column width and are left-aligned with the content above. The primary export button (e.g., 'Export Plan') sits towards the left side of this block, with any supporting options (format dropdowns, include-notes toggle) arranged in a single horizontal row to its right or directly below it, depending on available width, but always maintaining a clean, low-density layout. Internal padding within this control group remains moderate (16–24px vertically) so the section feels substantial but not crowded. There is an additional 24–40px of whitespace below the controls before the export result viewer begins, preserving the vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the normal flow, aligned to the main column; no sticky or floating behavior. On very narrow screens, controls wrap but remain within the single-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The export result viewer sits directly beneath the export controls, separated by about 24–40px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the central content column and presents as a large, flat panel with a light border or subtle background tint to differentiate it from the page background while keeping the overall design minimal. Inside, the exported content preview (text or structured layout) is arranged in a single column with generous internal padding of 24–32px on all sides, allowing the content to breathe and remain easy to read. The viewer’s height grows with the amount of exported information, extending down the page while maintaining large margins to the left and right. The bottom of this component has extra padding (32–48px) to create a comfortable visual endpoint for the page content before the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky block at the bottom of the main content area; its height is flexible and it scrolls with the rest of the page.

name: Booking
title: Book a Session
description: Page for selecting a consulting or coaching service, choosing an available time slot, entering contact details, and submitting a booking using dynamic availability and URL-driven state.

core_features: Load and display active booking services in a selectable control using getBookingServices, with optional preselection from the serviceId URL parameter.
For the selected service, load available session durations via getServiceDurations and let the user choose a duration in minutes.
Provide a week-based calendar navigator controlled by the weekStartDate URL parameter and load availability for that week using getAvailabilitySlots.
Filter visible availability slots by time_of_day (morning, afternoon, evening) using the timeOfDay URL parameter and getAvailabilitySlots.
Display bookable time slots for the chosen service and week, allow the user to select a slot, and reflect the selection in the slotId URL parameter.
Collect user details (full name, email, company/organization) in a form that is required to submit a booking.
Create a booking by calling createBooking with the selected service, slot, duration, and client details, then display a confirmation state using returned Booking data.
Maintain deep-linkable and back/forward-friendly state for service, week, time-of-day filter, and selected slot entirely via URL parameters (no local storage).

user_workflows.name: Workflow 1: Standard booking from navigation (no preselected service)
steps: User opens booking.html without URL parameters.
On page load, the system reads URL parameters (none in this case) and calls getBookingServices.
User opens the service selector and chooses a service. The serviceId URL parameter is set or updated, and dependent state (slotId, optionally timeOfDay and weekStartDate) is reset if needed.

After service selection, the system calls getServiceDurations(serviceId) to load duration options and calls getAvailabilitySlots(serviceId, effectiveWeekStartDate, effectiveTimeOfDay) to load slots for the current week.
User selects a preferred duration from the loaded options.

User navigates through weeks using the calendar controls if needed; each navigation updates weekStartDate in the URL and triggers getAvailabilitySlots(serviceId, newWeekStartDate, effectiveTimeOfDay).
User optionally applies a time-of-day filter (morning/afternoon/evening). The timeOfDay URL parameter is updated and getAvailabilitySlots(serviceId, weekStartDate, newTimeOfDay) is called.
User clicks on an available slot in the slot list. The selected slotId is stored in state and reflected in the slotId URL parameter.
User fills in full name, email, and optionally company in the client details form.

User submits the booking form. The page validates that serviceId, slotId, durationMinutes, fullName, and email are present, then calls createBooking(serviceId, slotId, durationMinutes, fullName, email, company).

If createBooking returns success=true, the page displays the confirmation component populated from the returned booking object and disables or hides the booking form. If success=false, an error message from the response is displayed and the form remains editable.

user_workflows.name: Workflow 2: Booking from CTA with preselected service
steps: User clicks a CTA on index.html or consultants.html that links to booking.html with a serviceId query parameter (and optionally weekStartDate and timeOfDay).
On page load, the URL-state controller reads serviceId, weekStartDate, and timeOfDay from the URL.

The page calls getBookingServices and, once loaded, auto-selects the service that matches serviceId if it exists and is active. If no matching service is found, the selector remains unselected and the invalid serviceId remains or is removed according to implementation.

After service selection, the page immediately calls getServiceDurations(serviceId) and getAvailabilitySlots(serviceId, weekStartDate or defaultWeekStartDate, timeOfDay or no filter) and displays the results.
User adjusts week and/or time-of-day filter if desired, selecting a slot and filling in client details as in Workflow 1.
User submits the booking form, triggering createBooking and subsequent confirmation or error states as in Workflow 1.

user_workflows.name: Workflow 3: Deep link to a specific week/time-of-day and slot
steps: User follows a link to booking.html that includes serviceId, weekStartDate, timeOfDay, and slotId.
On page load, URL parameters are parsed and the serviceId is used to initialise the service selection.
The page calls getBookingServices and, when services are loaded, auto-selects the service matching serviceId.
The page then calls getServiceDurations(serviceId) and getAvailabilitySlots(serviceId, weekStartDate, timeOfDay).

When slots are loaded, the system checks whether any returned slot has an id equal to slotId. If found and not booked, that slot is selected and highlighted. If not found or not available, the slotId parameter may be cleared from the URL and no slot is preselected.
User reviews the preselected slot, optionally changes week/time-of-day/slot, fills in client details, and submits the booking using createBooking as in the previous workflows.

interactions: Service selection change: When the user changes the selected service, the page updates the serviceId URL parameter, clears any existing slot selection (slotId), may reset weekStartDate and timeOfDay depending on design, then calls getServiceDurations and getAvailabilitySlots for the new service.

Week navigation: When the user clicks next/previous week or selects a week in the calendar, the weekStartDate URL parameter is updated and getAvailabilitySlots is called again with the current serviceId and timeOfDay.

Time-of-day filter change: When the user toggles or selects a time-of-day filter, the timeOfDay URL parameter is set or cleared. The page then reloads availability via getAvailabilitySlots(serviceId, weekStartDate, timeOfDay).
Slot selection: Clicking an available slot sets the selected slot in component state and updates the slotId URL parameter, which allows the selection to persist on reload or when sharing the link.
Client details input: Typing into full name, email, and company fields updates internal form state; the form is validated on blur and on submit, but no API calls are made until submission.

Booking form submission: Submitting the form triggers validation; if valid, createBooking is called with serviceId, slotId, durationMinutes, fullName, email, and company. UI shows a pending state during the API call, then shows success or error based on the response.

URL back/forward navigation: When the browser back or forward buttons are used, a popstate handler re-reads URL parameters and re-syncs service selection, week, time-of-day filter, and slot selection, reissuing getServiceDurations/getAvailabilitySlots as necessary.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s key state (selected service, displayed week, time-of-day filter, and selected slot) is derived from and persisted to URL parameters, ensuring deep linking and back/forward navigation compatibility. On initial load, the page reads serviceId, weekStartDate, timeOfDay, and slotId from the query string. If serviceId is present and matches an active service from getBookingServices, that service is preselected; otherwise the user must choose a service. The effective weekStartDate is taken from the URL if valid, otherwise a default week start based on the current date is used; when the user navigates weeks, weekStartDate is updated in the URL. The timeOfDay filter is taken from the URL if it matches allowed values; choosing or clearing this filter updates timeOfDay in the URL and triggers getAvailabilitySlots. When the user selects a slot, slotId is written to the URL so that reloading or sharing the link preserves the selection. Preselection of a slot from URL parameters occurs only if serviceId and weekStartDate are present and getAvailabilitySlots returns a slot with the given slotId. No booking data or user details are stored in localStorage; all persisted navigational state is handled via URL parameters and server-side data via the defined interfaces.

components.functionality: Parses and manages URL query parameters (serviceId, weekStartDate, timeOfDay, slotId), initializes page state from them on load, and updates them on relevant user interactions. Listens to browser history changes to keep UI state in sync with URL.

Fetches the list of active booking services using getBookingServices and renders them in a dropdown or similar control. Preselects a service if the serviceId URL parameter matches a returned service. Notifies other components when the selected service changes.

Once a service is selected, calls getServiceDurations(serviceId) to load available session durations. Displays them as selectable options (e.g., radio buttons or dropdown). Maintains the currently selected durationMinutes in its internal state and exposes it to the booking form and summary.

Displays filter options corresponding to the time_of_day values supported by AvailabilitySlot (such as morning, afternoon, evening). Initializes its selected value from the timeOfDay URL parameter if present. When the user changes the filter, it updates the timeOfDay URL parameter and triggers a reload of availability via getAvailabilitySlots.

Controls the currently displayed week of availability. Initializes the week start from the weekStartDate URL parameter or from the current date if not set. Provides next/previous week navigation and optionally direct week selection. Each change updates the weekStartDate URL parameter and causes availability to be re-fetched.

Uses the selected serviceId, current weekStartDate, and timeOfDay (from state/URL) to call getAvailabilitySlots. Displays the returned slots grouped by date and time. Filters out any slots that are already booked if necessary. Highlights the slot whose id matches slotId URL parameter or current selection. On slot click, updates the selected slot state and slotId URL parameter.

Collects user contact information required to create a booking, including full name, email, and optional company or organization. Validates required fields and email format. Exposes the collected values to the booking submission logic.

Displays a live summary of the pending booking before submission, including selected service name (from BookingService), chosen durationMinutes (from duration-selector), and selected slot date/time/timezone (from AvailabilitySlot). Updates whenever the service, duration, or slot changes.

Coordinates submission of the booking. On submit, reads selected serviceId, slotId, durationMinutes, and client details. Validates that all required values are present. Calls createBooking(serviceId, slotId, durationMinutes, fullName, email, company). Manages loading state while waiting for the API response, then triggers success or error UI updates.

Shows the confirmation state after a successful createBooking call. Renders details from the returned Booking object, such as service_name_snapshot, duration_minutes, start_datetime, end_datetime, and client_full_name. Optionally hides or disables the booking form to prevent duplicate submissions.

Displays global feedback messages related to API operations on this page, such as errors from getBookingServices, getServiceDurations, getAvailabilitySlots, or createBooking, and optional success notices. Messages are driven purely by API responses and validation results, not by hardcoded data.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom, single-column structure as the mockup. Below the existing global header (cyan utility bar + navigation), the Booking page content is centered in the viewport as one main column. Within that column, components are stacked in a clear sequence: page title → API status (if present) → selectors and filters → week calendar → available slots → client details form → booking summary → submit control → confirmation (shown instead of selection/form after success). Any horizontal groupings (e.g., service and duration) stay within this central column and remain visually light to preserve the simple columnar feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom, single-column structure as the mockup. Below the existing global header (cyan utility bar + navigation), the Booking page content is centered in the viewport as one main column. Within that column, components are stacked in a clear sequence: page title → API status (if present) → selectors and filters → week calendar → available slots → client details form → booking summary → submit control → confirmation (shown instead of selection/form after success). Any horizontal groupings (e.g., service and duration) stay within this central column and remain visually light to preserve the simple columnar feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a max-width content container of about 1100–1200px, with the actual booking flow constrained to a narrower, central column occupying roughly 50–60% of the page width (approx. 640–720px). On larger screens, wide side margins create focus on the booking flow. The header and cyan bar occupy the top 15–20% of the viewport; the rest is mostly white space with the booking column floating comfortably in the center. Vertical spacing between major groups (selectors vs. calendar vs. form) is generous (32–64px), with tighter but still airy spacing (16–24px) between related controls.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep content density low and uncluttered. Each logical step (choose service, pick time, enter details, confirm) is visually distinct, with sufficient white space above and below. Avoid compact multi-column forms or dense slot grids; instead, use a short list of clearly spaced interactive elements. Labels and text are crisp and minimal, and decorative elements are restrained so the layout feels calm and professional.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Mirror the strong vertical reading flow of the mockup. The eye moves from the global header down to a bold, centered 'Booking' title, then to a slim API status banner (when active), then sequentially through service selection, calendar navigation, available slots, and finally the form and summary leading to the primary submit action. The layout encourages scrolling straight down rather than scanning laterally. Key transition points (from selection to form to confirmation) are emphasized with extra spacing and subtle typographic hierarchy instead of heavy visual devices.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the full-width cyan utility bar and white navigation header, the Booking page opens with a bold, centered page title sitting in a wide field of white, echoing the spacious editorial feel of the mockup. The booking interface itself is contained in a single, centered column that occupies about 55% of the page width, with large left and right margins on desktop, making the content feel focused and uncluttered. Directly under the title, an unobtrusive status banner appears only when needed, framed by generous white space so it feels informative rather than noisy. The core interaction then flows straight down: first a pair of lightweight selectors to choose the service and duration, followed by a slim time-of-day filter and a week-based calendar strip to move between days. Immediately below, a vertically stacked list of available time slots fills the center of the page, each slot isolated with ample line spacing for easy tapping. After a clear vertical break, the user encounters a simple, single-column client details form, and beneath that a compact summary panel that recaps their choices before a centered, primary booking button. Upon successful submission, the central area below the header is visually simplified again, replaced by a confirmation panel that occupies the same central column, giving the impression of a clean, linear journey from top to bottom, entirely in keeping with the minimal, corporate, and calm aesthetic of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual, structural component that sits logically at the top of the Booking page content hierarchy, just inside the main booking column container. It does not occupy visible space or change the visual layout directly; instead, it governs how the service, date, time, and other selection components initialize and react to URL parameters. Visually, the space where it resides appears as normal whitespace above the first interactive selectors, keeping the interface clean and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered in the UI; behaves as an invisible controller wrapping or coordinating visible booking components without affecting spacing or flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service selector is the first visible interactive element in the booking flow. It appears within the central booking column, directly below the page title (and any active API status banner), separated from the title by approximately 40–56px of vertical whitespace. On desktop, it spans nearly the full width of the booking column (about 90–100% of that column, which itself is ~55% of the page width), with its label left-aligned and the control (dropdown or segmented control) occupying a single clean line. The component is visually anchored to the center of the page through the column alignment, but its text is left-aligned to maintain a professional, form-like feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column, participating in normal vertical document flow. On narrower screens, it expands to 100% of available width with consistent side padding tied to the 8px spacing system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The duration selector is closely associated with the service selector and appears immediately below or alongside it. On medium to large screens, it sits on the same horizontal row to the right of the service selector within the central column: the service selector takes roughly 60% of the column width and the duration selector about 40%, with a slim 16–24px horizontal gap between them, all still centered as a group within the main content column. This creates a light, two-control band without breaking the single-column feel, as the duo still reads as one horizontal unit in the center. On small screens, the duration selector stacks directly beneath the service selector with about 16–24px vertical spacing, both still left-aligned within the column. It maintains the same clean, minimal look with a single-line label and control.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive within the central column: pairs horizontally with the service selector when space permits, otherwise stacks vertically, always remaining in the normal flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-of-day filter sits directly beneath the service/duration selection band, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 24–32px to indicate a related but distinct refinement step. It occupies the full width of the booking column but presents as a slim horizontal row of small pills, buttons, or toggles (e.g., Morning, Afternoon, Evening) centered within the column while keeping text left-aligned where appropriate. There is generous padding above and below the filter row (8–16px) so it doesn’t feel cramped. The overall effect is a light, unobtrusive control row that visually bridges the selectors above and the calendar below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline with the vertical flow; it reflows so pills wrap to multiple lines on narrow screens while still remaining within the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The week calendar navigator is positioned centrally below the time-of-day filter, with a clear vertical separation of about 32–40px to mark the transition into date selection. It spans the full width of the booking column, presenting a horizontally oriented strip of days (e.g., Mon–Sun) with previous/next week controls at the far left and right edges of this strip. The calendar strip is visually light—no heavy borders or shadows—using thin lines and subtle accent colors aligned with the site’s palette. It sits roughly in the middle of the page’s vertical space once the user has scrolled a bit, reinforcing the feeling that selecting a date is the core step in the center of the journey.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column. As the user scrolls, it moves with the content; it does not stick to the top. On small screens, the day cells may compress or scroll horizontally but remain within the same central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability slot list is directly tied to the calendar and appears immediately below it with a relatively tight vertical gap of about 16–24px, so the relationship between selected date and available times is obvious. It occupies the entire width of the booking column, presenting each time slot as a separate, clearly delineated row or pill with generous vertical padding (8–12px per slot) and spacing between slots (8–16px). The list is not overwhelmingly long on screen; if there are many slots, the component extends downwards, encouraging vertical scrolling consistent with the page’s linear reading pattern. The first few slots appear roughly in the center of the page width, reinforcing focus on choosing a time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically expanding as needed. If the list becomes very long, it scrolls with the page; there is no internal scrollbar, keeping the experience consistent with natural page scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The client details form appears after a noticeable vertical break—approximately 40–56px below the last visible availability slot—signaling a transition from choosing options to providing personal information. It is fully contained within the central booking column and uses a single-column form layout: labels are left-aligned, and input fields span nearly the full column width, maintaining a simple, linear structure. Each form field has comfortable vertical spacing (16–24px) beneath it, and section dividers within the form (e.g., contact info vs. additional notes) are indicated by extra spacing rather than heavy lines. On desktop, the form visually occupies the middle third of the viewport height once scrolled into view, framed by white space above the form and below the submit area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static single-column form within the central layout. On small screens, fields expand to 100% width with consistent horizontal padding, preserving readability and tap targets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking summary panel sits directly beneath the client details form, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space to make it feel like a distinct, confirming step before submission. It remains fully within the same central column, extending nearly its full width but appearing as a lightly framed card or block: a subtle border or faint background tint (no heavy shadows) distinguishes it from the white page while preserving the minimal aesthetic. Internally, content is left-aligned, listing the selected service, duration, date, and time, with comfortable line spacing (8–12px between summary lines). Visually, it feels like a concise recap band near the bottom of the main content, anchoring attention just above the final call-to-action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component, flowing naturally after the form fields. On smaller screens, the panel’s padding and spacing scale down slightly but remain airy to avoid crowding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking submit controller is positioned immediately beneath the booking summary panel, with a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to emphasize its importance as the final action. It consists primarily of a prominent submit button (e.g., 'Confirm Booking') centered horizontally within the booking column, possibly accompanied by a brief line of supporting text (e.g., terms or reassurance) placed just below it in a lighter style. The button width is large but not full-bleed, occupying about 50–70% of the column width on desktop while remaining centered and surrounded by generous white space above and below (16–24px) so it stands out as the primary focal point at the end of the flow. On small screens, the button can expand to nearly 100% of the column width with adequate padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, at the bottom of the booking flow prior to confirmation. It sits in the normal document flow; when clicked and submission succeeds, content below the header transitions to show the confirmation panel in the same central area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking confirmation panel occupies the same central content region that previously contained the slots list, form, and summary. After a successful submission, this panel appears beneath the global header, directly below the page title, with generous top margin of about 40–56px to give the sense of a fresh, completed state. It spans the full width of the booking column and is vertically compact but visually clear: a bold confirmation headline is centered or slightly left-aligned, with supporting text and key booking details arranged in a simple, single-column layout underneath. Ample white space surrounds the content so the panel feels calm and resolved rather than busy. The user’s eye naturally drops from the familiar header and title into this centered confirmation message, maintaining the same vertical flow as other pages while clearly indicating completion.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel that replaces or hides the selection, form, and submit components once booking is complete, occupying their space in the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API status banner is a horizontal information strip that appears within the main booking column just below the page title and above the first interactive selector. When active, it spans 100% of the booking column’s width but remains relatively low in height, with 8–16px of vertical padding and a subtle background color keyed to status (e.g., soft yellow for warnings, light red for errors). The banner’s text is left-aligned, with an optional small icon on the left to draw attention without overwhelming the layout. It is separated from the title above and the service selector below by about 16–24px of white space, so it feels like an inline notice within the flow rather than a heavy, separate section. When inactive, this space collapses entirely, restoring the continuous white space between title and selectors.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and non-sticky, appearing and disappearing within the normal flow of the central column. It does not overlay content; instead, it pushes other components down when visible.

name: Case Studies
title: Case Studies
description: Browse, filter, sort, and view detailed case studies, and save selected case studies for later review.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options for case studies using getCaseStudyFilterOptions.
Display a paginated list of case studies based on current filters, sort option, and page number using searchCaseStudies.
Allow users to filter case studies by industry, budget range, and duration bucket, with controls reflecting current URL parameters.
Allow users to sort case studies using available sort options (e.g., by date, impact, relevance) returned from getCaseStudyFilterOptions.
Show detailed information for a selected case study when a caseStudyId is provided, using getCaseStudyDetails.
Allow users to save a case study to their Saved Items list using saveCaseStudy from both list and detail views.
Maintain page state (filters, sort, page, selected caseStudyId) in URL parameters so it can be bookmarked, shared, and navigated via browser back/forward.
Provide navigation to the Saved Items page after one or more case studies have been saved.
user_workflows:
Browse and filter case studies:
1. User navigates to case_studies.html without URL parameters or with some pre-set (e.g., industry).
2. Page controller reads URL parameters: industry, minBudget, maxBudget, durationBucket, sort, page, caseStudyId.
3. Page calls getCaseStudyFilterOptions to obtain available industries, budget presets, duration buckets, and sort options.
4. Filter controls are initialized using URL parameters; if a parameter is missing, the control uses a neutral default such as no filter or the first available sort option from getCaseStudyFilterOptions.
5. Page constructs a filters object from the current filter state (including only those filters that have values) and calls searchCaseStudies with { filters, sort, page, pageSize }.
6. The returned list of CaseStudy items is displayed in the case study list, along with pagination controls based on totalCount/page/pageSize.
7. User adjusts any combination of filters (industry, budget, duration) or sort; on each change, the URL is updated to reflect new parameters and page is reset to the first page.
8. After URL update, searchCaseStudies is called again with the updated parameters, and the list refreshes.

View case study details from the list:
1. From the case study list, user clicks a case study card or a dedicated "View details" interaction.
2. The page updates the URL to include caseStudyId for the selected case study while preserving current filter, sort, and page parameters.
3. The page calls getCaseStudyDetails(caseStudyId).
4. The detailed case study content (objectives, approach, results, and other fields from CaseStudy) is displayed in a detail panel or dedicated detail section.
5. User can close the detail view (e.g., via a close button or back action); this removes caseStudyId from the URL and hides the detail view, while leaving filters and current page intact.
6. The case study list remains visible and unchanged during this interaction.

Save a case study and navigate to Saved Items:
1. From either a case study card in the list or the detail view, user clicks a "Save" interaction for a specific case study.
2. The page invokes saveCaseStudy(caseStudyId).
3. On successful response, the component updates its local representation of saved state (e.g., marking that case study as saved using the returned SavedItem) and may display a confirmation message or indicator.
4. A "View Saved Items" navigation control is available (conditioned on at least one case study having been saved in the current session or indicated via API response/state).
5. When the user selects "View Saved Items", the page navigates to saved_items.html (no additional parameters are required for this navigation according to the outgoing connections).

Open case studies page pre-filtered or deep-linked:
1. User arrives from index.html or another page with a link such as case_studies.html?industry=... or case_studies.html?caseStudyId=... plus optional other parameters.
2. On load, the page controller reads the incoming parameters and initializes filter controls and selected caseStudyId accordingly.
3. getCaseStudyFilterOptions is called to retrieve allowed values, which are then matched against the URL parameters (e.g., the passed industry value is selected in the industry filter if it exists in the returned industries list; if it does not, the filter control falls back to a neutral value and the invalid parameter is effectively ignored).
4. searchCaseStudies is called with filters and sort derived from the URL and the matched filter options.
5. If caseStudyId is present, getCaseStudyDetails is called and the detail panel is opened directly for that item.
6. User may change filters, sort, or page; the URL is updated and the listing refreshes as described in the browse workflow.

Paginate through case studies:
1. After a searchCaseStudies call returns results, pagination controls are rendered based on totalCount, page, and pageSize.
2. User selects a different page index (next, previous, or specific page number) in the pagination controls.
