component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list occupies the main central column directly below the filter controls when the page is in list state. It is centered within the same max-width container and spans approximately 60–70% of the page width (about 7–9 of 12 columns), matching or slightly exceeding the width of the search/filter block while still leaving wide margin bands on either side. Articles are presented as stacked cards in a single column, not a grid: each card extends the full width of this column, with a clear title, a short excerpt, and concise metadata. Between each card there is generous vertical spacing of about 24–32px, and there is a slightly larger gap (32–40px) between the last filter control and the first article card, reinforcing the transition from controls to content. The top of the first article card sits well below the filter bar to preserve the spacious, editorial look. On smaller screens, the list simply scales to ~100% of the container width while maintaining the one-article-per-row structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable stack of items within the main column; it expands vertically as more articles load but remains centered and single-column across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-pagination component is placed directly beneath the article-list, centered within the same main column. It occupies only as much horizontal space as its content requires but aligns visually with the article cards and search/filter block above. Pagination controls (previous/next arrows and page numbers) are arranged in a single horizontal line, with ample white space around them. There is a distinct vertical gap of about 32–48px between the last article card and the pagination row, clearly indicating the end of the list. Below the pagination, an additional 40–64px of bottom padding is preserved before the end of the content area, mirroring the generous spacing at the top and maintaining the airy feel. On mobile, the pagination controls remain centered and might span up to 70–80% of the container width while staying visually light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored at the bottom of the list view; it scrolls with the content and does not stick to the viewport, preserving the straightforward top-to-bottom reading pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-view takes over the main content column in place of the article-list when a user selects an article. It starts in the same vertical position where the list content would normally begin—just below the filter controls (or optionally with the filters hidden while in detail state, creating an even cleaner top to the article). The detail view’s reading column is slightly narrower than the list width for optimal readability, occupying about 50–60% of the page width and remaining centered, with generous side margins. At the top of this column sits the article title aligned left within the column, followed by metadata (date, category, author) in a subtle line beneath. The body text flows downward with a comfortable line length (~65–75 characters), consistent line-height, and clear spacing between paragraphs (16–24px). Major content elements such as featured images or pull quotes are aligned to this same column width, maintaining the strict single-column editorial feel. A substantial gap (about 40–56px) separates the header/navigation area from the article title, and similar gaps separate major sections within the article, continuing the low-density aesthetic. The article-detail-view may embed the newsletter-signup-inline component partway down the article while keeping it within the same column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the article-list inside the main content region; it scrolls vertically in a standard way, without sticky headers or side elements, to keep the reading experience simple and focused.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-signup-sidebar is a compact, vertically oriented card that appears alongside the article-detail-view on larger screens, but only within the boundaries of the same max-width container. On desktop (approximately ≥1024px), the main content area effectively becomes a subtle two-column layout inside the container: the primary article column on the left occupying about 65–70% of the container width, and the newsletter sidebar on the right occupying about 30–35%, with a clear gutter of roughly 32px between them. The top of the sidebar card aligns approximately with the upper portion of the article body (slightly below the title and hero area), so the article remains the first focus while the newsletter card sits at eye level as a secondary call to action. The card itself has moderate padding (16–24px) and a simple border or background to distinguish it from the page while still feeling light. On tablet and mobile (below ~1024px), the newsletter-signup-sidebar drops out of the side-by-side column and is instead placed within the main vertical flow, typically inserted after the first portion of the article content: it then expands to about 90–100% of the main column width and is centered, preserving the single-column reading pattern seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive and context-sensitive: on large screens it behaves like a secondary right-hand column within the container (but does not fix to the viewport), scrolling in parallel with the article; on smaller screens it collapses into a standard block within the main column and scrolls with the article content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-signup-inline component is presented as a horizontal card inserted directly into the central content flow, visually matching the width of the main column in whichever context it appears. In the article list view, it can appear as a full-width card within the article-list, typically after the first few article cards (for example, after the 3rd item). In that case, it occupies the same 60–70% width as the article cards and is centered, with vertical spacing on par with or slightly larger than the gaps between article cards (about 32–40px), making it noticeable but not overwhelming. In the article detail view, the inline signup appears within the text flow, usually after an introductory section or midway through a longer article. It spans the full width of the article’s reading column (about 50–60% of the page width), centered, with 32–48px of space above and below to clearly separate it from the surrounding paragraphs. The internal layout is simple: a short heading or line of copy at the top and an email input with a submit button aligned horizontally or vertically depending on screen width. On smaller screens it remains centered, scaling to nearly 100% of the container width while keeping generous padding so it still feels like a distinct, yet integrated, section of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and fully integrated into the vertical flow of content; it appears at predefined insertion points within the article list or article detail, scrolling naturally with surrounding content and adapting its width to the same column as its host context.

name: Pricing & Calculator
title: Pricing & Calculator
description: This page shows standard training and consulting pricing tiers and provides an interactive calculator to configure a custom training scenario, get an estimated total, and send that configuration to a proposal request when it fits within a specified budget.

core_features: Load and display standard training and consulting pricing tiers using getPricingOverview, including access to calculatorSettings for the custom training calculator.
Load the current CustomTrainingConfiguration and its latest estimate using getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration.

Apply optional URL parameters (deliveryFormat, numParticipants, workshopLengthDays, includeFollowupGroupCoaching, followupGroupCoachingSessions, includeOnlinePreworkModules, budgetLimit) as overrides to the current configuration via updateCustomTrainingConfiguration.

Provide a configurable form for users to select delivery format, participant count, workshop length, and add-ons (follow-up group coaching sessions, online pre-work modules), all bound to CustomTrainingConfiguration and updated via updateCustomTrainingConfiguration.
Display the calculated estimated_total, currency, and budget_limit status from CustomTrainingConfiguration, including whether the configuration is under budget (is_under_budget).
Guide users to adjust their configuration to fall under a specified budgetLimit, with dynamic feedback whenever configuration changes.

Enable navigation to proposal_request.html with URL parameters sourceType=custom_training_configuration and customTrainingConfigurationId equal to the current configuration.id, only when the configuration is under budget according to CustomTrainingConfiguration.is_under_budget.
user_workflows:
View standard pricing tiers:
1. User navigates to pricing.html.
2. On page load, the page calls getPricingOverview.
3. trainingTiers and consultingTiers returned by getPricingOverview are displayed in a structured list, showing names, descriptions, and startingFrom values using their currency.
4. calculatorSettings from getPricingOverview are stored for use by the calculator (e.g., showing base rates and informing explanations, without hardcoding values).

Load or initialize a custom training configuration:
1. On the same initial load, the page calls getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration.
2. The current CustomTrainingConfiguration and associated calculatorSettings are received.
3. The page reads URL parameters (deliveryFormat, numParticipants, workshopLengthDays, includeFollowupGroupCoaching, followupGroupCoachingSessions, includeOnlinePreworkModules, budgetLimit).
4. If any of these URL parameters are present, the page calls updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the current configuration’s values overridden by the URL parameter values.
5. The updated configuration returned from updateCustomTrainingConfiguration becomes the active configuration for the calculator and is bound to the form fields and estimate display.

Configure a new or adjusted custom training estimate:
1. User interacts with the calculator form fields:
   - Changes deliveryFormat (e.g., from on_site_at_office to in_house_training or other supported values).
   - Adjusts numParticipants.
   - Adjusts workshopLengthDays (and workshopLengthLabel if applicable, e.g., via a dropdown mapped to supported labels).
   - Toggles includeFollowupGroupCoaching and, if enabled, sets followupGroupCoachingSessions.
   - Toggles includeOnlinePreworkModules.
   - Sets or edits budgetLimit.
2. On each significant change (e.g., on blur or on change for numeric fields, on toggle for booleans), the page calls updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the new values.
3. updateCustomTrainingConfiguration returns an updated CustomTrainingConfiguration with a recalculated estimated_total, currency, is_under_budget, and last_calculated_at.
4. The estimate summary area updates to display the new estimated_total, currency, and budget status, and the form fields reflect the latest configuration values.
5. The URL query string is updated to reflect the current configuration parameters so that reloading or sharing the URL restores the same configuration.

Use budget guidance to reach a target budget:
1. User enters or edits budgetLimit in the calculator.
2. The new budgetLimit is sent in the next updateCustomTrainingConfiguration call.
3. The updated CustomTrainingConfiguration contains budget_limit and is_under_budget.
4. A budget status indicator shows whether the estimate is above or below budget based on is_under_budget.
5. If estimate is above budget, guidance text explains that the user can reduce participants, days, or remove add-ons. The user makes adjustments, which trigger updateCustomTrainingConfiguration and live feedback until is_under_budget is true.

Add configuration to Proposal Request:
1. Once CustomTrainingConfiguration.is_under_budget is true (and an estimated_total is available), the “Add this configuration to Proposal Request” button becomes enabled.
2. When the user clicks this button, the component reads configuration.id from the current CustomTrainingConfiguration.
3. The browser navigates to proposal_request.html with URL parameters:
   - sourceType=custom_training_configuration
   - customTrainingConfigurationId=<configuration.id>
4. On proposal_request.html, the system can use customTrainingConfigurationId to pre-populate the proposal request (handled there, not on this page).

Revisit the page with a preconfigured scenario:
1. User receives or bookmarks a URL to pricing.html with some or all configuration parameters in the query string (e.g., pricing.html?deliveryFormat=on_site_at_office&numParticipants=25&workshopLengthDays=2&budgetLimit=8000).
2. On load, pricing.html calls getPricingOverview and getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration.
3. The URL parameters are read and passed to updateCustomTrainingConfiguration as overrides to the current configuration.
4. The page displays the calculator already configured according to the URL parameters, and the estimate summary shows the corresponding estimated_total and budget status.
5. User can then further adjust the configuration, which updates both server-side configuration and URL parameters.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load initialization
- Trigger: Window onLoad for pricing.html.
- Actions:
  1. Call getPricingOverview.
  2. Call getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration.
  3. Read current URL query parameters relevant to the calculator.
  4. If any relevant URL parameters exist, call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration using the existing configuration values from getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration merged with URL parameter overrides.
  5. Bind the resulting configuration and calculatorSettings to the calculator form and estimate summary components.
- Result: Page displays pricing tiers and a live calculator initialized according to existing server-side configuration and/or URL parameters.

Interaction: Change delivery format
- Trigger: User selects a different delivery format in the calculator (e.g., from a select dropdown).
- Actions:
  1. Capture new deliveryFormat value.
  2. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with deliveryFormat set to the new value and all other configuration fields set to their current values.
  3. On success, update local page state with the returned CustomTrainingConfiguration.
  4. Update the URL query parameter deliveryFormat to the new value.
- Result: The estimate_total, is_under_budget, and any dependent fields update, and the URL reflects the selected delivery format.

Interaction: Change number of participants
- Trigger: User edits the numParticipants input and either blurs the field or triggers a form update action.
- Actions:
  1. Validate the input (e.g., that it is a positive number; exact rules defined server-side; client may perform basic checks without hardcoded thresholds).
  2. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the new numParticipants and current values for other fields.
  3. On success, update the configuration-bound form fields and estimate summary.
  4. Update the URL query parameter numParticipants.
- Result: The estimated_total adjusts according to participant count, and budget status updates accordingly.

Interaction: Change workshop length
- Trigger: User selects or enters workshopLengthDays and optionally a workshopLengthLabel (e.g., from a dropdown).
- Actions:
  1. Capture the new workshopLengthDays and workshopLengthLabel.
  2. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with those values plus the current values of other fields.
  3. On success, update the configuration in UI.
  4. Update URL parameters workshopLengthDays and, if used, an appropriate representation of workshopLengthLabel.
- Result: The estimate and budget status refresh, and the selected length is shareable via URL.

Interaction: Toggle follow-up group coaching add-on
- Trigger: User toggles includeFollowupGroupCoaching checkbox or switch.
- Actions:
  1. If toggled off, set includeFollowupGroupCoaching to false and optionally null or reset followupGroupCoachingSessions client-side.
  2. If toggled on, keep or set a default followupGroupCoachingSessions value (without hardcoded amounts; any default should originate from current configuration or server-specified defaults).
  3. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with updated includeFollowupGroupCoaching and followupGroupCoachingSessions.
  4. On success, update configuration and reflect values in UI.
  5. Update URL parameters includeFollowupGroupCoaching and followupGroupCoachingSessions as appropriate.
- Result: The estimated_total and budget status reflect the inclusion or removal of follow-up coaching.

Interaction: Adjust number of follow-up coaching sessions
- Trigger: User changes followupGroupCoachingSessions (e.g., via numeric input or stepper) while includeFollowupGroupCoaching is true.
- Actions:
  1. Validate the new sessions count.
  2. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the new followupGroupCoachingSessions and current values for other fields.
  3. On success, update configuration and estimate.
  4. Update URL parameter followupGroupCoachingSessions.
- Result: Estimate and budget status update in line with the chosen number of sessions.

Interaction: Toggle online pre-work modules add-on
- Trigger: User toggles includeOnlinePreworkModules.
- Actions:
  1. Capture the new boolean value.
  2. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with includeOnlinePreworkModules and current values for other fields.
  3. On success, update configuration and estimate summary.
  4. Update URL parameter includeOnlinePreworkModules.
- Result: The estimate reflects the inclusion or absence of online pre-work modules and updates budget status.

Interaction: Set or update budget limit
- Trigger: User enters or edits a budgetLimit value.
- Actions:
  1. Validate the input as a number.
  2. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with budgetLimit and current configuration values.
  3. On success, read budget_limit and is_under_budget from the returned CustomTrainingConfiguration.
  4. Update the budget status indicator and enable/disable the proposal navigation button accordingly.
  5. Update URL parameter budgetLimit.
- Result: The user sees whether the current configuration fits the specified budget and knows whether they can proceed to a proposal request.

Interaction: Refresh estimate on demand
- Trigger: User clicks a “Recalculate estimate” or similar button if the design requires an explicit recalculation, in addition to automatic updates.
- Actions:
  1. Use the current values in the form as input to updateCustomTrainingConfiguration.
  2. On success, update configuration and estimate summary.
- Result: The estimate is recalculated and synchronized with server-side logic on demand.

Interaction: Add configuration to proposal request
- Trigger: User clicks the “Add this configuration to Proposal Request” button.
- Preconditions:
  - CustomTrainingConfiguration.estimated_total is present.
  - CustomTrainingConfiguration.is_under_budget is true.
  - CustomTrainingConfiguration.id is available.
- Actions:
  1. Read configuration.id from the current CustomTrainingConfiguration.
  2. Construct a URL to proposal_request.html including:
     - sourceType=custom_training_configuration
     - customTrainingConfigurationId=<configuration.id>
  3. Navigate the browser to the constructed URL.
- Result: The user is taken to the proposal_request.html page with enough information in URL parameters to associate the proposal request with the existing CustomTrainingConfiguration.

Interaction: URL-driven state restoration and sharing
- Trigger: User navigates directly to pricing.html with existing query parameters or modifies query parameters manually.
- Actions:
  1. On load or on URL change, read supported query parameters.
  2. Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with corresponding values, using current configuration values for any fields not specified in URL.
  3. On success, update calculator form fields and estimate summary to match.
- Result: The calculator reflects the state encoded in the URL, allowing shareable and restorable configurations.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is governed by a combination of server-returned configuration data and URL query parameters. On initial load, pricing.html calls getPricingOverview and getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration. The result of getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration provides a baseline CustomTrainingConfiguration and calculatorSettings. The page then inspects URL parameters: deliveryFormat, numParticipants, workshopLengthDays, includeFollowupGroupCoaching, followupGroupCoachingSessions, includeOnlinePreworkModules, and budgetLimit. If any of these parameters are present, the page calls updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the configuration’s existing field values overridden by the corresponding URL parameter values. The CustomTrainingConfiguration returned from this call becomes the active configuration, and all calculator components bind to it.

Whenever the user changes a field in the calculator, the page calls updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the complete set of current configuration values, including any budgetLimit. On successful response, the page updates the in-memory representation of CustomTrainingConfiguration (for this page lifecycle) and immediately synchronizes relevant fields back into the URL query string, ensuring that the URL always represents the current calculator state. For example, if the user changes numParticipants, the numParticipants query parameter is updated; if the user toggles includeOnlinePreworkModules, that parameter is updated.

The URL is therefore the primary mechanism for persisting and sharing the calculator state between sessions or across users, without using localStorage or other client-side persistence. Reloading the page or sharing the full pricing.html URL with its query parameters will re-establish the same configuration by replaying the URL parameter override logic via updateCustomTrainingConfiguration. The CustomTrainingConfiguration.id itself is not written to the URL from this page, but it is used when navigating to proposal_request.html. Budget-related state (budgetLimit and is_under_budget) is fully held in CustomTrainingConfiguration and reflected in the URL via budgetLimit only; is_under_budget is recalculated on the server side and not stored in the URL.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays standard training and consulting pricing tiers, and exposes calculatorSettings values used for explanation alongside the calculator.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getPricingOverview and store trainingTiers, consultingTiers, and calculatorSettings for use by this component and the calculator.
onApiSuccess(getPricingOverview): Render dynamic lists of training and consulting tiers using names, descriptions, startingFrom, and currency fields from the response.
onApiError(getPricingOverview): Display an error state or fallback message (without static pricing data).

components.functionality: Coordinates initial loading of the current CustomTrainingConfiguration, applies URL parameter overrides, and initializes the calculator components with the resulting configuration.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration to retrieve the baseline configuration and calculatorSettings.

onApiSuccess(getCurrentCustomTrainingConfiguration): Read URL parameters (deliveryFormat, numParticipants, workshopLengthDays, includeFollowupGroupCoaching, followupGroupCoachingSessions, includeOnlinePreworkModules, budgetLimit). If any are present, merge them onto the configuration and call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the merged values; otherwise, broadcast the configuration to dependent components.

onApiSuccess(updateCustomTrainingConfiguration from initialization): Store the returned configuration as the active configuration and broadcast it to the calculator form, estimate summary, budget indicator, and proposal button components.
onUrlChange: If the URL changes externally (e.g., browser navigation), re-read the query parameters and call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration to re-sync the configuration.

components.functionality: Provides inputs for all configurable fields in CustomTrainingConfiguration and triggers server-side recalculation via updateCustomTrainingConfiguration whenever the user changes values.
event_handlers: onConfigChange(from calculator-initializer or updateCustomTrainingConfiguration): Populate form fields with configuration.delivery_format, configuration.num_participants, configuration.workshop_length_days, configuration.workshop_length_label, configuration.include_followup_group_coaching, configuration.followup_group_coaching_sessions, configuration.include_online_prework_modules, and configuration.budget_limit.
onChange(deliveryFormat): Validate selection, then call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with deliveryFormat and current values of all other configuration fields.
onChange(numParticipants): After validation, call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with new numParticipants and other current configuration values.
onChange(workshopLengthDays/workshopLengthLabel): Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with updated workshopLengthDays and workshopLengthLabel plus other current values.
onToggle(includeFollowupGroupCoaching): If toggled off, set includeFollowupGroupCoaching to false and adjust followupGroupCoachingSessions as appropriate; then call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration.
onChange(followupGroupCoachingSessions): When includeFollowupGroupCoaching is true, call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the new followupGroupCoachingSessions.
onToggle(includeOnlinePreworkModules): Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the new includeOnlinePreworkModules value.
onChange(budgetLimit): Call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration with the new budgetLimit and current configuration values.
onApiSuccess(updateCustomTrainingConfiguration): Update all bound form fields with the returned CustomTrainingConfiguration and emit an event to the URL-state-sync and estimate-summary components.
onApiError(updateCustomTrainingConfiguration): Show validation or error messaging tied to relevant inputs without falling back to any hardcoded pricing.

components.functionality: Displays the latest estimated_total, currency, calculation timestamp, and a concise summary of the configuration for user confirmation.
event_handlers: onConfigChange: When CustomTrainingConfiguration updates, render estimated_total, currency, last_calculated_at, and key configuration fields (e.g., num_participants, workshop_length_days, delivery_format).

onConfigChange: Optionally display explanatory text derived from calculatorSettings (base_daily_rate, per_participant_fee, per_coaching_session_fee, online_prework_module_fee) without performing client-side pricing calculations or defining static numbers.
onBudgetChange: Reflect is_under_budget status with appropriate messaging (e.g., under budget vs above budget) without changing the underlying numeric values.

components.functionality: Shows whether the current configuration is under the specified budgetLimit and gives dynamic guidance based on CustomTrainingConfiguration.is_under_budget.
event_handlers: onConfigChange: Read configuration.budget_limit, configuration.estimated_total, and configuration.is_under_budget. If budget_limit is set, compare estimate vs budget using is_under_budget and render corresponding status messaging.
onConfigChange: Enable or disable visual emphasis that the configuration is ready to proceed, depending on is_under_budget.
onConfigChange: If budget_limit is not set, prompt the user to enter a budget for guidance, without providing any default budget value from the client side.

components.functionality: Keeps the URL query parameters in sync with the current CustomTrainingConfiguration so that the calculator state can be restored and shared.
event_handlers: onConfigChange: Update URL query parameters deliveryFormat, numParticipants, workshopLengthDays, includeFollowupGroupCoaching, followupGroupCoachingSessions, includeOnlinePreworkModules, and budgetLimit based on the latest configuration values, adding or removing parameters as necessary.
onUrlChange: Detect changes to the URL (e.g., via browser navigation) and notify the calculator-initializer to reapply URL-driven overrides through updateCustomTrainingConfiguration.
onBeforeUnload: No persistent storage is used; ensure that no localStorage or similar mechanisms are written to, relying solely on the URL for state persistence.

components.functionality: Controls the “Add this configuration to Proposal Request” action, only enabling navigation to proposal_request.html when the configuration meets budget criteria and has a valid ID.
event_handlers: onConfigChange: Check CustomTrainingConfiguration.id, CustomTrainingConfiguration.estimated_total, and CustomTrainingConfiguration.is_under_budget. Enable the button only when id is present, estimated_total is defined, and is_under_budget is true.

onClick: When enabled, construct a URL to proposal_request.html with sourceType=custom_training_configuration and customTrainingConfigurationId set to CustomTrainingConfiguration.id, then navigate the browser to that URL.
onClick(disabled): If is_under_budget is false or required fields are missing, prevent navigation and optionally emit an event to highlight budget guidance or missing input fields.

components.functionality: Optionally provides a single control to force recalculation using the current form values, in setups where automatic recalculation is throttled or deferred.
event_handlers: onClick: Collect current values from the training-calculator-form fields and call updateCustomTrainingConfiguration.

onApiSuccess(updateCustomTrainingConfiguration): Update CustomTrainingConfiguration state and broadcast to estimate-summary-panel, budget-status-indicator, url-state-sync-controller, and proposal-navigation-button.
onApiError(updateCustomTrainingConfiguration): Display error feedback without falling back to any hardcoded estimate.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain a linear, top-to-bottom single-column experience within the main content area, mirroring the mockup’s editorial feel. The global header (cyan utility bar + white nav bar) remains unchanged at the top. Below it, a centered content column holds: page title → pricing overview → calculator initializer → training calculator form → estimate summary → budget status → action buttons. All interactive pieces follow one another vertically, with no sidebars or complex multi-column structures.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain a linear, top-to-bottom single-column experience within the main content area, mirroring the mockup’s editorial feel. The global header (cyan utility bar + white nav bar) remains unchanged at the top. Below it, a centered content column holds: page title → pricing overview → calculator initializer → training calculator form → estimate summary → budget status → action buttons. All interactive pieces follow one another vertically, with no sidebars or complex multi-column structures.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a max-width content container of about 1100–1200px, centered. Constrain the main interactive column (pricing, form, summary) to ~60–70% of the page width to keep a strong central focus, leaving generous left/right margins. Apply 40–64px vertical spacing between major sections (e.g., between pricing overview and calculator form, between form and summary) and 24–32px spacing between closely related elements (e.g., between summary and budget indicator, between buttons). The header + cyan bar together occupy ~15–20% of initial viewport height, with the remainder devoted to airy, scrollable content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density low and the layout breathable. Each component sits in its own clearly separated block with ample white space around it. Form fields are arranged in a clean vertical stack with 16–24px spacing between groups. The summary and budget indicator are compact but surrounded by generous padding so the page never feels crowded, even where there are more controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Reinforce a simple vertical reading flow: the user’s eye moves from the bright cyan utility bar to the logo and navigation, then down to the page title and into the pricing overview. From there, the flow continues down through the calculator initializer and into the main form, then to the estimate summary and budget indicator, ending at the primary call-to-action button. Emphasis is created through typography, spacing, and alignment rather than heavy visual effects, supporting a straightforward scroll-down interaction pattern.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width cyan utility bar and the white navigation bar, the page opens into a calm, spacious content area with a centered column. A bold, left-aligned page title 'Pricing & Calculator' sits near the top of this column, with plenty of vertical breathing room above and below. Directly under the title, a moderately wide pricing overview block (about 60% of the page width) presents standard training and consulting tiers in a simple, text-first layout. Below this, a slim, centered calculator initializer panel introduces the configuration flow, leading the eye downward into the main training calculator form—a vertically stacked, card-like section that spans about 65–70% of the page width. Immediately beneath the form, a compact but visually clear estimate summary panel presents the calculated total; a small budget status indicator sits just under it, subtly signaling whether the estimate fits the chosen budget. At the bottom of this vertical stack, two horizontally aligned buttons—a primary proposal navigation button and a secondary manual recalculate trigger—are centered within the same column, with ample whitespace around them. The entire page reads as a clean, linear narrative: understand pricing, configure your scenario, see the estimate and budget fit, then proceed to request a proposal, all within a focused, minimal layout that mirrors the original mockup’s professional, airy feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned directly below the page title within the main content container, the pricing-overview-section appears as the first content block. It is centered on the page and constrained to roughly 60% of the full page width (within the ~1100–1200px max-width container), creating generous left and right margins that echo the mockup’s central text column. The top of this section sits about 40–56px below the page title, and the section itself has 24–32px internal vertical padding so that headings, short copy, and any tier list feel airy. Content is arranged in a simple vertical stack—brief explanatory text, then the tiers—without side-by-side price cards, maintaining low density. There is a 40–56px gap below this section before the calculator initializer begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section within the normal document flow; scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calculator-initializer sits immediately below the pricing overview, still within the same centered column. It appears as a low-height, horizontally oriented strip or card spanning about 60–65% of the page width, aligned to the center. Vertical spacing above and below is modest but clear: ~32px above (from the pricing overview) and ~32–40px below (before the main form). Within this strip, any label or introductory text is left-aligned, with any primary input (e.g., initial budget or high-level configuration choice) and supporting control (like a 'Start calculator' toggle) aligned on the same horizontal line, but still within the single central column. Padding of 16–24px on all sides keeps it visually separated yet integrated into the linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level component, fully integrated into the scroll; no fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The training-calculator-form is the main interactive block and sits directly below the calculator initializer, forming the visual core of the page. It takes the shape of a tall, card-like panel centered on the page, spanning roughly 65–70% of the page width. Its top edge starts about 32–40px beneath the initializer, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides to preserve the clean, spacious feel. Inputs are arranged in a strictly vertical layout: high-level options at the top, followed by detailed configuration fields, each group separated by about 16–24px. Labels are left-aligned within the panel to preserve a businesslike, form-first aesthetic. There are no sidebars or multi-column field arrangements; at most, small paired fields (like two short inputs) can sit side by side within the form’s width while still feeling part of a single column. The bottom of the form sits 40–56px above the estimate summary, keeping a clear visual break between configuration and results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block that grows vertically with its content. It scrolls naturally with the page and does not use sticky or floating positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The estimate-summary-panel is positioned directly beneath the training calculator form, aligned to the same central axis. It appears as a compact, horizontally oriented card spanning about 50–60% of the page width, making it slightly narrower than the form to visually signal that it is a summary, not a configuration area. The panel’s top edge sits approximately 40–48px below the bottom of the form. Inside, key numbers (estimated total, breakdown highlights) are arranged in a simple vertical stack or a very light left-to-right line, but always within the same column, with 16–24px internal padding. The summary headline or total amount is left-aligned and more visually prominent (larger font or bolder weight), while supporting details sit beneath. The panel is surrounded by ample white space to make the results feel immediately noticeable yet not overwhelming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky block element. It sits directly in the content flow beneath the form and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-status-indicator sits immediately below the estimate summary panel, still centered within the main content column. It occupies a small horizontal slice—about 40–50% of the page width—with its content (e.g., an icon and a short line of text such as 'Within your budget' or 'Exceeds budget') left-aligned within that slice. The top of this indicator is roughly 16–24px beneath the estimate summary, visually tying it closely to the summary but keeping it as a separate row. The indicator may use subtle color coding (e.g., a small colored dot or left border) but otherwise remains minimal and flat, respecting the corporate aesthetic. Below this component, leave about 32–40px before the action buttons.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block-level component within the vertical flow; no fixed positioning or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync-controller is non-visual and does not render a dedicated UI area. It operates in the background, synchronizing the state of the calculator and summary with the URL. Conceptually, it is attached to the main content column, but it does not consume any width, height, or visible space. Its behavior should not disrupt the simple, linear visual flow; users will only perceive it through URL changes and restored configurations, not as a visible element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-footprint controller that does not affect layout; it hooks into the page’s state lifecycle rather than occupying physical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proposal-navigation-button appears below the budget status indicator as the primary call-to-action in the main column. It is centered horizontally within the same width as the form/summary stack (effectively aligned with them), with its top edge about 32–40px below the budget indicator. The button is large enough to stand out (e.g., 16px vertical padding, 24–32px horizontal padding) and may use the brand’s accent color, but remains flat in style. On wider screens, this button sits to the left when paired with the manual recalculate trigger, forming a small horizontal button row; on narrow screens, it can stack vertically while maintaining a centered alignment. The space below the button row is generous (48–64px), giving a clear sense of completion at the end of the page’s interaction flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard interactive button within the flow. It does not stick or float; its position is defined by the vertical stacking order under the budget indicator.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The manual-recalculate-trigger is placed alongside the proposal navigation button as a secondary action. On desktop widths, it appears to the right of the proposal button, aligned on the same horizontal baseline, with about 16–24px of horizontal space separating the two. Both buttons are jointly centered within the main content column, forming a compact, two-button row. The recalculate button is visually de-emphasized relative to the proposal button—using a lighter outline or text style—but matches in height and basic shape to maintain visual harmony. On smaller screens, this button stacks below the proposal button, centered, with 12–16px vertical spacing between them. This placement keeps the primary flow intact while making the manual recalculation option clearly accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline button that participates in the normal layout flow; it does not have sticky or floating behavior and simply follows the budget indicator and sits next to the proposal button.

name: Consultants & Experts
title: Consultants & Experts
description: Directory and profile view where users can search, filter, and sort consultants, open a consultant’s full profile, and submit direct contact requests via an embedded profile form.

core_features: Load dynamic filter and sort options for consultants using getConsultantFilterOptions and display them as selectable controls.
List consultants using searchConsultants with filters, sort, and pagination derived from URL parameters (expertise, region, sort, page).
Display key consultant attributes in the directory listing (e.g., expertise areas, region, years of experience, client information) based on Consultant data returned by searchConsultants.
Allow users to filter consultants by expertise and region; changes update URL parameters and re-run searchConsultants.
Allow users to sort consultants by one of the sort options returned by getConsultantFilterOptions; changes update the sort URL parameter and re-run searchConsultants.
Provide pagination for the consultants listing using page and pageSize with searchConsultants, with current page reflected in the page URL parameter.
Display a consultant’s detailed profile when a consultantId URL parameter is present by calling getConsultantDetails.
Show a contact form on the consultant profile that captures subject, preferred contact method, and message, and submits via submitConsultantContactRequest.
Show a confirmation state on successful contact submission, based on the response from submitConsultantContactRequest.
Provide a "Request a call" action from the consultant profile that navigates to booking.html with a serviceId URL parameter while preserving the current consultant context in the UI as needed.
user_workflows:
Browse consultants via directory:
1. User navigates to consultants.html with no URL parameters.
2. Page calls getConsultantFilterOptions to obtain expertiseAreas, regions, and sortOptions.
3. Page reads URL parameters (expertise, region, sort, page). Since none are provided, it falls back to defaults (e.g., first sort option and page 1).
4. Page calls searchConsultants with filters derived from URL state and displays the consultant listing.
5. User scrolls the listing and reviews key consultant information.

Filter consultants by expertise and region:
1. User selects one or more expertise filters from the filter controls.
2. Filter component updates the expertise URL parameter to match the selected value(s) according to the defined URL format (single or combined).
3. Page re-calls searchConsultants with updated filters (expertise and any existing region, sort, and page) and resets page to 1.
4. Listing updates to show consultants matching the selected expertise.
5. User optionally selects a region filter; component updates the region URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers searchConsultants again.

Sort consultants:
1. User opens the sort control and selects one of the sortOptions returned by getConsultantFilterOptions.
2. Page updates the sort URL parameter to the selected option’s value and resets page to 1.
3. Page re-calls searchConsultants with the same filters and new sort value.
4. Listing updates to show consultants ordered according to the selected sort option.

Paginate through consultant results:
1. After searchConsultants returns a result set and totalCount, the pagination component computes the number of pages based on pageSize.
2. User clicks a different page number or next/previous controls.
3. Pagination component updates the page URL parameter to the selected page.
4. Page calls searchConsultants again with the same filters and sort but updated page.
5. Listing updates to show consultants for the selected page while keeping the filter and sort selections intact.

Open a consultant profile from the directory:
1. User clicks on a consultant card in the listing.
2. Directory component triggers navigation to consultants.html with consultantId set to the clicked consultant’s id and expertise, region, sort, and page preserved from the current URL state.
3. On load (or history state change), page reads consultantId and calls getConsultantDetails.
4. Page continues to display the directory (with searchConsultants results) and additionally renders the selected consultant’s profile details in the profile component.
5. User can scroll within the profile panel and review full bio, expertise, experience, and client information.

Deep-link directly to a consultant profile:
1. User arrives at consultants.html with consultantId in the URL (optionally with expertise, region, sort, and page pre-set from another page such as index.html).
2. Page reads URL parameters and calls getConsultantFilterOptions.
3. Page calls searchConsultants with expertise, region, sort, and page from the URL (or default where missing) to render the context listing.
4. In parallel, page calls getConsultantDetails with consultantId.
5. Page displays the profile for the specified consultant alongside the directory filtered/sorted per URL state.

Submit a consultant contact request:
1. With a consultant profile visible (consultantId present), user fills in the contact form fields: subject, preferred contact method, and optional message.
2. On form submission, the contact form validates that subject and preferred contact method are present and that a consultantId is available from the URL.
3. Form calls submitConsultantContactRequest with consultantId (from URL), subject, preferredContactMethod, and message.
4. On response, if success is true, page displays a confirmation state and may clear or disable the form inputs while still showing key submitted details.
5. If success is false or an error occurs, page shows an inline error message and allows the user to adjust inputs and retry.

Navigate to general booking with a consultant context:
1. While viewing a consultant profile, user clicks a "Request a call" CTA.
2. Page navigates to booking.html, appending a serviceId URL parameter using the value configured for consulting calls.
3. Depending on the broader application design, booking.html may use serviceId to preselect a relevant booking service, while the originating consultants.html page state (filters, consultantId) remains available in browser history for easy return.

Close a consultant profile while keeping current directory view:
1. User viewing a consultant profile (consultantId present) decides to close the detailed view.
2. User clicks a close or back-to-list action in the profile component.
3. Page removes the consultantId parameter from the URL while keeping existing expertise, region, sort, and page parameters intact.
4. Directory listing remains as-is; profile component hides, leaving only the directory on screen.
interactions:
Filter selection interaction:
- Trigger: User selects or changes an expertise or region filter control.
- Behavior: Component updates corresponding URL parameter(s) and resets page to 1, then triggers searchConsultants with new filters, current sort, and page 1.

Sort selection interaction:
- Trigger: User selects a sort option from a dropdown or equivalent control.
- Behavior: Component sets the sort URL parameter to the selected option’s value, resets page to 1, then triggers searchConsultants with the current filters and new sort value.

Pagination interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks a page number, Previous, or Next in the pagination controls.
- Behavior: Component sets page URL parameter to the selected page, then triggers searchConsultants with the current filters and sort, updating the listing to show that page.

Consultant card click (open profile) interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks on a consultant card in the directory.
- Behavior: Component navigates to consultants.html with consultantId set to the selected consultant’s id, and expertise, region, sort, and page preserved from the current URL. Page then calls getConsultantDetails and displays the profile while keeping the listing visible.

Profile close interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks a close/back control on the consultant profile.
- Behavior: Component updates URL by removing consultantId while preserving expertise, region, sort, and page. Profile component hides; directory listing stays unchanged.

Contact form input interaction:
- Trigger: User types in subject, selects preferred contact method, and enters a message.
- Behavior: Component stores current input values in local component state (not persisted) and enables/disables the submit action based on required fields being filled.

Contact form submission interaction:
- Trigger: User submits the consultant contact form.
- Behavior: Form validates inputs. If valid, it calls submitConsultantContactRequest with consultantId (from URL), subject, preferredContactMethod, and message. It shows a loading state until the response returns. On success, shows confirmation state; on failure, shows an error and keeps the form inputs for correction.

Request a call CTA interaction:
- Trigger: User clicks the "Request a call" CTA on a consultant’s profile.
- Behavior: Component navigates to booking.html with a serviceId URL parameter set to the configured consulting call service id. It does not modify filters or other consultant-related URL parameters on consultants.html, which remain available in the browser history.

URL-driven state initialization interaction:
- Trigger: Page load or browser navigation event (back/forward) that changes URL parameters.
- Behavior: Page reads consultantId, expertise, region, sort, and page from the URL. It calls getConsultantFilterOptions first, then searchConsultants with filters/sort/page derived from URL, and, if consultantId is present, calls getConsultantDetails to render the associated profile.

Empty or no-result listing interaction:
- Trigger: searchConsultants returns an empty items array for the current filters.
- Behavior: Listing component shows a dynamic empty-state message (without hardcoded assumptions about content) and prompts the user to adjust filters or clear them; no consultant cards are rendered.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is managed through URL query parameters combined with in-memory component state for transient form input. No localStorage or other persistent client storage is used.

On initial load, the page parses the following URL parameters:
- consultantId (string): If present, triggers the loading and display of the corresponding consultant profile via getConsultantDetails. If absent, no profile is shown initially.
- expertise (string): If present, used to derive the expertise filter passed into searchConsultants. If absent, no expertise filter is applied by default.
- region (string): If present, used to set the region filter passed into searchConsultants. If absent, no region filter is applied by default.
- sort (string): If present, used as the sort value in searchConsultants. If absent, the page chooses a default sort (e.g., first option from sortOptions) without hardcoding any specific value; the actual value is determined at runtime from getConsultantFilterOptions.
- page (number): If present and valid, used as the current page number in searchConsultants. If absent or invalid, the page defaults to page 1.

Lifecycle and updates:
1. On every load or URL change, the page calls getConsultantFilterOptions once to retrieve the current expertiseAreas, regions, and sortOptions.
2. Using the parsed URL state and filter options, the page constructs a filters object (including expertise and region if present) and calls searchConsultants with {filters, sort, page, pageSize} where page is from URL (or default 1) and pageSize is determined by the application configuration (not hardcoded in the page specification).
3. If consultantId exists in the URL, the page calls getConsultantDetails with that consultantId and displays the result in the profile component.
4. When filters (expertise, region) change via user interaction:
   - The corresponding URL parameter(s) are updated to reflect the new filter state.
   - The page parameter is reset to 1 in the URL.
   - searchConsultants is called with updated filters, the current or default sort, and page 1.
5. When sort changes:
   - The sort URL parameter is set to the selected sort option value.
   - The page URL parameter is reset to 1.
   - searchConsultants is called with the current filters and new sort value.
6. When pagination changes:
   - The page URL parameter is set to the selected page.
   - searchConsultants is called with current filters and sort plus the new page.
7. When a consultant card is clicked:
   - Navigation to consultants.html occurs with consultantId set to the selected consultant's id. Existing expertise, region, sort, and page parameters are preserved.
   - getConsultantDetails is called with the new consultantId. The directory remains scoped to the preserved filters, sort, and page.
8. When the consultant profile is closed:
   - consultantId is removed from the URL.
   - No new calls to searchConsultants are required solely for closing the profile; the listing remains unchanged.
9. Contact form does not alter URL parameters. It relies on consultantId from URL to determine which consultant contact request to submit. On successful submitConsultantContactRequest, internal component state tracks that the form is in a 'submitted/confirmed' state, but URL parameters remain untouched.

Overall, the URL is the single source of truth for list context (filters, sort, pagination, selected consultant), ensuring deep links and back/forward navigation reproduce the same directory and profile state.

components.functionality: Loads dynamic filter options using getConsultantFilterOptions and allows users to select expertise and region filters. On change, it updates the expertise and region URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchConsultants call via parent orchestration logic.
event_handlers: onFiltersInit: Called after getConsultantFilterOptions resolves to populate expertise and region controls with available options.
onExpertiseChange: Updates the expertise URL parameter to the selected expertise value, resets page parameter to 1, and notifies parent to call searchConsultants with updated filters.
onRegionChange: Updates the region URL parameter to the selected region value, resets page parameter to 1, and notifies parent to call searchConsultants with updated filters.
onClearFilters: Clears expertise and/or region URL parameters, resets page to 1, and triggers searchConsultants with cleared filters.

components.functionality: Displays sort options for consultants using data from getConsultantFilterOptions. When the user selects a sort option, it updates the sort URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers a new searchConsultants call.
event_handlers: onSortInit: Sets initial selected sort based on sort URL parameter or default derived from sortOptions.
onSortChange: Updates the sort URL parameter with the chosen sort option value, resets page parameter to 1, and notifies parent to call searchConsultants with current filters and new sort.

components.functionality: Displays the list of consultants returned by searchConsultants, including key attributes such as full name, title, expertise areas, region, years of experience, and client information. Each consultant entry is rendered as a clickable card that opens the consultant’s profile.
event_handlers: onDirectoryInit: Called after URL state and filter options are resolved to trigger searchConsultants with current filters, sort, and page.
onCardClick: Reads the clicked Consultant id and navigates to consultants.html with consultantId set to that id while preserving existing expertise, region, sort, and page URL parameters.
onNoResults: Triggered when searchConsultants returns an empty items array; displays an empty-state message and suggests filter adjustments.

components.functionality: Manages pagination for the consultant directory based on totalCount and pageSize from searchConsultants. Updates the page URL parameter when the user navigates between pages and triggers a new searchConsultants call for the selected page.
event_handlers: onPageChange: Sets the page URL parameter to the selected page index and triggers searchConsultants with current filters and sort.
onNextPage: Increments the page URL parameter (if not on last page) and triggers searchConsultants.
onPreviousPage: Decrements the page URL parameter (if not on first page) and triggers searchConsultants.

components.functionality: Displays the full profile of the consultant whose id is specified in the consultantId URL parameter. Uses getConsultantDetails to load detailed information and presents attributes such as full name, title, bio, expertise_areas, region, years_experience, and client information. Provides controls to close the profile and to access contact and booking actions.
event_handlers: onProfileInit: When consultantId is present in URL, calls getConsultantDetails with consultantId and renders profile once data is returned.
onProfileIdChange: Listens for URL changes to consultantId and re-calls getConsultantDetails when the selected consultant changes.
onCloseProfile: Removes consultantId from the URL while preserving expertise, region, sort, and page parameters, then hides the profile panel.
onRequestCallClick: Navigates to booking.html with serviceId set for a consulting call, using standard navigation with URL parameters.

components.functionality: Embedded within the consultant-profile-view, this form allows users to send a message or inquiry directly to the selected consultant. It collects a subject, preferred contact method, and optional message, then submits the data via submitConsultantContactRequest. It also handles validation and displays a confirmation or error state based on the response.
data_binding: Consultant (from getConsultantDetails response for context such as full_name)
event_handlers: onFormInit: Initializes form state ensuring consultantId is available from URL; if consultantId is missing, the form is disabled.
onSubjectChange: Updates internal form state for subject and checks if required fields are satisfied.
onPreferredContactMethodChange: Updates internal form state with selected preferredContactMethod and checks required fields.
onMessageChange: Updates internal form state for message.

onSubmit: Validates that subject and preferredContactMethod are present and that consultantId exists. If valid, calls submitConsultantContactRequest with {consultantId, subject, preferredContactMethod, message}. Shows a loading state during the call.

onSubmitSuccess: Triggered when submitConsultantContactRequest returns success true; stores the returned ConsultantContactRequest for reference, switches the form into a confirmation state (e.g., hides or disables inputs and shows a success message), and may provide a link or button to reset the form.
onSubmitError: Triggered when submitConsultantContactRequest indicates failure or an error occurs; displays an inline error message and keeps user input so they can correct and retry.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter parsing, and interactions between filter controls, sort control, directory list, pagination controls, and profile view. Ensures that changes in one component propagate correctly to others and to the URL, and that getConsultantFilterOptions, searchConsultants, and getConsultantDetails are called in the right sequence.
data_binding: URL parameters (consultantId, expertise, region, sort, page)
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parses URL parameters, calls getConsultantFilterOptions, then calls searchConsultants with filters derived from expertise and region, sort from URL or default from sortOptions, and page from URL or default 1. If consultantId exists, also calls getConsultantDetails.
onUrlChange: Responds to browser navigation (back/forward or internal pushes) by re-reading URL parameters and re-triggering getConsultantDetails and/or searchConsultants as needed.
onFiltersUpdated: Receives events from consultant-filter-controls when filters change, updates URL, and re-calls searchConsultants.
onSortUpdated: Receives events from consultant-sort-control when sort changes, updates URL, and re-calls searchConsultants.
onPageUpdated: Receives events from consultant-pagination-controls when page changes, updates URL, and re-calls searchConsultants.
onProfileSelection: Receives events from consultant-directory-list when a consultant is selected, updates consultantId in URL, and calls getConsultantDetails.
onProfileClosed: Receives close events from consultant-profile-view, removes consultantId from URL, and leaves list state untouched.

layout.grid_system: Likely a 12-column responsive grid (common in corporate WordPress/Bootstrap-based layouts) with a centered max-width content container (~1100–1200px) and full-bleed header bar.
layout.layout_pattern: All consultants-related content sits in a single, centered main column beneath the existing cyan utility bar and white navigation header. The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, linear flow: page title at the top of the content area, followed by filter controls, then sort control, then the consultant directory list, pagination, and (in detail mode) the consultant profile view with its embedded contact form. No sidebars are used; filters and details do not sit in parallel columns but stack vertically to preserve the editorial, scroll-down reading pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All consultants-related content sits in a single, centered main column beneath the existing cyan utility bar and white navigation header. The page follows a strictly top-to-bottom, linear flow: page title at the top of the content area, followed by filter controls, then sort control, then the consultant directory list, pagination, and (in detail mode) the consultant profile view with its embedded contact form. No sidebars are used; filters and details do not sit in parallel columns but stack vertically to preserve the editorial, scroll-down reading pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A max-width content container (~1100–1200px) is centered on the page. Within that, the main interactive column (filters, list, profiles) occupies roughly 60–70% of the container width, centered, leaving ample left/right whitespace to echo the narrow privacy policy text column in the mockup. Vertical spacing between major blocks (title → filters → list → profile) is generous (≈40–64px), and internal spacing within components uses the 8px scale (12–24px paddings), keeping the layout airy and uncluttered. The header/utility bar area visually occupies the top ~15–20% of the viewport, with the consultants content making up the remainder, dominated by whitespace around the central column.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low. Filters and sort controls are presented in one or two clean horizontal rows with plenty of breathing room around each control. The directory shows a single column of consultant cards instead of a dense grid, with clear separation between each card. The profile view and contact form follow the same editorial feel: generous margins, large headings, and short line lengths for readability. No heavy borders, tight clusters, or multi-column text blocks are used, reflecting the minimal, corporate editorial character of the mockup.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the cyan utility bar and navigation (consistent across the site), then moves to a bold, centered 'Consultants & Experts' page title. From there, the eye is guided straight down through filter controls, then to the subtle sort control, then to the vertically stacked consultant cards. Clicking a consultant naturally leads further downward into a full-width profile section and then into the embedded contact form. The layout avoids complex Z-patterns, instead reinforcing a simple vertical reading path with carefully spaced sections and a consistent centered column to maintain focus.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width cyan utility bar and the white navigation header, the page transitions into a calm, white content area. Centered within this is a max-width container, inside which a slightly narrower main content column (about two-thirds of the page width) anchors all consultants-related elements. At the very top of this column, a bold, left-aligned or gently centered 'Consultants & Experts' heading sits with ample whitespace above and below, visually mirroring the spacious 'Privacy Policy' title from the mockup. Beneath the title, the filter controls occupy a shallow, tidy band, spanning the width of the main column but with clear gaps between each control so nothing feels crowded. Just beneath, aligned to the right edge of the same column, a compact sort dropdown quietly offers ordering options without competing for attention. The consultant directory then unfolds as a single vertical stack of consultant cards, each card matching the width of the main column, separated by generous vertical gaps to preserve the low-density editorial feel. Pagination controls sit centered below the list, providing navigation with minimal visual weight. When a consultant is opened, the layout keeps the same centered, single-column structure: the profile detail section appears in a broad, clean block with the consultant’s name and key information at the top and the embedded contact form directly below, both surrounded by ample white space. Throughout, elements align to a subtle 12-column grid, but the user’s experience is of a simple, elegant, scrollable page with content clearly staged one section at a time.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultant-listing-shell occupies the full width of the page below the site-wide cyan utility bar and white navigation header. It introduces a centered max-width container (~1100–1200px) with automatic side margins. Within this container, it establishes the main content column for the page at roughly 60–70% of the container width, centered horizontally, leaving broad left and right gutters of whitespace. The shell is responsible for stacking the page title at the top of this column, followed by the consultant-filter-controls, consultant-sort-control, consultant-directory-list, consultant-pagination-controls, and (in detail mode) the consultant-profile-view and consultant-contact-form, each separated by 32–64px of vertical space. All child components align to this central column, preserving a clean, single-column, top-to-bottom flow that echoes the privacy policy layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width wrapper. It does not scroll independently or behave as sticky; instead, it defines structure and spacing for the entire consultants page content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultant-filter-controls block sits near the top of the main content column, directly below the page title and any brief introductory text, with around 40–56px of vertical space separating them. The filters span nearly the full width of the main column (about 60–70% of the page width), centered, but they are laid out in a low-density horizontal arrangement: for example, a keyword search field on the left, followed by role/specialty dropdowns, location selectors, or other filter chips arranged in a single row where space permits. Each control has 12–16px internal padding and at least 16–24px horizontal spacing from its neighbors. On narrower viewports, the filter controls gracefully wrap into two or more rows while still centered, ensuring that everything remains aligned with the same main column edges. Vertical padding inside the filter area (top and bottom) is generous (~16–24px) so the section feels like a calm, well-defined band rather than a cluttered toolbar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page. On desktop it stays near the top but is not sticky; on smaller screens it simply stacks and grows vertically while remaining centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultant-sort-control is positioned directly below the filter controls, separated by a modest vertical gap (~16–24px) to indicate functional relation but distinct hierarchy. It is anchored to the right edge of the main content column, aligning its right side with the right edge of the consultant directory cards. The control itself is visually compact—typically a label like 'Sort by' with a dropdown or select field—occupying roughly 20–25% of the main column’s width and no more than one line of height. Ample whitespace surrounds it: there is clear white space below (24–32px) before the first consultant listing appears, and on the left, an open area of blank space separating it from where the filter controls end. On smaller viewports, the sort control shifts to a full-width row placed below the filters but remains aligned to the same central column, with the select element left-aligned or centered for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline with the page flow. It does not float or stick; it simply occupies a clean, right-aligned slot between filters and directory list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultant-directory-list occupies the heart of the page content, positioned immediately below the sort control with approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing. It adopts a single-column layout: each consultant entry is a card that spans the full width of the main content column (about 60–70% of the page width), centered with equal whitespace to the left and right. Cards are stacked one above the other, each separated by 24–32px of vertical space to maintain the low-density, editorial feel. Within each card, content follows a clear left-to-right hierarchy—such as a small avatar or initials block on the far left, with the consultant’s name, title, specialties, and a brief summary aligned to the right—but the overall card height remains moderate with plenty of padding (16–24px) inside and no heavy drop shadows. The cards’ visual weight is deliberate but clean, using subtle dividers or light borders if needed to distinguish entries, ensuring that scrolling feels like moving down a neatly curated directory rather than scanning a dense table.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack that grows downward as more consultants are listed. It does not implement infinite scroll by default; instead, it works in tandem with the pagination controls below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultant-pagination-controls are placed directly below the consultant-directory-list, with a noticeable vertical gap of about 32–40px after the last card to visually separate navigation from content. The controls are centered within the main content column, aligning with the center of the consultant cards, and occupy only as much width as needed for the page numbers and previous/next buttons—typically about 25–40% of the column width. Buttons or page numbers are spaced apart following the 8px grid (8–16px between items), and the entire pagination strip has comfortable top and bottom padding (~8–16px) so it does not feel cramped. Overall, the pagination appears light and unobtrusive, allowing the directory list above and any profile content below to stay visually dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline in the vertical flow. It anchors the end of each paginated set of results and scrolls off-screen as the user moves further down, without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultant-profile-view appears in the same central column as the directory and uses the same overall width (about 60–70% of the page width), maintaining consistent left and right margins. In a typical interaction, when a consultant is selected from the directory list, the page transitions into a detail state where the profile view replaces the directory section within the consultant-listing-shell, occupying the area where the list and pagination previously sat. The profile panel begins with a prominent consultant name heading near the top, with 40–56px of vertical spacing above it to echo the spacing used for the main page title. Beneath the name, the layout flows downward: a short role/title line, key specializations, location, and a concise introduction or bio arranged in a single-column, text-focused format. Supporting information (expertise tags, industries, credentials) is grouped into clearly labeled subsections, each separated by 24–32px of vertical space. The entire profile body rests on a white background, with generous internal padding (24–32px at the sides and top/bottom) so the content feels like a well-structured editorial profile rather than a cramped resume. The profile view ends with a clear visual lead-in to the consultant-contact-form below—either via a subheading like 'Contact this Consultant' or a soft divider line—maintaining the scroll-down narrative.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the shell but mode-dependent. In list mode it is hidden; in detail mode it becomes the main content below the filters and sort control, fully replacing the directory list and pagination in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The consultant-contact-form is positioned immediately below the consultant-profile-view within the same central content column, separated by a generous vertical gap (~32–40px) and optionally introduced by a succinct heading. It spans the full width of the main column (matching the profile view), with consistent side padding (24–32px) and vertical padding (24–32px) inside the form container. The form fields are laid out in a simple, single-column stack to maintain clarity and low visual density: name, email, company, message, and any additional fields each occupy the full form width, separated by 16–24px of vertical space. The submit button is aligned to the left edge of the fields or centered within the form, but always within the same column boundaries, and has enough surrounding whitespace (16–24px above and below) to stand out without feeling heavy. The overall impression is of a clean, approachable form that reads as a natural continuation of the profile narrative, encouraging the user to continue scrolling downward to complete their contact request.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and only visible in detail mode. It scrolls as part of the main page and does not float; it is tightly coupled to the profile view above as the final section in that detail flow.

name: About
title: About
description: Provides structured information about the company’s mission, values, leadership team, and impact, using dynamic content from the About page API and supporting navigation to consultants and case studies.

core_features: Load mission, values, and philosophy from getAboutPageContent and display them in a structured overview section.
Display the leadership team list from getAboutPageContent.leadershipTeam with expandable/accessible details for each leader.
Present impact metrics from getAboutPageContent.impactMetrics as a concise summary of company results.
Display client testimonials from getAboutPageContent.testimonials as supporting proof of impact.
Provide a call-to-action in the leadership section that navigates to consultants.html when the user selects "Meet our consultants".
Provide a call-to-action in the impact metrics section that navigates to case_studies.html when the user selects "See our client results".
Support URL-based section selection via an optional section query parameter (e.g., about.html?section=leadership) to focus or scroll to the relevant section on load.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View mission, values, and philosophy
1. User navigates to about.html (direct link or footer navigation).
2. On initial load, the about-data-loader component calls getAboutPageContent.
3. After successful data retrieval, the mission-section component reads mission, values, and philosophy from the loaded data and renders them.
4. If the section URL parameter is not present or is set to a value corresponding to the mission area, the page optionally scrolls or focuses to the mission-section.

Workflow: Explore leadership team
1. User navigates to about.html (optionally with section=leadership).
2. The about-data-loader component calls getAboutPageContent and passes the leadershipTeam data to the leadership-team-list component.
3. The leadership-team-list component renders a list of leadership profiles summarizing name and title for each entry.
4. User clicks a leadership profile to expand it; the leadership-member-detail component displays the selected leader’s bio, credentials, and profile image.
5. User can collapse or switch to another leader by clicking a different profile entry.
6. If the user clicks the "Meet our consultants" control within this section, the page navigates to consultants.html, optionally with URL parameters indicating the navigation source (e.g., consultants.html?source=about&fromSection=leadership).

Workflow: Review impact metrics and navigate to case studies
1. User scrolls to the impact metrics section or arrives with section=impact in the URL.
